Document 29 of 500

Honda to unveil 'massive' new EV project: Sources say deal for Ontario factory has support from Ottawa, province

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/honda-unveil-massive-new-ev-project/docview/3043657541/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Honda+to+unveil+%27massive%27+new+EV+project%3A+Sources+say+deal+for+Ontario+factory+has+support+from+Ottawa%2C+province&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-04-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Japanese automaker Honda has agreed to establish a major new, multibillion-dollar electric vehicle operation in Ontario, according to federal and provincial government sources.
The deal is expected to be announced Thursday at Honda's existing factory in Alliston, north of Toronto, where the company will reveal plans to bring a "massive project" to Ontario for the manufacturing of electric vehicles, batteries and other components, a government source with knowledge of the situation told the Star.
The source, who spoke on condition they not be named, said the deal is worth billions of dollars, and would be based on federal funding through investment tax credits - rather than direct subsidies worth billions of dollars that attracted other automakers, including Volkswagen and Stellantis, to set up EV battery plants in Ontario - along with additional support from the provincial government.
A second federal government source confirmed the Honda agreement is coming without providing any details, as did insiders at Queen's Park.
Media reports revealing the accord prompted Premier Doug Ford to hail what he described as the "largest deal" yet in his government's push - alongside Ottawa and Quebec - to attract companies to set up manufacturing in Canada for the emerging industry of electric vehicles and their components.
Speaking in Toronto, Ford said Honda's investment in Alliston would dwarf the massive Stellantis electric vehicle battery factory in Windsor and the even larger Volkswagen project in St. Thomas, each of which received billions of dollars in subsidies from Queen's Park and the federal government.
Swedish battery manufacturer Northvolt has also committed to building a battery mega-factory outside Montreal, with $4.6 billion in subsidies from Ottawa and Quebec City.
"Over the past three years alone, we've attracted over $28 billion in auto and EV battery investments - and folks, stay tuned," Ford told the First Nations Major Projects Coalition meeting Monday.
"But this week we landed a new deal. It will be the largest deal in Canadian history. It'll be double the size of Volkswagen so stay tuned, we'll be announcing it this week," the premier enthused.
Ford noted Stellantis was spending $5 billion on a 4.2-million-square-foot facility in Windsor, and VW another $7 billion on a 16-million-square-foot gigafactory in St. Thomas that will be the fourth-largest building in the world.
"Ontario now is the only place in the entire world - think of this - where the six largest automakers call home," he said.
In a statement, Honda Canada spokesperson Ken Chiu declined to confirm the company's plans, but said Honda is "considering a number of initiatives and is in contact with governments at all levels as we move into the electrified era."
Chiu added that, "in order to achieve our 2040 electrification target of 100 per cent electrified vehicles, we are considering various options to increase local production capacity. However we have nothing further to share at this time."
At Queen's Park, provincial Economic Development Minister Vic Fedeli was tight-lipped while touting "the largest Canadian auto announcement ever."
"It's very exciting," said Fedeli, declining to even specifically mention the word Honda due to commercial sensitivities.
"We've never talked about any of our prospects by name but I would say the scenario is that we do have incentives," said the minister, referring to tax breaks and other sweeteners Queen's Park and Ottawa have put in place.
"That's where we think our tax dollars are best invested because we had 100,000 jobs that were at risk in the auto sector," he said, pointing to competition stateside. "Not only did we save those 100,000 jobs, we've created tens of thousands more."
Asked about Ford Canada recently slashing EV production in Oakville, Fedeli emphasized "no money is distributed to any company until they've met their hiring goals and their construction goals."
Bloomberg first reported that a deal with Honda, which has long been rumoured, was expected to be announced this week.
Honda's potential $18.4-billion auto assembly and EV battery factory would expand the complex 107 kilometres north of Toronto that currently makes gasoline-powered Civics and CR-Vs.
Asked about that dollar figure, a federal government source declined to share specifics but said the total is "in the ballpark" of what will be announced with Honda.
Fedeli would only say that Ontario's overall $28 billion in EV investments would balloon to around $43 billion within the next week, suggesting a $15-billion Honda investment.
To Flavio Volpe, head of the Automotive Parts Manufacturers' Association, the prospect of such a deal is "incredible." He said it would be an even bigger coup after the federal government signalled there would be no more direct subsidies like those it used to land EV factories from Stellantis, Volkswagen and Northvolt in Quebec.
Volpe pointed to a new measure unveiled in the recent federal budget, which promised a new "electric vehicle supply chain investment tax credit" worth 10 per cent of the cost that companies put toward buildings used in "key segments" of the industry.
Volpe said that tax credit seems tailor-made for major companies like Honda, and noted Ottawa and the Ontario and Quebec governments are also still trying to court Toyota - another major Japanese automaker - to set up electric vehicle manufacturing in Canada.
If the tax credit helped attract a big company like Honda, that would mark a significant victory for the government's shift from direct subsidies to a simpler offering that doesn't need to be negotiated with the same depth.
That could position Canada to better compete for jobs and manufacturing in the sector with the United States, which has its own massive suite of incentives under U.S. President Joe Biden's Inflation Reduction Act.
"I've got to give them full credit, no pun intended, on (potentially) pulling in a big fish - maybe the biggest - with credits," Volpe said.
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall Toronto Star Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Letters: Jury acquits Zameer as judge gives rare apology: 'Deepest apologies for what you have been through, April 21 Ukraine claims it shot down a Russian strategic bomber as Moscow's missiles kill 8 Ukrainians, April 19

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3043656977/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Jury+acquits+Zameer+as+judge+gives+rare+apology%3A+%27Deepest+apologies+for+what+you+have+been+through%2C+April+21+Ukraine+claims+it+shot+down+a+Russian+strategic+bomber+as+Moscow%27s+missiles+kill+8+Ukrainians%2C+April+19&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-04-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Judge in Zameer case is a class act, ensured justice
Jury acquits Zameer as judge gives rare apology: 'Deepest apologies for what you have been through, April 21
Congratulations to Justice Anne Malloy. She analyzed the evidence in the complex Umar Zameer case thoroughly. She instructed the jury in a clear and concise manner. Through her efforts, the jury came to logical conclusions and acquitted. To top it off, Justice Malloy had the good grace to apologize to Zameer and his family on behalf of the public for the ordeal through which they had been put. She demonstrated a fine example of how justice should be administered. Truly a class act.
Bill Howes, Toronto
Politicians need to apologize to
an innocent man
My faith in the Canadian justice system has been restored. Notwithstanding the deep hurt suffered by the family of Toronto police Const. Jeffrey Northrup, it is clear this was none other than a tragic accident. What we need to now see is an apology from those politicians - Premier Doug Ford, and former Toronto mayor John Tory and Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown - who so quickly and publicly assumed Umar Zameer's guilt without knowing the full facts of the case. I also wonder what will be the result of possible collusion on the part of the police officers involved in the matter. Testifying falsely under oath is a serious offence and needs to be addressed, particularly when doing so put an innocent man's life and future at stake.
Jack Fearnall, Owen Sound, Ont.
Chief Demkiw's statement is
truly baffling
One is left wondering what Toronto Police Chief Myron Demkiw meant by his statement that "we were hoping for a different outcome?" Was he hoping that the police would be seen as infallible and that the verdict would vindicate their lies and twisting of the truth to cover up a fundamental error in judgment by one of their undercover teams, which resulted in the death of an officer? Or is he now ashamed and unable to look Mrs. Margaret Northrup in the eye knowing that the police have put her through three years of unnecessary agony because they felt it more important to vilify Umar Zameer as a "cop killer" than admit that her husband died due to a series of mistakes made by his team.
Matthew Marosszeky, Aurora
Opportunity for police to change outdated training
After such a long arduous time Umar Zameer is a free man. Many of us who welcome the not guilty verdict rejoice. This is a chance for the Toronto Police Service to totally change their outdated training process. It showed how deeply entrenched they are when their leader expressed disappointment with the jury as they were expecting a guilty verdict. This thinking has to change. I hope the actions of both Justice Anne Malloy, lawyers for the defence and jury will open up the need for more education in dealing with the public. My condolences to both families affected by this most unnecessary tragedy.
Andrea Lee-Burnet, Orillia
Confused chief should resign
Since it's now been revealed that the Toronto police chief doesn't understand when a charge of first-degree murder is actually justified, I'd like him to do the right thing and resign from his position.
Max Hausmann,
Niagara-on-the-Lake, Ont.
Disabled experts are being taken advantage of
Accessibility in Canada is not a difficult concept, but for those living with disabilities, we are often left baffled. Able-bodied people tend to not view it as a priority until it affects them. The Carter Churchill case in Newfoundland and Labrador was heartbreaking and the person at the top, is rewarded for the failures, fixing it only after being successfully sued. Those with disabilities don't like having to go to court and use reason, logic and the law to force compliance with accessibility and allow us to be included. We also don't like being taken advantage of, to write reports, put together research committees and lay the groundwork, only to watch an abled-bodied person with limited concepts of accessibility take jobs we're suited for. With 2025 around the corner, and school boards refusing to be accessible because their able-bodied experts say that inclusion is more important than accessibility, we are going to see far more legal cases, anger and frustration. Accessibility is not that expensive, and we would all save so much money, time and energy if we made it a priority and not an afterthought. Include us, pay us and value us.
Travis Morgan, Whitefish, Ont.
Don't forget about Ukraine
Ukraine claims it shot down a Russian strategic bomber as Moscow's missiles kill 8 Ukrainians, April 19
Once again, Chernihiv got slammed with Russian missiles last Wednesday, killing at least 17 people and wounding at least 61, some of which were children. Then, Russian missiles struck cities and killed eight people, including a 14-year-old girl and eight-year-old boy, and injuring 28 more. Where are the protests? Where is the anger and angst aimed at Russia for these atrocities? Where are the boycotts of Russian owned and supported places of business? Why do all the anger, protests and hate fuelled calls only apply to Israel?
Sharon Pitto, Aurora
Right to protest doesn't include the workplace
As Sundar Pichai CEO at Google capsulized in his firing of the sit-in protesters, "this is a business." Personal beliefs should not interfere with the conduct of business. Protesters have the right to protest, but not within the workplace. A university is a business where those who attend do so to acquire an education. Students are free to protest but protesters do not have the right to intimidate or harass others because of their personal beliefs. Nor should a city be held hostage to protesters who block roads, block airport access and block shopping access for any reason whether its mandatory vaccines or political views. If civil disobedience is not reigned in and prevented from disrupting the lives of others, society will devolve into anarchy. Let's see the government and police focus more on preserving our democracy and less on their popularity.
Jerrold Lerman, Toronto

Document 31 of 500

'We lost a lot ... we're just trying to move on': Zameer tells Star of relief, hope and the stigma of being called a 'cop killer'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-lost-lot-were-just-trying-move-on/docview/3043656648/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27We+lost+a+lot+...+we%27re+just+trying+to+move+on%27%3A+Zameer+tells+Star+of+relief%2C+hope+and+the+stigma+of+being+called+a+%27cop+killer%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-04-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Umar Zameer lost his job, house and friends and for nearly three years, he lived with the stigma of being a "cop killer."
He was the man known as the murderer of a beloved Toronto police officer; for that, he faced life in prison.
That was until Sunday, when a jury cleared his name and a Toronto judge addressed him directly - to apologize.
"Those words gave us hope that Canada is a good country after all," Zameer said in a wide-ranging interview with the Star on Monday.
"I couldn't breathe," he said, remembering the moment of the verdict - "It was so, so overwhelming."
Now, Zameer said he hopes his jury's verdict will convince any doubters that what led to Const. Jeffrey Northrup's death in the underground parking lot beneath Nathan Phillips Square was a tragic accident - and that he, his wife, Aaida Shaikh, and their three young children can rebuild their lives in their adopted homeland.
"We lost a lot, and we're just hopeful and we're just trying to move on from all of this," Zameer said from his lawyer's downtown law office, seated next to Shaikh and Nader Hasan, the lawyer who has been on a mission to clear Zameer's name since his arrest on July 2, 2021.
Still, even as Zameer, Shaikh and Hasan spoke to the Star of their relief, they stressed their regret for what happened that night. When Zameer testified earlier this month, he wept asking the Northrup family for forgiveness; on Monday, he repeated the thought: "I can't stop thinking about them, about the kids especially."
Just 24 hours earlier, the trio was inside courtroom 4-9 at the Superior Court of Justice, bracing for the verdict. It had already been a roller-coaster few days as the couple, both 34, waited for the jury to grapple with the complex decision before them.
Finally, "when I heard the jury say 'not guilty,' I was like - I couldn't believe it, I couldn't believe it that there are people who believe in our truth," Shaikh said.
Late after Canada Day in 2021, she was eight-and-a-half months pregnant and seated in the passenger seat next to her husband when they were approached in the parking garage by two people they believed to be thugs. Their two-year-old son (he's now five) was seated in the back seat.
It was only after Zameer had driven away - to flee - and their BMW was rammed by an unmarked van that they were told he had run over a plainclothes police officer. Zameer was arrested. He went to jail. Their lives came crashing down.
Zameer and Shaikh had arrived in Canada in January 2019, following his sister, Saima Zamir, who had moved to the GTA a few years earlier.
Born in Pakistan, Zameer came to Canada as a permanent resident under the federal skilled worker program. After working several part-time jobs, including driving for Uber and Lyft, he landed work in his chosen field, accounting. At the time of his arrest, he was flourishing in the accounting department of a property management company.
"Umar has done well here, considering the backlog of work that he handled independently," his manager wrote in a January 2021 performance review.
He was fired from that position after his arrest, but has since opened his own accounting firm.
He said some of his darkest days came while locked up in the Toronto South Detention Centre - he was there when Shaikh gave birth to the baby, a daughter, she was carrying that Canada Day evening.
"She was all alone in the hospital when she was delivering," he told the Star as tears welled up in Shaikh's eyes.
From that, "I don't know if I can ever - we can ever - move on," he said.
From the start, the prosecution's case that Zameer had intentionally killed Northrup - the standard for him to be convicted of murder - was weak.
At Zameer's bail hearing in September 2021, the judge in charge said so repeatedly: The idea that a man with no criminal record, out with his pregnant wife and toddler, would suddenly decide to intentionally kill Northrup by running him over, "runs contrary to logic and common sense," she wrote.
To support Zameer's bail application, his family "moved heaven and earth" to transfer their assets - "substantially all of our collective worth," his sister Zamir wrote in an affidavit - into Canada. They were successful - but still, there was the stigma, including from Premier Doug Ford, who slammed the judge's decision despite not knowing her reasons. (A court-ordered publication ban on the reasons behind her decision ended Sunday, after the verdict.)
Amid all this, the couple, who moved in with Zameer's sister and her children, lived in terror and questioned whether it was safe to remain in Canada - although leaving was not an option. Zameer had to surrender his passport and lived under house arrest where the extended family lived like prisoners.
"We stopped going out of the house," he recalled. "We were scared ... (of) every person who was walking out in front of our house." They would peer out the windows and question why a car was driving by slowly; they installed four surveillance cameras on the exterior and kept the TV on all the time for light to "see if anyone is going to come in and do something."
At the same time, with the trial looming, they said they were unable to address the public portrayal of Zameer as a monster who had deliberately killed a cop.
"We had lost hope many times," Shaikh said.
In truth, Zameer said, he admires police; he repeated a story he told when he testified, about his now five-year-old son wants to be a police officer when he grows up.
Hasan recalled on Monday that one of the first questions Zameer asked him when they first met at the Toronto South Detention Centre was not, "How can I get bail?" or "How can you fight this case?" but instead: "Do you know how the family of that poor officer are doing?"
"None of these two families will ever be the same," Hasan said. "There are no winners here. But it wouldn't have done our country one bit of good to send to jail an innocent man, an innocent man who happens to be a very good person also."
Nor is Zameer bitter toward the officer who punched him in the face while he was handcuffed following the guns-drawn takedown at the parking garage exit.
"I forgot about the punch when I saw the blood on the car," he said. (Asked about the possibility of any future litigation his client might consider "to right certain wrongs that occurred in this case," Hasan said the thought was premature.)
After court on Sunday, Zameer was able to literally cut off the electronic monitoring ankle bracelet he's been wearing - and paying for.
He and Shaikh picked up their three children at a friend's house - for the entire lives of his two youngest daughters, he'd had a prosecution hanging over his head.
"This was the first time I wasn't thinking anything else other than hugging," he said, describing giving his son - who was in the car that evening - a particularly tight squeeze.
"I wasn't thinking, 'What will happen?' I hugged them for so long."
During the month-long trial, Zameer and Shaikh said they'd told their children their father was busy working on "a project."
On Sunday, they were able to tell them: "Bubba's project went well."
CREDIT: Betsy Powell Courts Reporter

Subject: Court hearings & proceedings; Police; Parking facilities; Trials

Title: 'We lost a lot ... we're just trying to move on':   Zameer tells Star of relief, hope and the stigma of being called a 'cop killer'

Document 32 of 500

Teacher shortages mean some students at this GTA school get 100% on midterms

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/teacher-shortages-mean-some-students-at-this-gta/docview/3043656605/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Teacher+shortages+mean+some+students+at+this+GTA+school+get+100%25+on+midterms&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Students in a York Catholic high school enrolled in Grade 12 classes with no teacher were given 100 per cent for their midterm mark, as the board struggles to address a shortage of educators.
At St. Maximilian Kolbe Catholic High School, two teachers have been on leave for much of the semester, and in their absence, students have faced a string of substitutes. With midterms approaching, the York Catholic District School Board took the unusual step of giving Grade 12 students in affected classes - two biology classes and one business class - a mark of 100 per cent.
While school boards across Ontario are grappling with staffing levels, what happened in York is an extreme example of what's going on as principals struggle daily to fill absences, sometimes combining or cancelling classes, bringing in student teachers and even asking community members to supervise.
"This is a very rare situation," said board spokesperson Mark Brosens, noting because the board couldn't find long-term replacements there "wasn't sufficient information" to give students a grade with which to apply to university.
"In order to let them apply to university on a normal schedule, the school thought it was best to give them this placeholder grade in order to complete their applications," said Brosens. "All university acceptances at midterms are conditional. And the students in these classes, their grades will be adjusted over the remainder of the semester so that their final grade will be reflective of their achievement in the class."
Meanwhile, Grade 9 students in three classes were also impacted by the absences, but their midterm marks were an"I," which stands for insufficient data for an actual mark.
The board says a long-term occasional business teacher recently started, and a long-term replacement for science is expected this week.
Mike Totten, president of the York Catholic Teachers, the local unit of Ontario English Catholic Teachers' Association, said the board's decision creates an equity issue. On the one hand, he says the Grade 12 students in the affected classes may be at an advantage when applying to university - but on the other, they may be at a disadvantage if they get to university and aren't as prepared as their peers.
"This was a one-off," Totten said. "The board was probably stuck between a rock and a hard place because they didn't have a solution."
When a regular teacher isn't available a class is typically cancelled, and students either enrol in other classes or take the course online. In this case, both teachers were present at the start of the semester and after their leave the board tried to secure long-term replacements. The board is discussing how to support the affected students so that they are ready for university.
Education Minister Stephen Lecce told reporters at Queen's Park on Monday that "kids need to work hard in order to be assessed on their achievement" and urged the Ontario Teachers' Federation to allow retired teachers to take on additional supply shifts to help in the short-term.
"We are calling for retired educators - who have decades of experience - to be leveraged to fill those exact circumstances so qualified teachers could be in the classroom and restore some stability for those kids," he said. "It's unacceptable. ."
The government had sought permission for retired educators to work up to 95 days to alleviate the ongoing shortage without impacting their pensions, as it has done in the past, but the teacher unions refused to budge from the usual 50-day limit.
Lecce called their move "obstructionist, denying qualified people, qualified educators who love these kids ... to fill the void."
But NDP Leader Marit Stiles, said the situation in York is an example of the "appalling state of education in this province after six years of Doug Ford's government."
Schools "have unqualified, uncertified people working in our classrooms ," she added. "This isn't about the teachers' unions or the Ontario Teacher Federation. The Premier and the minister need to need to take some responsibility here and be accountable for this mess.
"We don't have people who want to work in our schools, and we have people leaving at record rate because of this government's disrespect and lack of resources in education."
Patrick Daly, president of the Ontario Catholic School Trustees' Association, said "teacher and staff shortages remain a significant challenge for many Catholic school boards. We continue to work with Minister Lecce and provincial education partners to look for and introduce immediate and longer-term solutions ."
Lecce has said sick days are a contributing factor.
Teachers take an average of 16 sick days per school year. They are entitled to 11 sick days at full pay, and another 120 days of short-term leave at 90 per cent pay.
Boards have said the cost of hiring fill-ins has also cost them millions and contributed to the deficits.
CAPTION: York Catholic District School Board, unable to find a teacher for a Grade 12 class, is giving students 100 per cent as their midterm grade, even though the kids aren't writing one.

Subject: Catholic schools; Students; Teachers; Education; School boards; Teacher shortages

Document 33 of 500

Politicians also owe Zameer an apology

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/politicians-also-owe-zameer-apology/docview/3043656456/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Politicians+also+owe+Zameer+an+apology&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-04-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If some politicians had their way, an innocent man would have spent almost three years behind bars for a crime he didn't commit.
That would have been the result if the courts had followed the loud public advice - no, demands - of Premier Doug Ford, then-Toronto mayor John Tory and Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown in the case of Umar Zameer, the man charged with first-degree murder in the death of police officer Jeffrey Northrup on July 2, 2021.
When Zameer was freed on bail while awaiting trial, you couldn't keep those three away from their Twitter feeds.
Ford called it "completely unacceptable that the person responsible for this heinous crime is now out on bail" (the wording of his tweet was quickly changed from "responsible for" to "charged with," but the premier had already convicted Zameer in the court of public opinion).
Tory said it was "almost impossible to imagine" granting bail to such a person and Brown chimed in to call the decision "very disturbing" and "shameful."
Those statements looked decidedly foolish now that Zameer has been declared not guilty by a jury after a trial that exposed how weak was the prosecution's case against the 34-year-old accountant.
Justice Anne Molloy, who made no secret of her doubts about the case against Zameer, offered him her "deepest apologies" for the ordeal he was put through.
Those politicians ought to do at least that much. They ought to apologize to Zameer for getting it so wrong and stoking public hostility toward someone who was ultimately found to be no more than a participant in a tragic series of mistakes. They should have known better. They were clearly pandering to public opinion, which was understandably outraged by the death of an on-duty police officer. In the absence of any actual evidence about what happened that day in the parking garage under Toronto City Hall, it was all too tempting to play the "jail, not bail" card.
Two of them (Tory and Brown) are lawyers. They should have been particularly sensitive to the importance of the presumption of innocence - the foundation of our system of criminal law. It's also the underlying reason why people charged with crimes have a presumptive right to bail in most circumstances.
It's not a matter of "coddling criminals," as grandstanding politicians often claim these days. It's a basic principle that people shouldn't be deprived of their freedom until it's proven in a court of law that they actually committed a crime.
The evidence that unfolded during Zameer's four-week trial clearly didn't support the charge of murder. There's no need to rehearse it all here; suffice to say it was unbelievable that a man out celebrating Canada Day with his pregnant wife and two-year-old child would intentionally drive over a police officer. Plainly, the jury didn't believe that. Nor did they believe the testimony of police witnesses, whose version of events was contradicted by video and other physical evidence.
Northrup's death was a tragedy, especially for his family. But the police don't come out of this looking good, either. When Zameer was charged, then-Toronto chief James Ramer called what happened "an intentional, deliberate act" - prejudging the entire judicial process.
Even when Zameer was acquitted, Chief Myron Demkiw couldn't resist saying the force was "hoping for a different outcome." Demkiw may think he was just supporting his officers, but what does he want? A guilty verdict in the face of testimony showing Const. Northrup's death was the result of a terrible mistake, not murder? Didn't he learn anything from the trial - especially the disturbing evidence that police witnesses colluded on a version of events that couldn't be squared with physical evidence from the scene?
All these leaders whipped up public opinion against an innocent man. The fact that Zameer is from Pakistan made it worse, exposing him to xenophobic hatred. The system eventually worked, but no thanks to politicians and others who jumped to conclusions before the facts were in.
They - and the rest of us - should learn some lessons from that. The first is: when you don't really know what happened, just shut up.
Andrew Phillips is a Toronto-based staff columnist for the Star's Opinion page. Reach him via email: aphillips@thestar.ca
CAPTION: Politicians ought to apologize to Umar Zameer for stoking public hostility toward someone who was ultimately found to be no more than a participant in a tragic incident, writes Andrew Phillips.Andrew Francis Wallace Toronto Star
CREDIT: Andrew Phillips OPINION Andrew Phillips is a Toronto-based staff columnist for the Star's Opinion page. Reach him via email: aphillips@thestar.ca;

Families push Ford to disband police in Thunder Bay

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/families-push-ford-disband-police-thunder-bay/docview/3043656455/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Families+push+Ford+to+disband+police+in+Thunder+Bay&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-04-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Indigenous families who have lost faith in Thunder Bay police have lodged a formal complaint with the province to fuel their push to have the troubled force disbanded.
The paperwork - filed Monday with Ontario's inspector general of policing - follows mounting concerns over investigations into the deaths of First Nations people in the northwestern Ontario city, where repeated reports since 2018 have pointed to systemic racism in the police department.
"People are dying. My daughter is gone ... but the province, Doug Ford, has the power to give us justice," Vince Ostberg said at the legislature where three families experiencing losses urged the government to act.
They held pictures of deceased family members as politicians left the chamber following the daily question period.
"If we can't have our loved ones, we want justice," added Ostberg, whose 21-year-old daughter Jenna Ostberg, a member of Bearskin Lake First Nation, was found dead in a Thunder Bay home Dec. 30, 2023.
There had been three 911 calls to police.
Ostberg called the investigation into her death "incomplete" and said he hopes the formal complaint will bolster efforts to have Ontario Provincial Police replace the Thunder Bay Police Service over fears it cannot conduct "credible" investigations.
The Nishnawbe Aski Nation said the death investigations of Ostberg and two others should be assigned to another police service.
"The Thunder Bay Police Service has turned into a 'cold case' factory when it comes to investigations into the deaths of Indigenous Peoples," Grand Chief Alvin Fiddler said.
Pressed on the issue by New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles and MPP Sol Mamakwa (Kiiwetinoong) in the legislature, Solicitor General Michael Kerzner said Thunder Bay's new police chief and police services board have "good intentions" but need time.
"We have to give the new police service board and command leadership an opportunity to work with all community stakeholders so that members of the community feel served and protected," Kerzner told MPPs.
The new chief, Darcy Fleury, and police board chair, Karen Machado, held a news conference last week acknowledging an erosion of trust. Their comments came after the arrests of a former chief and the department's former in-house lawyer in a misconduct investigation by the OPP.
"More can and will be done to communicate with the families about their loved ones' investigations," Fleury said in a statement Monday.
Hired a year ago, Fleury spent 36 years with the Royal Canadian Mounted Police, most recently as district commander and chief superintendent for central Alberta, based in Edmonton. He is Métis and from Manitoba.
"I know TBPS (Thunder Bay Police Service) is under scrutiny. I take very seriously my responsibility to build confidence," Fleury said in the statement.
The former police chief, who was charged with obstructing justice, breach of trust and obstruction, is the third arrested in the investigation that began in late 2021 when Ontario's attorney general asked the OPP to look into allegations of misconduct.
Thunder Bay police have been under the microscope after several reports found investigations into the unexpected deaths of Indigenous people have been marred by stereotyping and racist attitudes. Several deaths have been re-investigated because the initial probes were poorly conducted.
The inspector general of policing is a new agency created under the Community Safety and Policing Act and took effect April 1.
CAPTION: Family members of 14-year-old Mackenzie Moonias,found dead in Thunder Bay in December, are shown Monday in Toronto. Their calls for the Thunder Bay Police Service to be disbanded follow reports that investigations by the force into unexpected deaths of Indigenous people have been marred by stereotyping and racist attitudes.Cole BurstonTHE CANADIANPRESS

Document 35 of 500

Tory says he regrets bail remarks: Former mayor says he's 'learned from this experience'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tory-says-he-regrets-bail-remarks/docview/3043655621/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tory+says+he+regrets+bail+remarks%3A+Former+mayor+says+he%27s+%27learned+from+this+experience%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-04-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Former Toronto mayor John Tory - who was among several politicians to controversially denounce a 2021 decision to release Umar Zameer on bail without knowing the reasons for it - said Monday that he's "learned from this experience" and should have held off on commenting.
On Sunday, Zameer was acquitted of all charges after his jury ultimately rejected the theory he intentionally killed Toronto police Const. Jeffrey Northrup in a parking garage beneath Nathan Phillips Square on July 2, 2021.
Soon after Zameer was released on bail in September 2021, Tory tweeted, "It is almost impossible to imagine a circumstance in which an accused in a case of first-degree murder would be granted bail."
In doing so, he joined Premier Doug Ford and Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown in condemning Justice Jill Copeland's decision - political reactions that critics have argued may have added pressure on the Crown to take the first-degree murder charge to trial.
Ford tweeted at the time "it's completely unacceptable that the person charged for this heinous crime is now out on bail"; Brown described Copeland's decision as "disgusting" and "very disturbing."
Evidence and reasons at bail hearings are covered by a temporary publication ban until the conclusion of a criminal case, to protect the accused person's right to a fair trial.
With the case now over, that publication ban has now lifted, allowing the public to know for the first time that Copeland granted Zameer bail after finding the case for murder was weak.
"There are many learnings. The first for me would be, despite pressure from the media and the public to comment on these kinds of matters, one should wait until significantly important information is available, including judicial reasons," Tory told the Star in an emailed statement.
"The second equally important learning for me comes from my own strong continued belief, which I believe to be shared by many, that the judicial system owes it to the public, public officials and the media to do a better job of ensuring some of that significant information surrounding bail decisions is made widely known. A first-degree murder charge is arguably the most serious charge of all, and everyone needs to know in an open, transparent and timely way the basic reasons behind any decision to grant bail in such a case."
(A publication ban does not prevent a member of the public - including politicians, their staff or journalists - from going to the courthouse and reading a decision before deciding whether to comment on it.)
In a later decision to maintain the publication ban, Copeland was harshly critical of the politicians' comments. (Zameer's lawyers had requested that parts of the ban be lifted, given the inflammatory comments from Tory and others.)
Responding Monday to the Star's questions about his initial comments, Brown said that the verdict "does not alter the reality" for Northrup's family.
His passing was "not only a tragedy for his family, but also a profound loss for our city," Brown said in a statement that did not name Zameer.
Ford's office did not return the Star's request for comment.
With files from Rob Ferguson
CAPTION: Former Toronto mayor John Tory says he should have held off on commenting in 2021 when it was announced that Umar Zameer would be released on bail.Steve Russell Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Jacques Gallant Courts and Justice Reporter With files from Rob Ferguson

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

Document 36 of 500

'I have learned from this experience': Ex-mayor John Tory reflects on comments slamming Umar Zameer bail decision

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/i-have-learned-this-experience-ex-mayor-john-tory/docview/3043974389/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27I+have+learned+from+this+experience%27%3A+Ex-mayor+John+Tory+reflects+on+comments+slamming+Umar+Zameer+bail+decision&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 37 of 500

Torontonians donate $90,000 and counting to Umar Zameer's legal bills

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/torontonians-donate-90-000-counting-umar-zameers/docview/3043974386/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Torontonians+donate+%2490%2C000+and+counting+to+Umar+Zameer%27s+legal+bills&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Torontonians have raised $90,000 and counting for the legal expenses of Umar Zameer, the man acquitted Sunday in the death of Toronto police Const. Jeffrey Northrup.
Zameer has faced mounting legal costs in the three years since his arrest prompted his wife to start an online fundraiser that is only now gaining traction in the wake of Sunday's verdict, reaching $91,200 of its $200,000 goal as of Monday afternoon.
"A case like this is all-consuming," Nader Hasan, one of Zameer's defence lawyers, told the Star in an email.
"An innocent man's life hangs in the balance. There is no excuse for not giving 110 per cent. But that requires time, energy, expert witnesses, private investigators, all of which are expensive - especially for regular folks."
Zameer was arrested and charged with first-degree murder after he ran Northrup over with his car following a chaotic incident in the underground parking garage under Nathan Phillips Square just after midnight on July 2, 2021.
At trial, Zameer testified he did not know he was being approached by plainclothes Toronto police officers after a night out celebrating Canada Day with his wife and two-year-old son. Saying he feared for his life and was trying to flee what he thought were armed criminals, he testified he had no idea he had run Northrup over until after the beloved 55-year-old veteran officer was already dead.
On Sunday, after three days of deliberations, Zameer's jury found him not guilty of both murder and manslaughter, definitively rejecting the Crown's theory that he had intentionally killed Northrup, knowing he was an officer.
"I think today I see that Canada didn't let injustice happen, so I thank Canada," Zameer said, bursting into tears outside the courthouse.
Addressing his lawyers Hasan and Alexandra Heine, he said: "These are not just lawyers. These are angels."
Behind the scenes at trial, Superior Court Justice Anne Molloy was critical of the prosecution's case, saying the evidence contradicted the police eyewitnesses who testified Zameer ran Northrup down intentionally. "What motive would this person have?" she asked prosecutors.
The cost of Zameer's defence would have been considerable, said Toronto criminal defence lawyer Melody Izadi, who was not involved in the case.
The longer the trial, the more complex it becomes and the more costs are incurred, she noted, adding: "The more senior the lawyer, often the higher the fees."
Then there are other complexities, such as the costs of the expert hired by Zameer's counsel and the potential travel costs associated with that expert. That's in addition to the cost of preliminary hearings, five weeks of trial, and two defence lawyers.
"There is a tremendous amount of effort that goes into defending any prosecution," Izadi said. "And defence lawyers often have someone's life in their hands."
The GoFundMe campaign was organized by Zameer's wife, Aaida Shaikh - who also testified - in late July 2021; it has gained enormous traction amid an outpouring of support since the verdict.
"We hope all the families involved can heal from this traumatic experience," one person wrote.
"So relieved justice has been served," said another.
Zameer, who arrived in Canada with his family in 2019, was jailed after his arrest until he was controversially granted bail in September 2021. In that time, he missed the birth of his daughter while becoming the focus of intense public scorn after Toronto police leaders described Northrup's death as "deliberate and intentional" and Premier Doug Ford called his release on bail "beyond comprehension."
Being a newcomer to Canada is hard enough as it is, but that, combined with being a father of three and facing one of the most serious crimes in Canada, takes "an enormous toll," said Husein Panju, chair of the Canadian Muslim Lawyers Association.
"So we're hoping this campaign helps to allay at least some of the financial costs that have got him to this point," he said.
Panju cautioned that the verdict was not a cause for celebration, adding the Canadian Muslim Lawyers Association mourns Const. Northrup's death and acknowledges the difficulty his family is facing.
On Sunday, the officer's widow, Margaret Northrup, said: "We miss Jeff every day. However, we continue on with him in our hearts, never to be forgotten, a hero in life not death."
After the verdict was read out, Justice Molloy took the extra step of apologizing directly to Zameer - telling him: "You have my ... deepest apologies for what you have been through."
CAPTION: Umar Zameer meets reporters outside the Ontario Superior Court of Justice after the verdict on Sunday.

Document 38 of 500

'People are dying": Indigenous families ask Doug Ford to disband Thunder Bay's police force

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/people-are-dying-indigenous-families-ask-doug/docview/3043974333/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27People+are+dying%26quot%3B%3A+Indigenous+families+ask+Doug+Ford+to+disband+Thunder+Bay%27s+police+force&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Indigenous families who have lost faith in Thunder Bay police have lodged a formal complaint with the province to fuel their push to have the troubled force disbanded.
The paperwork - filed Monday with Ontario's inspector general of policing - follows mounting concerns over investigations into the deaths of First Nations people in the northwestern Ontario city, where repeated reports since 2018 have pointed to systemic racism in the police department.
"People are dying. My daughter is gone ... but the province, Doug Ford, has the power to give us justice," Vince Ostberg said at the legislature where three families experiencing losses urged the government to act.
They held pictures of deceased family members as politicians left the chamber following the daily question period.
"If we can't have our loved ones, we want justice," added Ostberg, whose 21-year-old daughter Jenna Ostberg, a member of Bearskin Lake First Nation, was found dead in a Thunder Bay home Dec. 30, 2023.
There had been three 911 calls to police.
Ostberg called the investigation into her death "incomplete" and said he hopes the formal complaint will bolster efforts to have Ontario Provincial Police replace the Thunder Bay Police Service over fears it cannot conduct "credible" investigations.
The Nishnawbe Aski Nation said the death investigations of Ostberg and two others should be assigned to another police service.
"The Thunder Bay Police Service has turned into a 'cold case' factory when it comes to investigations into the deaths of Indigenous Peoples," Grand Chief Alvin Fiddler said.
Pressed on the issue by New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles and MPP Sol Mamakwa (Kiiwetinoong) in the legislature, Solicitor General Michael Kerzner said Thunder Bay's new police chief and police services board have "good intentions" but need time.
"We have to give the new police service board and command leadership an opportunity to work with all community stakeholders so that members of the community feel served and protected," Kerzner told MPPs.
The new chief, Darcy Fleury, and police board chair, Karen Machado, held a news conference a week ago acknowledging an erosion of trust. Their comments came after the arrests of a former chief and the department's former in-house lawyer in a misconduct investigation by the OPP.
"More can and will be done to communicate with the families about their loved ones' investigations," Fleury said in a statement Monday.
Hired a year ago, Fleury spent 36 years with the Royal Canadian Mounted Police, most recently as district commander and chief superintendent for central Alberta, based in Edmonton. He is Métis and from Manitoba.
"I know TBPS (Thunder Bay Police Service) is under scrutiny. I take very seriously my responsibility to build confidence," Fleury said in the statement.
The former police chief, who was charged with obstructing justice, breach of trust and obstruction, is the third arrested in the investigation that began in late 2021 when Ontario's attorney general asked the OPP to look into allegations of misconduct.
Thunder Bay police have been under the microscope after several reports found investigations into the unexpected deaths of Indigenous people have been marred by stereotyping and racist attitudes. Several deaths have been reinvestigated because the initial probes were poorly conducted.
The inspector general of policing is a new agency created under the Community Safety and Policing Act and took effect April 1.
CAPTION: Thunder Bay Police Service Chief Darcy Fleury responds to recent charges against the former Thunder Bay chiefduring a press conference in Thunder Bay, Ont. on April 15, 2024.

Document 39 of 500

Here are the private Ontario colleges getting international student study permits

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/here-are-private-ontario-colleges-getting/docview/3043974183/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here+are+the+private+Ontario+colleges+getting+international+student+study+permits&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Flight centres and religious universities are among the private schools that have been allocated a small number of international student study permits by the province.
With the federal government limiting the number of foreign students, leaving the provinces to sort out how to divvy them up, the Ontario government said it would set aside 96 per cent of applications for public colleges and universities, and the remaining four per cent - or 10,000 - for private universities, language schools and others.
A number of the schools provide training for in-demand sectors, and the language schools, in some cases, help prepare students to move on to programs in the public college system.
The largest recipient was Yorkville University, with two campuses in Greater Toronto, which received 5,154 applications (the number of actual permits will be smaller, depending on who ultimately enrols and how many students immigration officials approve). A distant second was International Business University with 127.
Also on the list is Canada Christian College, run by the controversial Charles McVety - the vocal social conservative preacher who opposed a revamp of the provincial sex-ed curriculum amid accusations against him of homophobia, and whose bid to get the school upgraded to a university was rejected by the provincial post-secondary assessment board.
Among the other schools receiving permits are Brampton Flight Centre, Durham Flight Centre Inc., Toronto Airways Inc., The Michener Institute for Applied Health Sciences, University of Niagara Falls Canada, Redeemer University, St. Philip's Seminary, Tyndale University and Kaplan International Languages.
Most of the schools included are allowed to grant post-graduation work permits under federal government rules, which is why they were included. All permit allocations were based on previous years.
"In response to the federal government's cap on the number of study permit applications issued to international students over the next two years, Ontario has taken a fair, transparent and data-driven approach to allocating applications to the province's post-secondary institutions," said Liz Tuomi, press secretary and senior communications adviser to Colleges and Universities Minister Jill Dunlop.
"Through this approach, the provincial government is protecting the integrity of Ontario's post-secondary education system and prioritizing public post-secondary programs to help prepare graduates for in-demand jobs that support Ontario's labour market needs."
In January, Ottawa decided to clamp down on the surge of international enrolment amid a worsening affordable housing crisis and imposed a two-year cap on the number of new study permits issued for each province. Last month, Ontario revealed it was given 235,000 study permit applications.
The province did not provide any permits to public-private college partnerships, which have been blamed as the reason for the huge influx of international students and resulting housing crunch. The quality of those programs had come under fire, with some classes being delivered in strip malls, leading the federal immigration minister to refer to them as "puppy mills."
But those partnerships were lucrative for public colleges and universities, helping shore up funding as COVID-19, salary increases and inflation have impacted their budgets, and the loss is hitting particularly hard amid an ongoing tuition freeze in Ontario.
Outside of Greater Toronto, publicly assisted colleges including Kitchener-based Conestoga College, Fanshawe College in London and Windsor's St. Clair College have received the largest number of international student application spots from the province - 19,885, 16,752 and 10,753, respectively.
While small public colleges outside of Greater Toronto - especially those in Northern Ontario - are said to be harmed more by federal rules to discontinue issuing postgraduation work permits for graduates from the public-private college partnerships, a spokesperson for St. Clair said he's not been part of or aware of any discussion of merging and closing campuses involving his college.
At Queen's Park, NDP Leader Marit Stiles said fewer foreign students, combined with insufficient funding from the provincial government, will lead to cutbacks and, potentially, schools shutting down.
"This is going to negatively impact a lot of communities, a lot of small towns that rely on their colleges and universities," she said. "How are they going to continue to meet the needs of the community, as well as the province, to produce the workers that they need to produce?"
Algoma is the only public university to see a decline in study permit applications from 2023, but the school said it is in good financial shape and the change aligns with its strategic plan to moderate growth from this year onwards.
"While we recognize the potential impact of the two-year cap on international study permits, this adjustment will not impact our students in Brampton, Sault Ste. Marie or Timmins," said Mohamed El Kahlout, a spokesperson for Algoma, which has been allotted with 3,038 study permit applications by the province.
"It is not expected to impact Algoma University's staffing levels. We remain committed to providing a high-quality and caring university experience for our students across all campuses."
The university's Brampton campus, established in 2009, is the only university in the municipality and offers both bachelor's and master's degree programs, and attracts a lot of international students. El Kahlout said Algoma will continue to grow its Brampton campus infrastructure, programs and support services to meet the needs of the community.
CAPTION: Algoma University's Brampton campus is shown in 2018.

Honda makes multibillion-dollar deal to build electric vehicle factory in Ontario: sources

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/honda-makes-multibillion-dollar-deal-build/docview/3043209658/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Honda+makes+multibillion-dollar+deal+to+build+electric+vehicle+factory+in+Ontario%3A+sources&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Japanese automaker Honda has agreed to establish a major new, multibillion-dollar electric vehicle operation in Ontario, according to two federal government sources.
The deal is expected to be announced this week at Honda's existing factory in Alliston, north of Toronto, where the company will reveal plans to bring a "massive project" to Ontario for the manufacturing of electric vehicles, batteries and other components, a government source with knowledge of the situation told the Star.
The source, who spoke on condition they not be named, said the deal is worth billions of dollars, and would be based on federal funding through investment tax credits - rather than direct subsidies worth billions of dollars that attracted other automakers, including Volkswagen and Stellantis, to set up EV battery plants in Ontario - along with unspecified support from the provincial government.
A second federal government source confirmed the Honda agreement is coming, but did not provide any details.
Media reports revealing the deal prompted Premier Doug Ford to hail what he described as the "largest deal" yet in his government's push - alongside Ottawa and Québec - to attract companies to set up manufacturing in Canada for the emerging industry of electric vehicle and their components.
Speaking in Toronto, Ford said Honda's investment in Alliston would dwarf the massive Stellantis electric vehicle battery factory in Windsor and the even larger Volkswagen project in St. Thomas, each of which received billions of dollars in subsidies from Queen's Park and the federal government.
Swedish battery manufacturer Northvolt has also committed to building a battery mega-factory outside Montreal, with $4.6 billion in subsides from Ottawa and Quebec City.
"Over the past three years alone, we've attracted over $28 billion in auto and EV battery investments - and folks, stay tuned," Ford told the First Nations Major Projects Coalition meeting Monday.
"But this week, we landed a new deal. It will be the largest deal in Canadian history. It'll be double the size of Volkswagen so stay tuned, we'll be announcing it this week," the premier enthused.
Ford noted Stellantis was spending $5 billion on a 4.2-million-square-foot facility in Windsor, and VW another $7 billion on a 16-million-square-foot gigafactory in St. Thomas that will be the fourth largest building in the world.
"Ontario now is the only place in the entire world - think of this - where the six largest automakers call home," he said.
In a statement, Honda Canada spokesperson Ken Chiu declined to confirm the company's plans, but said Honda is "considering a number of initiatives and is in contact with governments at all levels as we move into the electrified era."
Chiu added that, "in order to achieve our 2040 electrification target of 100 per cent electrified vehicles, we are considering various options to increase local production capacity, however we have nothing further to share at this time."
At Queen's Park, provincial Economic Development Minister Vic Fedeli was tight-lipped while touting "the largest Canadian auto announcement ever."
"It's very exciting," said Fedeli, declining to even specifically mention the word Honda due to commercial sensitivities.
"We've never talked about any of our prospects by name but I would say the scenario is that we do have incentives," said the minister, referring to tax breaks and other sweeteners Queen's Park and Ottawa have put in place.
"That's where we think our tax dollars are best invested because we had 100,000 jobs that were at risk in the auto sector," he said, pointing to competition stateside.
"Not only did we save those 100,000 jobs, we've created tens of thousands more."
Asked about Ford Canada recently slashing EV production in Oakville, Fedeli emphasized "no money is distributed to any company until they've met their hiring goals and their construction goals."
Bloomberg first reported that a deal with Honda, which has long been rumoured, was expected to be announced this week.
Honda's potential $18.4-billion auto assembly and EV battery factory would expand the complex 107 kilometres north of Toronto that currently makes gasoline-powered Civics and CR-Vs.
Asked about that dollar figure, a federal government source declined to share specifics but said the total is "in the ballpark" of what will be announced with Honda this week.
Fedeli would only say that Ontario's overall $28 billion in EV investments would balloon to around $43 billion within the next week, suggesting a $15-billion Honda investment.
To Flavio Volpe, head of the Automotive Parts Manufacturers' Association, the prospect such a deal is "incredible." He said it would be an even bigger coup after the federal government signalled there would be no more direct subsidies like those it used to land EV factories from Stellantis, Volkswagen and Northvolt in Quebec.
Volpe pointed to a new measure unveiled in the recent federal budget, which promised a new "electric vehicle supply chain investment tax credit" worth 10 per cent of the cost that companies put toward buildings used in "key segments" of the industry.
Volpe said that tax credit seems tailor-made for major companies like Honda, and noted that Ottawa and the Ontario and Quebec governments are also still trying to court Toyota - another major Japanese automaker - to set up electric vehicle manufacturing in Canada.
If the tax credit helped attract a big company like Honda, that would mark a significant victory for the government's shift from direct subsidies to a simpler offering that doesn't need to be negotiated with the same depth.
That could position Canada to better compete for jobs and manufacturing in the sector with the United States, which has its own massive suite of incentives under President Joe Biden's Inflation Reduction Act.
"I've got to give them full credit, no pun intended, on (potentially) pulling in a big fish - maybe the biggest - with credits," Volpe said.
CAPTION: FILE PHOTO FILE - The logo of the Honda Motor Co. is seen in Yokohama, near Tokyo, Dec. 15, 2021.

Document 41 of 500

Doug Ford, John Tory and Patrick Brown all owe Umar Zameer their 'deepest apologies' as well

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-john-tory-patrick-brown-all-owe-umar/docview/3043209306/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%2C+John+Tory+and+Patrick+Brown+all+owe+Umar+Zameer+their+%27deepest+apologies%27+as+well&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If some politicians had their way, an innocent man would have spent almost three years behind bars for a crime he didn't commit.
That would have been the result if the courts had followed the loud public advice - no, demands - of Premier Doug Ford, then-Toronto mayor John Tory and Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown in the case of Umar Zameer, the man charged with first-degree murder in the death of police officer Jeffrey Northrup on July 2, 2021.
When Zameer was freed on bail while awaiting trial, you couldn't keep those three away from their Twitter feeds.
Ford called it "completely unacceptable that the person responsible for this heinous crime is now out on bail" (the wording of his tweet was quickly changed from "responsible for" to "charged with," but the premier had already convicted Zameer in the court of public opinion).
Tory said it was "almost impossible to imagine" granting bail to such a person and Brown chimed in to call the decision "very disturbing" and "shameful."
Those statements looked decidedly foolish now that Zameer has been declared not guilty by a jury after a trial that exposed how weak was the prosecution's case against the 34-year-old accountant.
Justice Anne Molloy, who made no secret of her doubts about the case against Zameer, offered him her "deepest apologies" for the ordeal he was put through.
Those politicians ought to do at least that much. They ought to apologize to Zameer for getting it so wrong and stoking public hostility toward someone who was ultimately found to be no more than a participant in a tragic series of mistakes.
They should have known better. They were clearly pandering to public opinion, which was understandably outraged by the death of an on-duty police officer. In the absence of any actual evidence about what happened that day in the parking garage under Toronto City Hall, it was all too tempting to play the "jail, not bail" card.
Two of them (Tory and Brown) are lawyers. They should have been particularly sensitive to the importance of the presumption of innocence - the foundation of our system of criminal law. It's also the underlying reason why people charged with crimes have a presumptive right to bail in most circumstances.
It's not a matter of "coddling criminals," as grandstanding politicians often claim these days. It's a basic principle that people shouldn't be deprived of their freedom until it's proven in a court of law that they actually committed a crime.
The evidence that unfolded during Zameer's four-week trial clearly didn't support the charge of murder. There's no need to rehearse it all here; suffice to say it was unbelievable that a man out celebrating Canada Day with his pregnant wife and two-year-old child would intentionally drive over a police officer. Plainly, the jury didn't believe that. Nor did they believe the testimony of police witnesses, whose version of events was contradicted by video and other physical evidence.
Northrup's death was a tragedy, especially for his family. But the police don't come out of this looking good, either. When Zameer was charged, then-Toronto chief James Ramer called what happened "an intentional, deliberate act" - prejudging the entire judicial process.
Even when Zameer was acquitted, Chief Myron Demkiw couldn't resist saying the force was "hoping for a different outcome." Demkiw may think he was just supporting his officers, but what does he want? A guilty verdict in the face of testimony showing Const. Northrup's death was the result of a terrible mistake, not murder? Didn't he learn anything from the trial - especially the disturbing evidence that police witnesses colluded on a version of events that couldn't be squared with physical evidence from the scene?
All these leaders whipped up public opinion against an innocent man. The fact that Zameer is from Pakistan made it worse, exposing him to xenophobic hatred. The system eventually worked, but no thanks to politicians and others who jumped to conclusions before the facts were in.
They - and the rest of us - should learn some lessons from that. The first is: when you don't really know what happened, just shut up.
CAPTION: A nervous Umar Zameer faces the media after jurors found the 34-year-old accountant not guilty of all charges including first- and second-degree murder and manslaughter at Ontario Superior Court of Justice in Toronto on Sunday.

Subject: Public opinion; Evidence; Crime; Police; Acquittals & mistrials; Murders & murder attempts; Manslaughter; Politicians; Apologies; Trials; Criminal law

Title: Doug Ford, John Tory and Patrick Brown all owe Umar Zameer their 'deepest apologies' as well

Document 42 of 500

Zameer trial achieved the correct result - the rest of it was a legal debacle

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/zameer-trial-achieved-correct-result-rest-was/docview/3043209258/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Zameer+trial+achieved+the+correct+result+-+the+rest+of+it+was+a+legal+debacle&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As we exhale a collective sigh of relief following the jury's verdict, there will be no end of talk in days to come about how, in the end, the justice system worked for Umar Zameer. There is a grain of truth to this, but in one respect only. An innocent man - with the help of a top-notch defence team - was ultimately acquitted of murder.
By virtually every other measure, however, the Zameer case stands as an egregious example of how horribly the system can go wrong when emotionalism, tunnel vision and political pressure rule the day. We owe it to the Zameer family and our justice system itself to sift through the embers of this legal debacle and learn from it.
The first blunder was for the police to overcharge Zameer with first-degree murder by relying on a fanciful and untethered theory that he intended to run over a police officer. This is especially true where surveillance video of the event and expert evidence presented by both sides contradicted testimony from the prosecution's three police eyewitnesses and disproved that the hood of Zameer's vehicle struck Const. Jeffrey Northrup as he drove away.
This theory of liability was tested and soundly rejected by the judge who granted Zameer bail soon after he was charged. Justice Copeland deserves great praise for her courage.
Her meticulous decision was instead promptly - and very publicly - disparaged by several political leaders, including Premier Doug Ford and Toronto Mayor John Tory, neither of whom heard any of the evidence that influenced the judge's ruling. This rhetoric led many to believe Zameer was guilty of murder long before his trial began and helped shape a politically-motivated narrative that granting him bail in this case placed the public's safety at risk.
Prosecutors in Ontario must scrutinize each case with a view to determining whether a criminal charge is supported by the evidence, is in the public interest, and meets the modest threshold of having a reasonable prospect of conviction.
Given how much time, money, and scarce court resources were dedicated to this failed case, we must re-evaluate whether a more critical and robust standard ought to be applied in future. For example, in British Columbia, all criminal charges must meet a much higher substantial likelihood of conviction standard before a matter can proceed to trial, ensuring that precious court time is not wasted on hopeless prosecutions at the expense of cases already in danger of being tossed due to lengthy court delays.
The Zameer trial itself left most observers reeling. How could three police eyewitnesses all testify in court to an identical narrative of events that did not conform to the physical and forensic evidence? Defence claims that the officers colluded with each other to strengthen the prosecution should leave many to question whether this has happened before and whether it will happen again.
However, we should not expect that these officers will be held accountable. When asked about their problematic testimony following the verdict, Toronto Police Chief Myron Demkiw said that "today is not the day for that" after expressing his disapproval with the verdict itself.
Recent history suggests that, regardless of judicial findings of misconduct or false evidence from police witnesses, little or nothing is ever done in consequence. Tragically, we can again anticipate the same, unacceptable result.
The Zameer family has endured grievous suffering in the past three years. Despite being released on bail after months in custody, Zameer was confined to his home, banned from driving, and prohibited from leaving the country. The abject fear the family lived in can never be undone.
This is a family that arrived in Canada with the usual immigrant's dream - a fresh start in an exciting and prosperous new land. They ran headlong into some of the worst our justice system can inflict.
Rather than condemning the travesty of justice to which he was subjected, Zameer's parting words after his acquittal were to thank his adopted country. It is difficult to comprehend why we deserve it.
CAPTION: Umar Zameer speaks to media, with lawyers Nader Hasan and Alexandra Heine, left, after jurors found the 34-year-old accountant not guilty of all charges including first- and second-degree murder and manslaughter at Ontario Superior Court of Justice in Toronto on Sunday.
CREDIT: Daniel Brown Contributor

Subject: Evidence; Convictions; Acquittals & mistrials; Murders & murder attempts; Trials

Title: Zameer trial achieved the correct result - the rest of it was a legal debacle
