Trudeau government says it will give more Canadians the rural carbon rebate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-government-says-will-give-more-canadians/docview/3040758602/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+government+says+it+will+give+more+Canadians+the+rural+carbon+rebate&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-The federal government says it will give more people the rural carbon price rebate, one of several measures in Tuesday's budget that touch on parts of the controversial climate policy that have come under fire in recent months.
The move promises to redefine what is classified as 'rural' under the policy, opening the door for more people to qualify for the larger climate rebates given to many Canadians living outside urban centres. It comes as previous reporting from the Star pointed out that not all rural Canadians impacted by the carbon levy are eligible for the extra cash.
There is, however, scant information as to what the rebate expansion will look like and how it may affect the overall distribution of carbon pricing proceeds - seven per cent of which are still owed to small- and medium-sized businesses and Indigenous groups.
The change is one of several budget measures that addresses points of contention in the federal government's carbon pricing scheme, which has come under fierce criticism from opposition Conservatives and provincial premiers who say it is a cost-of-living issue for Canadians. Some of the other moves introduced in the budget include a rebate for businesses that are owed $2.5 billion in returns, forcing banks to identify rebate payments as the government wishes - part of a government effort to raise awareness around the quarterly payouts - and changing the carbon pricing law to allow federal officials to publicly share details regarding non-compliance. The carbon levy is meant to encourage Canadians to make the switch to technologies that pollute less.
"I don't think it's defence. I think it's offence," Natural Resources Minister Jonathan Wilkinson said Wednesday of the new measures.
Under the rules of the rebate policy, the federal government uses Statistics Canada's census metropolitan areas (CMA) to identify rural communities. CMAs are groups of densely populated centres, like many communities in the Greater Toronto Area, that are generally urban areas. For the purposes of the rebates, the government counts all areas outside CMAs as rural.
But StatsCan acknowledges you can still be living in a rural area and within a CMA. By one measure, just over 500,000 Ontarians live in rural areas within CMAs and are denied the extra cash. For a family of four in Ontario, that adds up to an extra $224 this year.
It has frustrated some small-town mayors and some MPs, including one Tory MP who told the Star it was a politically motivated decision to benefit Liberal ridings. In the fall, when Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced the top-up would double to 20 per cent from 10 per cent, the government also decided it would use CMAs according to the 2016 census rather than 2021, which federal officials said was a deliberate policy choice meant to expand eligibility. That meant Fredericton, Red Deer and their surrounding areas, for example, remained eligible.
Marcus Powlowski, Liberal MP for Thunder Bay, told the Star Wednesday he had pushed his party on this issue because he felt it was creating the perception that the so-called carbon tax isn't fair, and that was hurting the government.
"I think there's quite a few (Liberal MPs) who are in that category that have at least parts of their ridings, especially places which don't have natural gas, don't have public transport, but they're still considered urban, so it doesn't make a lot of sense," he said.
Asked if the issue had undermined his support for the party's carbon pricing regime, he said "possibly."
"It's a matter of good governance," he told the Star. "If you're rural, you should get the rebate. If you're not, well, you shouldn't."
Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland's office had not returned a request for comment by Wednesday evening. In the past, federal officials had defended the use of CMAs, arguing it was a "clearly defined and well-understood criterion" that treated Canadians consistently.
But according to Tuesday's budget, that is set to change.
That news was cautiously welcomed by John Creelman, the mayor of Mono, Ont., a town of 9,000 an hour-and-a-half drive north of Toronto.
Despite having no hospital or public transit, Mono is grouped in with the Toronto CMA, making its rural residents ineligible for the top-up. In March, the town council passed a motion demanding the government reform eligibility requirements. Creelman now hopes his town's residents would get the top-up.
"It makes me optimistic that they've realized the error of using an arbitrary mechanism to define 'rural' vs. 'urban,'" he told the Star. "But I'm disappointed that it may take a year to change."
CAPTION: Minister of Energy and Natural Resources Jonathan Wilkinson rises during Question Period in the House of Commons on Parliament Hill in Ottawa on April 10, 2024.

Ontario backtracks UP Express service changes amid backlash

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-backtracks-up-express-service-changes/docview/3040054381/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+backtracks+UP+Express+service+changes+amid+backlash&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The province is walking back changes it announced to its UP Express service at two popular stations just a day ago after thousands of Torontonians cried foul.
Ontario's Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria said in a statement on X Tuesday evening that he has directed Metrolinx not to proceed with planned changes that would have reduced the frequency of trains at two transit stations: Bloor station (at Dundas Street West) and Weston station.
"While the intention was to provide a faster option for people travelling between Union and Pearson, I've heard riders' concerns about servicing Weston and Bloor stations along the UP Express line," Sarkaria said.
The move to reduce service at the popular stops - which line up with TTC connections - from every 15 minutes to every half-hour was buried in other transit announcements Monday.
Over 5,000 UP Express riders expressed their dismay with the changes in an online petition calling for the decision, which would have affected city transit commute times for many considerably, to be reversed.
Earlier in the day at Queen's Park, Sarkaria had defended the changes - despite the petition and outrage on talk radio and social media.
"It is going to be an express every 30 minutes because it was always intended to be a connection between Union and Pearson," he assured reporters at the legislature around noon.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, whose Davenport riding constituency office is near the Bloor station, had pilloried the Progressive Conservatives for the decision.
"We are now spending more, again, to get less service for the people at Weston and the people at Dundas West," said Stiles.
"For a lot of people, they're going to be waiting a lot longer to get where they need to go, to get to work and to get back home," she said.
UP riders were quick to register their dismay, including in an online petition calling for the decision, which will affect city transit commute times for many considerably, to be reversed.
Prior to Tuesday night's backtracking, Green Leader Mike Schreiner said "what the government's doing to reduce service on the UP Express makes absolutely no sense."
"The UP Express has become a critical transit link to get people in the west end to Dundas West and Weston stations and into Union or out to the airport. Why don't we maximize utilization of our transit?" said Schreiner.
With files from Robert Benzie and Jim Rankin.
CAPTION: The province is walking back changes it announced to its UP Express service amid backlash.

Subject: Petitions

Title: Ontario backtracks UP Express service changes amid backlash

With increased flights, Pearson needs to manage nighttime noise

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/with-increased-flights-pearson-needs-manage/docview/3039908362/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=With+increased+flights%2C+Pearson+needs+to+manage+nighttime+noise&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto airports authority announces 'decade-long investment' in Pearson Airport, April 12
While the article notes that passenger traffic is expected to increase to 65 million passengers by the early 2030s it does not reveal that along with this growth there will be a proportionate increase in the number of night flights and therefore nighttime noise.
Pearson airport has one of the weakest programs for managing nighttime noise. Through a bizarre formula, Transport Canada permits the annual budget for nighttime operations to increase at the same rate as passenger growth. When the GTAA began operating the airport in 1996 the budget was around 9,600 movements annually. Today it is about 21,000 and with the projected 65 million passengers it will increase to 26,000 or more. Notably, the annual budget for night flights London Heathrow is only 5,800 movements!
There is no doubt that the facilities need to be modernized but it would give residents a "LIFT" if the GTAA also paid some attention to reducing the impact of the airport's nighttime operations.
Tom Driedger, Toronto
George Yonge's legacy is more problematic than that of Henry DundasChris Moise: The enigma, April13
I am sorry, but not surprised, that my city councillor Chris Moise experienced hatred and discrimination as a Black person, but Henry Dundas had nothing to do with it. He was a consistent and effective advocate for abolishing slavery. Fortunately, the proposed renaming of all of Dundas Street has been averted, but Yonge-Dundas Square? Sir George Yonge was a rascal who made money out of the slave trade while governor in Cape Colony (South Africa). Henry Dundas was the cabinet minister who had him recalled. He had earlier, as a lawyer, won a case that resulted in freeing all slaves in Scotland. Moise says that "community leaders" say he was right, but he/they are wrong if you look at the facts. I offered him a briefing, but he declined. Asked to take part in a town hall at Innis College on the subject - I would be another panelist - he failed even to reply to repeated invitations.
Lynn McDonald, Royal Historical Society, Toronto
Another reason to end public funding Toronto's Catholic board is being urged to fly the 'pro-life' flag at every school in May. Trustees are at odds over it, April 10
Another blatant reason to remove public funding for Catholic schools.
Marcella Draak, Guelph
Slogans and derision won't cut it as climate policyJustin Trudeau is facing an all-out carbon levy revolt. There is a way to fight back, April 2
Prime Minister Trudeau should convene a televised First Ministers' conference, not just on the carbon levy - as important as it is - but on the broader question of how we're going to come together to deal effectively with climate change. The agenda must require those opposed to the levy - and in some cases seemingly all actions to reduce emissions - to come prepared with what they would do and provide evidence as to why it is better than current policy. The only thing not open to debate is Canada's commitment to net zero in 2050. Canadians will have a chance to see who's serious about our climate commitments and who's just looking to score political points. They can then decide, based on informed opinion, which approaches to support. Slogans and derision aren't going to cut it as climate policy. It's time for those who oppose current policy to lay their cards on the table and deal with this critical matter constructively.
Richard Schertzer, Milton

It appears that Premier Doug Ford takes the saying "it's the small things that matter" very seriously. Buck a beer, free license plate renewal, free paper bags. The first elected him, the second re-elected him, will the third do likewise.
Alberto Sarthou, Toronto

If only you would acknowledge CLIMATE CHANGE you would know that all of us, you included Premier, must endure some small sacrifices to secure a decent future for ourselves and our children. What about equal access to High Park
High Park is a better place without vehicles, April 1
While the letter writers are happy to espouse the virtues of a vehicle-free park, I see a different issue - one of access. I am an able-bodied person and use transit. I see regulars who are not - along with their companions. There is enough area in High Park that is vehicle free for all to enjoy natures rapture without hindering those who are incapable of getting to and fro. The impact of a vehicle free park also affects residents on side streets. I also wonder at the financial effect this has on our cash strapped city and the groups that use various programs located within the park such as the hockey rink and the pool and the Grenadier restaurant.
Carol Irvine, Toronto

Bank description doesn't provide a clue as to what government deposit is forTrudeau government wants banks to use the word 'rebate' when you get your carbon levy refund - but a source says one bank is flat out refusing, April 15
I am a CIBC customer and my bank account recently showed a credit with just the following explanation: "Electronics Funds Transfer DEPOSIT CANADA." While I knew the payment was from the Canadian government, I had no clue what it represented. Then I logged into the Canada Revenue Agency website and learned that the payment was my "Canada carbon rebate." Incidentally, I get the same brief explanation for my Old Age Security and certain other payments from the Canadian government. I certainly hope, as CIBC spokesperson Stephanie Marcus stated, that CIBC "is still looking at options for labelling deposits."
Alan Pellettier, Scarborough

Document 88 of 500

Modern misery requires a public stamp of attention before it registers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/modern-misery-requires-public-stamp-attention/docview/3039703145/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Modern+misery+requires+a+public+stamp+of+attention+before+it+registers&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hard-luck stories have become a rich industry, once niche but now mainstream. The phenomenon is spreading like a puddle in a big rain.
Barring measurable economic problems, it's not necessarily true that people's luck is harder than it was before, it's that modern misery requires a public stamp of attention before it registers. People want affirmation.
In these times, they get harsh judgment instead and it hurts worse. This worries me. People get blasted with rage because they tell the world they made a decision, and all decisions are wrong.
Long ago, newspapers used to run a photo feature called Wednesday's Child, weekly listing the good points of a child up for adoption. (This is true.) They would end with "Sure, Lacey can be a bit of a handful but she'll settle in."
The modern hard-luck story is just a variation on Wednesday's Child, full of woe. Go Fund Me. Validate me. But what if there are no takers?
Memoirs based on trauma - an extensive and largely self-defined phenomenon - crowd the market; some traumas tiny, some lacerating, but invariably end with immigrants' anxiety-ridden children finding happiness by rediscovering their ethnicity. This is improbable.
All American memoirs end with the claim that everyone can heal, which is simply not true. I think it's more true that people can get used to anything, which makes memoirs kind of pointless.
Reddit's AITA ("Am I the a--hole?") posts draw tens of thousands of judgments, mostly "yes" but also astonishment about the things people have not yet learned, like laundry, courtesy, living on one's own dime, and "My boyfriend likes to be called 'daddy' in bed but when I told him to call me 'grandma' he got mad."
All immigration court stories are the same, a wearying exercise in implausible claims by people in impossible situations. Someone broke the rules and wants a break from the rule-enforcers, which is far beyond the remit of even that rare commodity, sympathetic readers.
I would make a terrible immigration judge. I would tear a dodgy claimant's farcical case to pieces in court and then say, "Are those your kids? Well, they're adorable. So stipulated! Yes, based not on the humanitarian grounds so frequently trotted out, but on photogeneity alone, you are one lucky landed immigrant." I'd let everyone in.
Readers' hearts are hard because there are so many hard-luck stories. Take rentals. Having been a tenant long ago, I find landlords wrong by definition.
But then there are landlord stories. Tenants had farm animals inside the house, neither goat nor lamb. A smell of acetone prevailed throughout. Hissing and boiling noises.
I can't decide who's right and since Doug Ford's Ontario doesn't have a functioning landlord and tenant tribunal, no one can. Readers weigh in. Renting sucks. You should buy.
Oh no. Home-ownership heartbreak manufactures the bulk of hard-luck stories. Readers point out the obvious, that your problem wasn't the bank or interest rates, it was that you couldn't afford that house/neighbourhood in the first place. You had no wiggle room.
So many problems ranging from tiny to minuscule, so many agony aunts. The Washington Post has Miss Manners ("a simple no will suffice"), Ask Amy ("Mom worried about daughter's alcoholism"), Ask Sahaj ("My immigrant parents annoy me"), Carolyn Hax ("Mom mortifies teens by asking server to replace dirty glass") and Work Advice ("My co-worker smells"), plus Readers' Questions to which reporters respond irritably that they've been answering that for seven years on the beat, look it up.
Since I won't read its Reaganite opinion page and my immersion in agony aunt-ics has made me think humans are a failed concept, there's little left to read post-Bezos layoffs. I shall cancel.
Certain horror stories obliterate all others. Gaza is one. Climate change is another. Are Wednesday's Child tales popular because we cannot countenance the worst stories of all? Very possibly. What a lucky country we are.
CAPTION: "Memoirs based on trauma - an extensive and largely self-defined phenomenon - crowd the market; some traumas tiny, some lacerating, but invariably end with immigrants' anxiety-ridden children finding happiness by rediscovering their ethnicity. This is improbable," writes Heather Mallick.

Subject: Autobiographies; Ethnicity; Immigrants; Immigration

Document 89 of 500

Metrolinx walks back UPX cuts: Transportation minister reverses course on service changes after opposition from thousands

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/metrolinx-walks-back-upx-cuts/docview/3039703103/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Metrolinx+walks+back+UPX+cuts%3A+Transportation+minister+reverses+course+on+service+changes+after+opposition+from+thousands&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-04-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The province is walking back changes it announced to its UP Express service at two popular stations Monday after thousands of Torontonians cried foul.
Ontario's Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria said in a statement on X Tuesday evening that he directed Metrolinx not to proceed with planned changes that would have reduced the frequency of trains at the Bloor station (at Dundas Street West) and the Weston station.
"While the intention was to provide a faster option for people travelling between Union and Pearson, I've heard riders' concerns about servicing Weston and Bloor stations along the UP Express line," Sarkaria's statement said.
The move to reduce service at the popular stops - which line up with TTC connections - from every 15 minutes to every half-hour as of April 28 was buried in other transit announcements Monday.
In a written statement, Metrolinx spokesperson Andrea Ernesaks confirmed with the Star that the transit agency is no longer moving ahead with those changes.
"We will be posting updated schedules in the coming days to reflect this direction," she said.
More than 5,000 UP Express riders expressed their dismay with the changes in an online petition calling for the decision, which would have considerably affected city transit commute times for many, to be reversed.
Hayley Fuller, the 34-year-old Weston resident who spearheaded the petition, said it's "incredible" to see the petition's traction and its direct impact.
"It's often easy to fall into hopelessness or apathy when you read an announcement that says, 'We're taking something away from you that you value so much,'" Fuller told the Star.
Fuller said her efforts were driven by the need to advocate for "transit equity" in an area of the city, around Weston station, that has traditionally been underserved.
"This is a neighbourhood that hasn't seen any significant social service or transit expansion since the 1970s," she said, noting how delighted community members were when the UP service provided an opportunity to change that.
"We're also a community full of people working shift work, minimum wage jobs and skilled labour jobs," Fuller continued. "Those people deserve transit equity just as much as those privileged enough to work an office job."
In a statement on X, Mayor Olivia Chow also expressed her contentment with the reversal.
"This is good news for the thousands of workers and travellers who rely on regular and reliable service along this corridor," she said.
Earlier Tuesday at Queen's Park, Sarkaria had defended the changes - despite the petition and outrage on talk radio and social media.
"It is going to be an express every 30 minutes because it was always intended to be a connection between Union and Pearson," he assured reporters at the legislature around noon.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, whose Davenport riding constituency office is near the Bloor station, had pilloried the Progressive Conservatives for the decision.
"We are now spending more, again, to get less service for the people at Weston and the people at Dundas West," Stiles said.
Prior to Tuesday night's backtracking, Green Leader Mike Schreiner said the decision "makes absolutely no sense."
"The UP Express has become a critical transit link to get people in the west end to Dundas West and Weston stations and into Union or out to the airport. Why don't we maximize utilization of our transit?" said Schreiner.
With files from Robert Benzie and Jim Rankin
CAPTION: In an online petition, more than 5,000 UP Express riders expressed their dismay with Metrolinx's plan to reduce the frequency of trains at the Bloor station (at Dundas Street West) and the Weston station.Richard LautensToronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Mahdis Habibinia Toronto Star With files from Robert Benzie and Jim Rankin

Document 90 of 500

Highway 413 construction could begin next year

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/highway-413-construction-could-begin-next-year/docview/3039703070/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Highway+413+construction+could+begin+next+year&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-04-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives say construction on their promised Highway 413 could begin as early as 2025 after a landmark environmental deal with Ottawa.
"I'm hoping to get shovels in the ground within the next year," Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria told reporters at Queen's Park on Tuesday.
His comments came the morning after a major impasse to the planned 52-kilometre freeway from Highway 401 in Milton to Highway 400 in Vaughan was cleared. On Monday, the federal and provincial governments struck a joint working group to "recommend appropriate measures to minimize environmental impacts" of the highway.
"We've come to a conclusion with our federal partners on this to ensure - from an environmental perspective through that working group - that all issues are being addressed, but we're very excited to now move forward and have that certainty to be able to build this highway," said Sarkaria.
Federal Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault, who had been a thorn in the side of the highway scheme, said Monday that "this agreement shows Canada and Ontario's ability to work together while recognizing their shared jurisdiction on matters to do with the environment."
But NDP Leader Marit Stiles blasted the federal Liberals for capitulating and warned 413 would be an unlucky number for Ontario taxpayers and the environment.
"A lot of that is about ... Doug Ford giving his insider friends a sweetheart deal just like he did with the Greenbelt so we don't trust them," said Stiles, referring to the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal under investigation by the RCMP.
Liberal house leader John Fraser said the freeway, which could take years and more than $10 billion to build, is ripe for potential cronyism.
"Once that highway becomes reality almost automatically that land goes up in value and so it's about land speculation and about the premier rewarding friends and insiders," said Fraser.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner, noting the highway would cut through the Greenbelt of protected lands while paving over thousands of acres of farmland and criss-crossing local streams and rivers more than 100 times, said he was "very disappointed with the federal government (for) not upholding their responsibility to protect endangered species."
Phil Pothen of Environmental Defence, a group opposed to the 413, also expressed alarm at the moves from Ottawa.
Unless the Liberals change course, "it would be a willing and reckless betrayal of everyone in Ontario who is experiencing unprecedented attacks on their communities by the provincial government and has trusted federal MPs to do their job and protect the environment," said Pothen.
There has been pressure on Ford's Tories to show progress on the highway, a cornerstone promise of their 2022 "Get It Done" re-election campaign. While the Tories won every riding along the proposed route, which at the time was estimated to be as long as 60 kilometres, little has happened since and they are scrambling with the June 2026 election on the horizon.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 91 of 500

Liberals must play small ball on care economy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-must-play-small-ball-on-care-economy/docview/3039702898/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+must+play+small+ball+on+care+economy&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-04-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Chrystia Freeland's Budget 2024 lets the federal Liberals play two styles of baseball.
It hits what could arguably be called a home run with its housing plan unveiled last week. Now it needs to ready the team for playing small ball in the care economy. That's the only way it can tackle the key concerns of Canadian voters and close the popularity gap that the Conservative party has opened.
The Liberals' housing plan is the most comprehensive set of policies we've seen since the end of the Second World War, narrated clearly and effectively by Housing Minister Sean Fraser. It finally addresses the rapidly expanding population of renters, more than doubling previous commitments. A finance department official confirmed Budget 2024 adds $19.5 billion over the next five years for housing, including the creation of 30,000 new purpose-built apartments. Half that money is in the form of loans, so taxpayers will get much of it back.
While the pace of builds is accelerating, so is the pace of renovictions and short-term rentals since the Bank of Canada started hiking interest rates in 2022. In order to preserve the affordable housing stock we still have, the budget also allocates $1 billion in loans, and not quite $500 million in grants to non-profits.
But in the decade from 2011 to 2021, Canada was losing 15 affordable rental units for every new unit built. We can't build fast enough. Your grandma was right: An ounce of protection is worth a pound of cure.
Real estate investment trusts and pools of private equity are buying up housing and churning out bigger than average returns since supply is so scarce.
Budget 2024 says, "When purchasing a home, Canadians might expect to be bidding against other potential buyers, not a multibillion-dollar hedge fund. ... Budget 2024 announces that the government intends to restrict the purchase and acquisition of existing single-family homes by very large, corporate investors." Expect consultations soon, and details in the 2024 Fall Economic Statement.
Preventing erosion of capacity and siphoning of wealth is a big concern in the care economy too, which is also in the crosshairs of private equity. In provinces like Ontario and around the world, private equity is scooping up small profitable providers, squeezing profits from public funding by lowering staffing levels and lobbying for less qualified staff to lower labour costs. These owners also charge operators more for rent and supplies, then lobby for higher public subsidies and fees. This can and must be stopped.
In politics, not everything can be a home run. All governments will be hard-pressed to avoid the accelerating dysfunction in health care, long-term care, and child care. Beyond more funding, the federal government can adopt small ball strategies to methodically advance a humane agenda, moving players around the bases slowly and strategically. Here are three ways federal small ball could deliver big results without big spending in the coming months.
Child care workforce deals
Round two of the Canada-wide Early Learning and Childcare Agreements are now being negotiated, with a focus on workforce attraction and retention. Funds for these deals are already baked into the budget. The new agreements need enforceable requirements for staffing levels, qualifications of staff, wage grids commensurate with training, and pensions. Such rules could pay big dividends for frustrated parents who can see existing child care spaces not available because of staff shortages and massive turnover.
Private equity
Build on the housing plan statement that "the role of private equity in our housing market needs to be addressed." The federal government should be tracking trends in the investments occurring in our long-term-care, child-care and health-care sectors, following the U.S., U.K., Australia, Europe and the Nordic countries, who are all examining ways of putting new guardrails on public funding.
Community benefits agreements
Care services such as child care, long-term care, medical or dental community clinics can be a built-in feature of new housing and infrastructure developments. Vancouver has led the way in such initiatives, increasing access to care while shielding non-profit providers from future rent hikes within for-profit buildings. Every federal dollar should do double duty like this wherever possible.
It's obvious why housing took centre stage in this budget. Freeland only had the opportunity for one home run, given her adversaries' weaponization of any new spending the Liberals undertake. But housing isn't all people need, and the care economy has galloped up the list of Canadians' concerns.
Child care, long-term care and health care has been crumbling all across Canada in the past year, a function of rising demand and shortage of supply, whether spaces, beds, or staff. Just last week another person asked for, and received, Medical Assistance in Dying in Quebec because the health system totally failed to meet their care needs. These needs can't wait. The provinces absolutely need to step up, but the feds can do more.
So, yes, it's disappointing there was no big answer to the challenges in the care economy in this federal budget; but it's understandable, especially after committing $200 billion over 10 years for health care last year. If the Liberals now tighten their focus on the workforce, private equity, and community benefits, every federal dollar could deliver more for the care economy, with life-altering changes along the way.
This is the hard, painstaking work of government. Small ball may not be a glamorous way to play the game, but so long as the runners keep moving around the bases on care economy reform, at least we'll keep scoring wins.
CREDIT: Armine Yalnizyan OPINION

Subject: Workforce planning; Long term health care; Child care; Private equity; Rents; Budgets; Rentals; Affordable housing; Funding; Profits; Workforce

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Moise%3A+The+enigma%2C+April+13+Justin+Trudeau+is+facing+an+all-+out+carbon+levy+revolt.+There+is+a+way+to+fight+back%2C+April+2+Doug+Ford+forces+LCBO+to+reinstate+paper+bags+for+customers%2C+April+8+Toronto+airports+authority+announces+%27decade-long+investment%27+in+Pearson+%2C+April+12+Donald+Trump+dresses+like+a+human+American+flag.+Here%27s+what+he%27s+saying+with+his+court+wardrobe%2C+April+15+Toronto%27s+Catholic+board+being+urged+to+fly+the+%27pro-life%27+flag+at+every+school+in+May.+Trustees+are+at+odds+over+it%2C+April+10&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-04-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Yonge is a lot
more problematic
than Dundas
Moise: The enigma, April 13
I am sorry, but not surprised, that my city councillor, Chris Moise, experienced hatred and discrimination as a Black person, but Henry Dundas had nothing to do with it. He was a consistent and effective advocate for abolishing slavery. Fortunately, the proposed renaming of all of Dundas Street has been averted, but Yonge-Dundas Square? Sir George Yonge was a rascal who made money out of the slave trade while governor in Cape Colony (South Africa). Henry Dundas was the cabinet minister who had him recalled. He had earlier, as a lawyer, won a case that resulted in freeing all slaves in Scotland. Moise says that "community leaders" say he was right, but he/they are wrong if you look at the facts. I offered him a briefing, but he declined. Asked to take part in a town hall at Innis College on the subject - I would be another panelist - he failed even to reply to repeated invitations.
Lynn McDonald,
Royal Historical Society, Toronto
Slogans, derision won't cut it as climate policy
Justin Trudeau is facing an all- out carbon levy revolt. There is a way to fight back, April 2
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau should convene a televised First Ministers' conference, not just on the carbon levy - as important as it is - but on the broader question of how we're going to come together to deal effectively with climate change. The agenda must require those opposed to the levy - and in some cases seemingly all actions to reduce emissions - to come prepared with what they would do and provide evidence as to why it is better than current policy. The only thing not open to debate is Canada's commitment to net zero in 2050. Canadians will have a chance to see who's serious about our climate commitments and who's just looking to score political points. They can then decide, based on informed opinion, which approaches to support. Slogans and derision aren't going to cut it as climate policy. It's time for those who oppose current policy to lay their cards on the table and deal with this critical matter constructively.
Richard Schertzer, Milton
It's the small things that get Ford re-elected
It appears that Premier Doug Ford takes the saying "it's the small things that matter" very seriously. Buck a beer, free licence plate renewal, free paper bags. The first elected him, the second re-elected him, will the third do likewise.
Alberto Sarthou, Toronto
Premier should put the paper bag over his head
Doug Ford forces LCBO to reinstate paper bags for customers, April 8
First it was the carbon tax Doug Ford opposed, now he wants paper bags back at the LCBO. The carbon tax is the price we pay for pollution. The bags will only contribute to our landfills. If only the premier would acknowledge climate change, he would know that all of us must endure some small sacrifices to secure a decent future for ourselves and our kids. Perhaps when climate change causes even more food insecurity, even he may wake up because then even the rich will suffer.
Phyllis Paroshy, Guelph
Pearson needs
to manage nighttime noise
Toronto airports authority announces 'decade-long investment' in Pearson , April 12
While the article notes that passenger traffic is expected to increase to 65 million passengers by the early 2030s it does not reveal that along with this growth there will be a proportionate increase in the number of night flights and therefore nighttime noise.
Pearson airport has one of the weakest programs for managing nighttime noise. Through a bizarre formula, Transport Canada permits the annual budget for nighttime operations to increase at the same rate as passenger growth. When the GTAA began operating the airport in 1996 the budget was around 9,600 movements annually. Today it is about 21,000 and with the projected 65 million passengers it will increase to 26,000 or more. Notably, the annual budget for night flights London Heathrow is only 5,800 movements!
There's no doubt that the facilities need to be modernized but it'd give residents a lift if the GTAA also paid some attention to reducing the impact of the airport's nighttime operations.
Tom Driedger, Toronto
Who cares about Trump's clothes?
Donald Trump dresses like a human American flag. Here's what he's saying with his court wardrobe, April 15
I can't imagine what possessed you to print an article detailing Donald Trump's fashion choices. This seems to send a message that his clothing choices would be of interest somehow. But to whom, I really have no idea. The man is a misogynistic, bombastic degrader of women, minorities and the disabled. He has been convicted of defamation and faces close to 100 criminal and civil legal suits. For you to parade him like this is obscene. What's next - the interior design stylings of Vladimir Putin? Please do better.
Jim Gough, Toronto
Government deposit should be labelled better
Trudeau government wants banks to use the word 'rebate' when you get your carbon levy refund - but a source says one bank is flat out refusing, April 15
I am a CIBC customer and my bank account recently showed a credit with just the following explanation: "Electronics Funds Transfer DEPOSIT CANADA." While I knew the payment was from the Canadian government, I had no clue what it represented. Then I logged into the Canada Revenue Agency website and learned that the payment was my "Canada carbon rebate." Incidentally, I get the same brief explanation for my Old Age Security and certain other payments from the Canadian government. I certainly hope, as CIBC spokesperson Stephanie Marcus stated, that CIBC "is still looking at options for labelling deposits."
Alan Pellettier, Scarborough
Another reason to end public funding
Toronto's Catholic board being urged to fly the 'pro-life' flag at every school in May. Trustees are at odds over it, April 10
Another reason to remove public funding for Catholic schools.
Marcella Draak, Guelph

Toronto Star picks up six CAJ award nominations

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/toronto-star-picks-up-six-caj-award-nominations/docview/3039702861/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+Star+picks+up+six+CAJ+award+nominations&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-04-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Toronto Star earned six nominations at the 2023 Canadian Association of Journalists (CAJ) awards that celebrate investigative reporting excellence.
Star journalists, along with reporters from regional papers in the Torstar family, received nominations in eight different categories.
In March, the Star was also nominated for eight National Newspaper Awards, capping off a year of investigative work that covered human trafficking rings, growing neo-Nazi communities and the Ontario government's Greenbelt scandal.
Written news
The Star's Sheila Wang and Noor Javed were nominated for collaborations with the Narwhal's Emma McIntosh and Charlie Pinkerton for their sustained coverage of Premier Doug Ford's Greenbelt scandal, which triggered a reversal from the government, the resignations of two cabinet ministers and a criminal investigation by the RCMP.
Data journalism
Declan Keogh, Max Binks-Collier and Naama Weingarten of the Investigative Journalism Bureau and investigative reporter Robert Cribb were nominated for their "Patient files" investigation that looked into patient survey data from a number of Ontario hospitals.
Scoop
The Star's Wendy Gillis was nominated for her scoop that broke the news of Toronto police's dated drug lockers that left the door open to officer theft.
Daily excellence
The Hamilton Spectator's Fallon Hewitt was nominated for the daily excellence award for her coverage of a Stoney Creek couple killed in what was described as a "landlord-tenant dispute."
Community written feature
Grant LaFleche, another reporter with the Torstar-owned Hamilton Spectator, was nominated for his investigation into Hamilton's fast-growing neo-Nazi community under the CAJ's community written feature award.
JHR/CAJ award for
human rights reporting
Investigative reporter Brendan Kennedy was nominated for his "Caged by Canada" investigation that looked into Ontario's continued practice of housing immigration detainees in jails.
CWA Canada/CAJ award
for labour reporting
For the second year in a row, a Star reporter duo has earned a nomination for the CWA Canada/CAJ Award for Labour Reporting. The Star's Work and Wealth reporter Sara Mojtehedzadeh and investigative reporter Rachel Mendleson were nominated for their multi-part "Work forced" investigation looking into labour trafficking and exploitation, which was also nominated for a National Newspaper Award.
Environmental
and climate change
Climate change reporter Kate Allen earned a nomination in the environmental and climate change reporting category for her deep dive into lettuce, and produce more broadly, and how worsening crop conditions in California could spell "the fall of the romaine empire."
Winners for the CAJ awards will be announced at a gala scheduled for June 1 at the Toronto Public Library's Bram & Bluma Appel Salon.
CREDIT: Andy Takagi Toronto Star

Document 94 of 500

Modern misery goes mainstream: People get blasted with rage because they dare to tell the world they made a decision

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/modern-misery-goes-mainstream/docview/3039702821/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Modern+misery+goes+mainstream%3A+People+get+blasted+with+rage+because+they+dare+to+tell+the+world+they+made+a+decision&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-04-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hard-luck stories have become a rich industry, once niche but now mainstream. The phenomenon is spreading like a puddle in a big rain.
Barring measurable economic problems, it's not necessarily true that people's luck is harder than it was before, it's that modern misery requires a public stamp of attention before it registers. People want affirmation.
In these times, they get harsh judgment instead and it hurts worse. This worries me. People get blasted with rage because they tell the world they made a decision, and all decisions are wrong.
Long ago, newspapers used to run a photo feature called Wednesday's Child, weekly listing the good points of a child up for adoption. (This is true.) They would end with "Sure, Lacey can be a bit of a handful but she'll settle in."
The modern hard-luck story is just a variation on Wednesday's Child, full of woe. Go Fund Me. Validate me. But what if there are no takers?
Memoirs based on trauma - an extensive and largely self-defined phenomenon - crowd the market; some traumas tiny, some lacerating, but invariably end with immigrants' anxiety-ridden children finding happiness by rediscovering their ethnicity. This is improbable.
All American memoirs end with the claim that everyone can heal, which is simply not true. I think it's more true that people can get used to anything, which makes memoirs kind of pointless.
Reddit's AITA ("Am I the a--hole?") posts draw tens of thousands of judgments, mostly "yes" but also astonishment about the things people have not yet learned, like laundry, courtesy, living on one's own dime, and "My boyfriend likes to be called 'daddy' in bed but when I told him to call me 'grandma' he got mad."
All immigration court stories are the same, a wearying exercise in implausible claims by people in impossible situations. Someone broke the rules and wants a break from the rule-enforcers, which is far beyond the remit of even that rare commodity, sympathetic readers.
I would make a terrible immigration judge. I would tear a dodgy claimant's farcical case to pieces in court and then say, "Are those your kids? Well, they're adorable. So stipulated! Yes, based not on the humanitarian grounds so frequently trotted out, but on photogeneity alone, you are one lucky landed immigrant." I'd let everyone in.
Readers' hearts are hard because there are so many hard-luck stories. Take rentals. Having been a tenant long ago, I find landlords wrong by definition.
But then there are landlord stories. Tenants had farm animals inside the house, neither goat nor lamb. A smell of acetone prevailed throughout. Hissing and boiling noises.
I can't decide who's right and since Doug Ford's Ontario doesn't have a functioning landlord and tenant tribunal, no one can. Readers weigh in. Renting sucks. You should buy.
Oh no. Home-ownership heartbreak manufactures the bulk of hard-luck stories. Readers point out the obvious, that your problem wasn't the bank or interest rates, it was that you couldn't afford that house/neighbourhood in the first place. You had no wiggle room.
So many problems ranging from tiny to minuscule, so many agony aunts. The Washington Post has Miss Manners ("a simple no will suffice"), Ask Amy ("Mom worried about daughter's alcoholism"), Ask Sahaj ("My immigrant parents annoy me"), Carolyn Hax ("Mom mortifies teens by asking server to replace dirty glass") and Work Advice ("My co-worker smells"), plus Readers' Questions to which reporters respond irritably that they've been answering that for seven years on the beat, look it up.
Since I won't read its Reaganite opinion page and my immersion in agony aunt-ics has made me think humans are a failed concept, there's little left to read post-Bezos layoffs. I shall cancel.
Certain horror stories obliterate all others. Gaza is one. Climate change is another. Are Wednesday's Child tales popular because we cannot countenance the worst stories of all? Very possibly. What a lucky country we are.
HEATHER MALLICK IS A TORONTO-BASED COLUMNIST COVERING
CURRENT AFFAIRS FOR THE STAR.
FOLLOW HER ON TWITTER:
@HEATHERMALLICK
CREDIT: Heather Mallick Toronto Star OPINION HEATHER MALLICK IS A TORONTO-BASED COLUMNIST COVERING CURRENT AFFAIRS FOR THE STAR. FOLLOW HER ON TWITTER: @HEATHERMALLICK;

Document 95 of 500

Chow disappointed by lack of funds for subway cars

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/chow-disappointed-lack-funds-subway-cars/docview/3039702807/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chow+disappointed+by+lack+of+funds+for+subway+cars&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-04-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Like a frustrated TTC user, Toronto is still looking down the track for $750 million in federal funds to help replace Bloor-Danforth line subway cars hurtling toward the end of their lifespan.
Mayor Olivia Chow on Tuesday applauded new funds to house refugees and other items in the federal budget, but was clearly disappointed that Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government left out her top request - the subway car money.
"We need to put the order in yesterday" for the manufacture of the vehicles that will take years to deliver, Chow told reporters at city hall, citing an urgent need to replace the cars that are all at least 25 years old and have an intended life of 30 years.
TTC chief executive Rick Leary last year called the existing Line 2 cars "very, very frail" and raised the spectre of having to "shut things down" if Toronto can't get commitments to help the city fund replacements.
Last autumn, as part of his government's financial "new deal" with Toronto, Ontario Premier Doug Ford committed $750 million toward the new subway cars - but only if the federal government matches the contribution. Chow said she failed to talk the province out of that condition.
Chow noted the budget includes $20 billion for infrastructure funding in Ontario but there is no word on how much Toronto will get or what types of projects.
The mayor was more upbeat about the Trudeau government commitment of $400 million this year, and a total of $1.1 billion over three years, to help provide emergency funding for refugee claimants arriving in Toronto, and expected to total 6,000 this year alone.
Chow also praised several housing initiatives in the federal budget she said will help Toronto spur new housing construction while helping alleviate the homelessness crisis and efforts to get people out of park encampments and into supportive housing.
Harbourfront Centre is also getting a boost with badly needed funding to help the arts hub make critical capital repairs.
Some $57 million in federal funding has flowed to Harbourfront since 2018, but the funding from Ottawa is supposed to be for operations, freeing up Harbourfront to find and use other sources of revenue.
Still, Ottawa came through again Tuesday with $10 million over two years for repairs.
Among other Toronto-specific pledges in the $61.2-billion spending plan is the opening for development of some federally owned lands.
The budget promises to free up land at Downsview for modular homes.
Another cultural organization getting a bump is the Toronto International Film Festival, which will receive $23 million over three years.
The money from Ottawa follows a decision by Bell, one of the festival's lead sponsors, to pull their support at the end of last year. Other corporate backers include the Toronto Star.
A new cultural hub is on the horizon with plans for a museum space for Sikh arts, culture and heritage that will be developed in partnership with the Royal Ontario Museum thanks to a new $11-million funding program.
Another organization getting a boost in Toronto is the Canadian Security Intelligence Service. The spy agency is getting $655.7 million over eight years for what's described as enhancing its intelligence capabilities and its "presence in Toronto."
And, the long-awaited dream of high-frequency rail service in the Toronto-Quebec City corridor also gets a boost to the tune of $371.8 million over six years.
CREDIT: Stephanie Levitz Deputy Toronto Star David Rider Toronto Star Chief

Document 96 of 500

$61.2B Projected new spending over five years $21.9B Projected new revenues over five years $39.8B Projected deficit for 2024-25: A bucket-list budget from a party that knows the clock is ticking

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/61-2b-projected-new-spending-over-five-years-21/docview/3039702783/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%2461.2B+Projected+new+spending+over+five+years+%2421.9B+Projected+new+revenues+over+five+years+%2439.8B+Projected+deficit+for+2024-25%3A+A+bucket-list+budget+from+a+party+that+knows+the+clock+is+ticking&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-04-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One big thing has changed between the 2024 budget and the one Justin Trudeau's government delivered last year - and it all revolves around an air of inevitability.
No matter who wins the next election, there is a sense that this current government is winding down to some kind of conclusion. It is impossible to view this eighth budget from Trudeau's government through any other lens. Granted, this may not be the last budget before a 2025 election, but it begs the question: what, if anything, will last?
Ask any Liberal around the budget launch on Tuesday, and they would say the answer to that question is none of it - if Pierre Poilievre and his Conservatives win the next election. "He could throw all of this away," one official said flatly.
By "all of this," this adviser was referring to all the "fairness" that this budget tried so hard to stress, not at all subtly. ("Fairness for Every Generation" is this year's budget title and some version of the word "fair" winds hundreds of times through its 400-plus pages.)
Leave aside the fairness for now, though. The best suggested title for this budget comes from Jennifer Robson, head of Carleton University's school of political management, who called it the "bucket list" budget.
Robson was thinking in particular of Barack Obama's final speech to the White House Correspondents Dinner in 2015, when he saw the end of his second term as president looming.
"After the midterm elections, my advisers asked me, 'Mr. President, do you have a bucket list?' And I said, 'Well, I have something that rhymes with bucket list.'"
Obama went on. "Take executive action on immigration. Bucket. New climate regulations. Bucket, it's the right thing to do."
Conservatives are not likely to mind that bucket nickname. First, bucket lists are what you make before your time is up, which they definitely believe is imminent for Trudeau and company. Also, Poilievre will be all too happy to talk about the buckets of spending in the budget - $39.3 billion of it. Poilievre's first social media post after the budget, predictably, hauled out the now-familiar slogan: "Not worth the cost."
However, maybe also predictably, Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland's budget speech was pointedly addressed to the Conservatives and those who might be tempted to choose Poilievre to succeed Trudeau.
The most partisan points in Freeland's speech were essentially a bucket list of her own - or, more widely, of her government. Six questions, framed around what kind of country Canadians want.
"Do you want to live in a country where you can tell the size of someone's paycheque by their smile?" (That's the dental-care promise, part of the Liberals' ongoing deal with the New Democrats. And, incidentally, nothing in this budget appears to imperil that deal, probably because there was a lot of back-and-forth between the parties in advance of it, I'm told.)
Freeland's other bucket-list questions revolved a lot around housing, no surprise, and all the things Canadians get from activist government.
She didn't say it outright, but one of the questions was: Do you want to live in a country like the United States, in the shadow of Donald Trump? Freeland noted a couple of times that birth control is covered under the emerging pharmacare program - $1.5 billion set aside over five years to support its launch, which also included coverage of diabetes medication.
"Women in other countries - our friends, our neighbours - are losing their right to control their own bodies. We will not let that happen here." No subtlety was intended there either, as the Liberals hold their breath and see whether the contest between Trump and Joe Biden later this year will ripple here in Canada in a way that puts Liberals in a more favourable light.
There is a way to view this latest, but possibly not last, budget from Trudeau's government as an exercise in what you could call Poilievre-proofing - battening down the hatches of progressive government in ways that Conservatives would find difficult to unravel if they win the next election.
That doesn't apply to the $42 million next year for the CBC, obviously, which Poilievre is vowing to defund, but an argument can be made that Canadians are too attached now to the Canada child benefit and national child-care system, for instance, to be easily reversed.
Back in 2015, when Obama was talking at the White House Correspondents Dinner and Trudeau was months away from winning power for the first time, the former president's "bucket" speech was seen as evidence of a man and government growing more feisty and combative as the next election loomed.
The same has been seen of Trudeau in recent weeks, ready to pick some fights with Conservatives or the provinces - and there is plenty in this budget showing a government ready to mix it up with the premiers too, on housing or climate change.
The F-word in this budget, we're told, is "fair." But what else rhymes with "bucket?"
CREDIT: susan delacourt OPINION

Subject: Speeches; Budgets; Elections

Document 97 of 500

Liberal budget hits a home run on housing, but plays small ball on care economy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/liberal-budget-hits-home-run-on-housing-plays/docview/3040054227/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberal+budget+hits+a+home+run+on+housing%2C+but+plays+small+ball+on+care+economy&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Chrystia Freeland's Budget 2024 lets the federal Liberals play two styles of baseball.
It hits what could arguably be called a home run with its Housing Plan unveiled last week. Now it needs to ready the team for playing small ball in the care economy. That's the only way it can tackle the key concerns of Canadian voters and close the popularity gap that the Conservative party has opened.
The Liberals' Housing Plan is the most comprehensive set of policies we've seen since the end of the Second World War, narrated clearly and effectively by Housing Minister Sean Fraser. It finally addresses the rapidly expanding population of renters, more than doubling previous commitments. A finance department official confirmed Budget 2024 adds $19.5 billion over the next five years for housing, including the creation of 30,000 new purpose-built apartments. Half that money is in the form of loans, so taxpayers will get much of it back.
While the pace of builds is accelerating, so has the pace of renovictions and short-term rentals since the Bank of Canada started hiking interest rates in 2022. In order to preserve the affordable housing stock we still have, the budget also allocates $1 billion in loans, and not quite $500M in grants to non-profits.
But in the decade from 2011 and 2021, Canada was losing 15 affordable rental units for every new unit built. We can't build fast enough. Your grandma was right: an ounce of protection is worth a pound of cure.
Real estate investment trusts (REITs) and pools of private equity are buying up housing and churning out bigger than average returns since supply is so scarce.Budget 2024 says "When purchasing a home, Canadians might expect to be bidding against other potential buyers, not a multibillion-dollar hedge fund. ... Budget 2024 announces that the government intends to restrict the purchase and acquisition of existing single-family homes by very large, corporate investors." Expect consultations soon, and details in the 2024 Fall Economic Statement.
Preventing erosion of capacity and siphoning of wealth is a big concern in the care economy too, which is also in the crosshairs of private equity. In provinces like Ontario and around the world, private equity is scooping up small profitable providers, squeezing profits from public funding by lowering staffing levels and lobbying for less qualified staff to lower labour costs. These owners also charge operators more for rent and supplies, then lobby for higher public subsidies and fees. This can and must be stopped.
In politics, not everything can be a home run. All governments will be hard-pressed to avoid the accelerating dysfunction in health care, long-term care, and child-care. Beyond more funding, the federal government can adopt small ball strategies to methodically advance a humane agenda, moving players around the bases slowly and strategically. Here are three ways federal small ball could deliver big results without big spends in the coming months:
Child care Workforce Deals: Round two of the Canada-wide Early Learning and Childcare Agreements are now being negotiated, with a focus on workforce attraction and retention. Funds for these deals are already baked into the budget. The new agreements need enforceable requirements for staffing levels, qualifications of staff, wage grids commensurate with training, and pensions. Such rules could pay big dividends for frustrated parents who can see existing child care spaces not available because of staff shortages and massive turnover.
Private Equity: Build on the Housing Plan statement that "the role of private equity in our housing market needs to be addressed." The federal government should be tracking trends in the investments occurring in our long-term care, child care and health-care sectors, following the U.S., U.K., Australia, Europe and the Nordic countries, who are all examining ways of putting new guardrails on public funding.
Community Benefits Agreements: Care services such as child care, long-term care, medical or dental community clinics can be a built-in feature of new housing and infrastructure developments. Vancouver has led the way in such initiatives, increasing access to care while shielding non-profit providers from future rent hikes within for-profit buildings. Every federal dollar should do double duty like this wherever possible.
It's obvious why housing took centre stage in this budget. Freeland only had the opportunity for one home run, given her adversaries' weaponization of any new spending the Liberals undertake. But housing isn't all people need, and the care economy has galloped up the list of Canadians' concerns.
Child care, long-term care and health care has been crumbling all across Canada in the past year, a function of rising demand and shortage of supply, whether spaces, beds, or staff. Just last week another person asked for, and received, Medical Assistance in Dying in Quebec because the health system totally failed to meet their care needs. These needs can't wait. The provinces absolutely need to step up, but the feds can do more.
So, yes, it's disappointing there was no big answer to the challenges in the care economy in this federal budget; but it's understandable, especially after committing $200 billion over 10 years for health care last year. If the Liberals now tighten their focus on the workforce, private equity, and community benefits, every federal dollar could deliver more for the care economy, with life-altering changes along the way.
This is the hard, painstaking work of government. Small ball may not be a glamorous way to play the game, but so long as the runners keep moving around the bases on care economy reform, at least we'll keep scoring wins.
CAPTION: The Liberals' Housing Plan, writes Armine Yalnizyan, is the most comprehensive set of housing policies we've seen since the end of the Second World War.

Subject: World War II; Workforce planning; Long term health care; Child care; Private equity; Rents; Rentals; Affordable housing; Funding; Profits; Workforce

Document 98 of 500

Justin Trudeau has a budget bucket list - and it's aimed at Pierre Poilievre

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-has-budget-bucket-list-aimed-at/docview/3040053961/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+has+a+budget+bucket+list+-+and+it%27s+aimed+at+Pierre+Poilievre&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One big thing has changed between the 2024 budget and the one Justin Trudeau's government delivered last year - and it all revolves around an air of inevitability.
No matter who one wants to win the next election, there is a sense that this current government is winding down to some kind of conclusion. It is impossible to view this eighth budget from Trudeau's government through any other lens. Granted, this may not be the last budget before a 2025 election, but it begs the question: what, if anything, will last?
Ask any Liberal around the budget launch on Tuesday, and they would say the answer to that question is: none of it - if Pierre Poilievre and his Conservatives win the next election. "He could throw all of this away," one official said, flatly.
By "all of this," this adviser was referring to all the "fairness" that this budget tried so hard to stress, not at all subtly. ("Fairness for Every Generation" is this year's budget title and some version of the word "fair" winds hundreds of times through its 400-plus pages.)
Leave aside the fairness, for now, though. The best suggested title for this budget comes from Jennifer Robson, head of Carleton University's school of political management, who called it the "bucket list" budget.
Robson was thinking in particular of Barack Obama's final speech to the White House correspondents' dinner in 2015, when he saw the end of his second term as president looming.
"After the midterm elections, my advisers asked me, 'Mr. President, do you have a bucket list?' And I said, 'Well, I have something that rhymes with bucket list.'"
Obama went on. "Take executive action on immigration. Bucket. New climate regulations. Bucket, it's the right thing to do."
Conservatives are not likely to mind that bucket nickname. First, bucket lists are what you make before your time is up, which they definitely believe is imminent for Trudeau and company. Also, Poilievre will be all too happy to talk about the buckets of spending in the budget - $39.3 billion of it. Poilievre's first social media post after the budget, predictably, hauled out the now-familiar slogan: "not worth the cost."
However, maybe also predictably, Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland's budget speech was pointedly addressed to the Conservatives and those who might be tempted to choose Poilievre to succeed Trudeau.
The most partisan points in Freeland's speech were essentially a bucket list of her own - or, more widely, of her government. Six questions, framed around what kind of country Canadians want.
"Do you want to live in a country where you can tell the size of someone's paycheque by their smile?" (That's the dental-care promise, part of the Liberals' ongoing deal with the New Democrats. And, incidentally, nothing in this budget appears to imperil that deal, probably because there was a lot of back and forth between the parties in advance of it, I'm told.)
Freeland's other bucket-list questions revolved a lot around housing, no surprise, and all the things Canadians get from activist government.
She didn't say it outright, but one of the questions was: do you want to live in a country like the United States, in the shadow of Donald Trump? Freeland noted a couple of times that birth control is covered under the emerging pharmacare program - $1.5 billion set aside over five years to support its launch, which also included coverage of diabetes medication.
"Women in other countries - our friends, our neighbours - are losing their right to control their own bodies. We will not let that happen here." No subtlety was intended there either, as the Liberals hold their breaths and see whether the contest between Trump and Joe Biden later this year will ripple here in Canada in a way that puts Liberals in a more favourable light.
There is way to view this latest, but possibly not last, budget from Trudeau's government as an exercise in what you could call Poilievre-proofing - battening down the hatches of progressive government in ways that Conservatives would find difficult to unravel if they win the next election.
That doesn't apply to the $42-million next year for the CBC, obviously, which Poilievre is vowing to defund, but an argument can be made that Canadians are too attached now to the Canada child benefit and national child care, for instance, to be easily reversed.
Back in 2015, when Obama was talking to the White House correspondents' dinner and Trudeau was months away from winning power for the first time, the former president's "bucket" speech was seen as evidence of a man and government growing more feisty and combative as the next election loomed.
The same has been seen of Trudeau in recent weeks, ready to pick some fights with Conservatives or the provinces - and there is plenty in this budget showing a government ready to mix it up with the premiers too, on housing or climate change.
The f-word in this budget, we're told, is "fair." But what else rhymes with "bucket?"
CAPTION: Deputy Prime Minister and Minister of Finance Chrystia Freeland arrives at a cabinet meeting on Parliament Hill in Ottawa on April 16, 2024.
