Document 183 of 500

Reflections on the eclipse, nicknames in politics, remembering genocide in Rwanda

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/reflections-on-eclipse-nicknames-politics/docview/3034751138/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Reflections+on+the+eclipse%2C+nicknames+in+politics%2C+remembering+genocide+in+Rwanda&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Eclipse was an opportunity
While of course the upcoming solar eclipse was an exciting event, it should serve as a reminder of how truly insignificant we are in the cosmic universe. The solar eclipse provided a powerful metaphor. Just as the moon's passage briefly obscured the sun's light, we often allow our focus on day-to-day matters to overshadow the larger human causes and conditions that should concern us all, in particular the extreme man-made humanitarian crises in Gaza. Now, despite being insignificant in the cosmos, every human being on this planet can and should make a difference when it comes to ensuring justice and peace. We should all use this occasion to reflect on our role in society and how we can create positive change in the world, making room for love for all, and hatred for none.
Saadiyya Ahmed, Vaughan
Nicknames make for childish politics
Is Doug Ford's put-down of Bonnie Crombie a smear, or just politics as usual? April 8
This is a big indication that Premier Doug Ford is worried about Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie beating him in the upcoming election. Like many Ontarians, I'm hoping he's kicked out when she wins. As for the title that he has labelled Crombie with, I feel that he deserves one for what he's done to Ontario.
Chris Andrews, Vaughan
Premier Doug Ford has lowered himself to the same level of former U.S. president Donald Trump by resorting to using nicknames for people who disagree with his position. I now realize why I voted against Ford in the last election.
Wilf McQuinn, Huntsville, Ont.
So the premier has dubbed Bonnie Crombie the "queen of the carbon tax." That refers to the levy, not a tax, that rebates more to 80 per cent of Ontarians than they pay. We pay the levy because the Ford government had no environmental policy to promote conservation and reduce emissions. Since he doesn't seem to understand anything about the policy he criticizes, would it be so incorrect to dub him the "dunce of Queen's Park?"
Terry Kushnier, Scarborough
We should have learned from Rwanda
French President Macron says France and its allies 'could have stopped' the 1994 Rwanda genocide, April 4
Emmanuel Macron notes that, "the international community had the means to know and to take actions" to stop the Rwandan genocide thirty years ago and yet the world did nothing. Here we are doing it again in Gaza. Even though the International Court of Justice determined that a "plausible genocide" is taking place in Gaza, our own Canadian government has yet to call for a permanent ceasefire there, sold arms to Israel and has not denounced genocide. More than 33,000 have been killed, countless face starvation and the world sits watching. It seems that we have learned nothing from history.
Joanne Clarke, Toronto
World Central Kitchen does good work
Airstrikes on aid workers don't 'just happen,' Trudeau says after Netanyahu comments, April 4
At the outset of the war in Ukraine, I witnessed first hand as a volunteer the work of the WCK. At a giant warehouse in Poland, thousands of Ukrainian refugees were arriving daily, exhausted, hungry and traumatized by the invasion and their long journey to get themselves safely across the border. There to greet the refugees was a large kitchen, open 24/7, with WCK volunteers always ready to serve hot drinks, a hearty stew, fresh buns, oranges and treats for the children. I got to know some of the volunteer cooks and servers, and they had come on their own dime from around the world. I told them more than once, they are amongst a special group of people doing God's work. I certainly believe this to be true about our lost Canadian, Jacob Flickinger.
Tony Dittenhoffer, Collingwood, Ont.
Law abiding citizens are punished
With everything that has been happening lately I strongly feel that us law abiding, rule-following citizens are continuously getting punished for doing the right thing. Our vehicles get stolen after taking every suggested precaution only to have our insurance go up. Too many people are taking advantage of stealing food through self checkouts only to have stores raise prices for the rest of us. Young adults follow the expected path and go to university, come out with lots of enthusiasm and huge debts, only to find decent paying jobs/careers will barely allow them to become homeowners. Now the city has to impose a home vacancy tax because of "investors" using housing for personal financial growth and disregarding the housing crisis. Of course, all the honest homeowners, who did their part, got shocked with a huge bill, had to waste their precious time in long lineups and fight to clear themselves of the city's wrong doings. When did our society become so reversed? I am so tired of good citizens getting consequences for other people's misbehaviour.
Melanie Kohek, Whitby, Ont.
Priced out from snacks at Rogers Centre
It is truly scrumdiddlyumptious thinking about the vegan tacos with chips, stuffed Jamaican beef patties, boozy slushies and cannolis at Rogers Centre. Although prices have not been finalized, I suspect these dishes will be far beyond what is usually shelled out at popular fast-food outlets. Sadly, taking children to watch millionaire baseball players is now beyond the affordability of many families. Praise the deities that loonie dog nights are back, at least. But there is a bright side for transients and the unhoused. Panhandling is back in vogue, staking out a spot on the sidewalk outside the Rogers Centre may prove rewarding. Go Jays!
Garry Burke, Oro-Medonte, Ont.
No sympathy for grocers
Police say retail crime rings are real - and worse than you think, April 6
My relatively new job as unpaid check out person and bagger at the grocery store leaves me little time to empathize with a corporation which did away with thousands of cashiers to provide raises for the suits in corporate headquarters and dividends for shareholders.
Ab Dukacz, Mississauga
Too many acronyms
Return-to-office strategies ignite hidden boardroom battles, April 6
After reading columnist Gleb Tsipursky's article about whether the biggest issue in corporate boardrooms is AI, DEI, ESG, RTO or WFH, I think I have uncovered the answer. It's TMFA (Too Many Friggin' Acronyms).
Gregory Hoggarth, Stratford, Ont.
CAPTION: The solar eclipse is seen through the cloud cover in Fort Erie, Ont.

Subject: Solar eclipses; Genocide

Location: Gaza Strip; Rwanda

Document 184 of 500

LCBO forced to bring back paper bags: Premier orders reintroduction, saying move will help families 'struggling to make ends meet'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lcbo-forced-bring-back-paper-bags/docview/3034628592/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO+forced+to+bring+back+paper+bags%3A+Premier+orders+reintroduction%2C+saying+move+will+help+families+%27struggling+to+make+ends+meet%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-04-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The LCBO will resume giving paper bags to customers after the ban was sacked by Premier Doug Ford.
In an unusual intervention with the operations of the Crown-owned Liquor Control Board of Ontario, Ford ordered the booze giant to rethink its bag policy that took effect last September.
"I'm requesting that you take immediate steps to reverse the decision to remove paper bags from the LCBO's retail locations," the premier wrote Sunday in a terse letter to George Soleas, president and CEO of the company.
"At a time when many Ontario families are already struggling to make ends meet, every additional expense counts. That includes charging customers for reusable bags instead of the free paper bags that the LCBO previously offered," Ford wrote.
"This change has left people stuck openly carrying alcohol in public when leaving a LCBO store," added the premier, a teetotaller.
The LCBO, which first announced the phasing out of the paper bags last spring, confirmed Monday it "has received direction from the provincial government to take steps to reintroduce single-use paper bags" at its more than 680 outlets.
"While we are not able to confirm an availability date at this time, we will share more details with our valued customers in the coming weeks," the company said.
Last April 27, Soleas said the scheme would remove about 135 million paper bags from circulation, diverting 2,665 tonnes of waste from landfill.
The company maintained at the time that it would save the equivalent of more than 188,000 trees annually.
It followed the decision to stop providing customers plastic bags in 2008.
But Ford said Sunday that "the environmental merits of this decision are questionable at best."
"Paper bags are an easily recyclable alternative to single-use plastic, which is why the LCBO adopted them in the first place," he reminded Soleas.
"As a government, we are focused on making life easier, more convenient and more affordable for the people of Ontario. The decision to remove paper bags has had the opposite effect."
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy, whose department oversees the LCBO, defended the premier's injecting himself into the operational processes of the agency.
"This government is obsessed with making life more affordable for the citizens of Ontario," Bethlenfalvy told reporters at Queen's Park.
Noting "there's 85 billion trees in Ontario and we're continuing to support that and our forest products industry," he played down environmental concerns.
But Green Leader Mike Schreiner emphasized "we're in a climate emergency - every tree makes a huge difference."
"We can help people save money and the LCBO save money by bringing in a reusable bag. It's not that hard," said Schreiner. "I have a really nice reusable LCBO bag that I use all the time, saving myself money, saving the LCBO money."
CAPTION: The LCBO first announced it was phasing out its paper bags last spring. The Crown-owned retailer confirmed Monday it "has received direction from the provincial government" to go back to giving out the bags at its more than 680 outlets.Richard LautensToronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 186 of 500

Will premiers back Ottawa's housing plan?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/will-premiers-back-ottawas-housing-plan/docview/3034627897/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+premiers+back+Ottawa%27s+housing+plan%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-04-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Throw stuff at the wall and see what sticks. This appears to be Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's communications strategy in the lead up to the federal government's budget rollout next week. In fact, nowhere is this mad dash, campaign-style, policy push more pronounced than on the housing file: a subject that produces fresh headlines almost daily.
In recent weeks the federal government has launched a $6-billion infrastructure fund to speed up housing construction; it has promised a renters' "bill of rights," that would establish a national standard lease agreement; it has announced $15 million in funding for provincial legal aid for tenants; it has announced $600 million in loans, in part to fast track the construction of prefabricated homes; it has announced a $15-billion top-up to its Apartment Construction Loan Program.
For it is plainly obvious that these announcements have not come out of the blue. The prime minister is trying to court young voters who are struggling amid a national housing crisis and lending their support to federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre as a result (the Conservative leader hasn't been shy about his plans to "build homes fast").
Yet despite the rather obvious political calculation inherent in the announcements, many of Trudeau's policy planks - particularly where renters are concerned - are positive and welcome news. Additional funding for legal aid for renters - an often overlooked, demographic - is helpful and necessary. So is the fact that the federal government clearly views housing as a national priority. After all, Canada's housing woes intensified when the federal government exited the affordable housing business.
It is a great thing it wants back in. The problem though is not the prime minister's motivation, but his methods, pumping out piecemeal policy announcements in quick succession to maximize publicity ahead of the release of the federal budget.
Indeed, the stream of announcements perfectly summarizes much of what is wrong with Canada's approach to the housing crisis. Fragmented policy will not get us out of this crisis. The only way forward is through holistic collaboration and problem solving with provincial and municipal governments at every step.
And the fact remains that while many of the federal government's policies could benefit Canadians, they require buy-in from other levels of government that are not always eager to collaborate. A prime example of this conflict is playing out right now in Ontario where Premier Doug Ford has declared his opposition to "forcing" municipalities in the province to allow fourplexes, residential buildings with four units inside. The federal government's infrastructure fund announcement comes with conditions for provinces and territories, one of them being fourplex legalization across municipalities.
Ford, however, is adamant that he won't play along; he told reporters that communities in Ontario would "lose their minds" if fourplexes were allowed as of right across the province. In other words, though we may perceive the issue as hyperlocal, the sheer possibility that voters will cry "Not in my backyard!" in the face of new development has the power to impact policy at a provincial and national level.
Trudeau insists he won't let provincial intransigence get in the way. "If a province decides it doesn't want to be ambitious on housing, that's their decision. We will work with the municipalities within that province that are ambitious."
But it isn't just Ford who is objecting to what he perceives to be federal government overreach. According to reporting by the Star's Tonda MacCharles, the prime minister's recent announcements on several files, from health care to housing, has angered some of Canada's premiers; one provincial source described the situation as "a risk for national unity."
However, at the end of the day the risk is not to national unity but to a Canadian public that is at the mercy of leaders who refuse to co-operate with one another.
The housing crisis in Canada is often described as intractable and complex. And it is. But there is one truth about the crisis that is straight forward: it will take all three levels of government working together in good faith to make a difference. Until that happens Canada's housing crisis will rage on unabated, no matter how many news conferences the prime minister calls.
CREDIT: ;

Subject: Federal government; Collaboration; Voters; Prime ministers; Legal aid

Location: Canada

Title: Will premiers back Ottawa's housing plan?

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: A.10

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Apr 9, 2024

Section: Editorial

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 03190781

Source type: Newspaper

Document 187 of 500

Ford fires back after criticism of staff payroll

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-fires-back-after-criticism-staff-payroll/docview/3034627802/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+fires+back+after+criticism+of+staff+payroll&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-04-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford is firing back at critics after the number of premier's office staff making at least $100,000 a year has more than doubled to 48 people.
"The gravy train has pulled into the premier's office," Liberal MPP John Fraser (Ottawa South) charged Monday as the legislature resumed sitting for the first time since the annual "Sunshine List" of $100,000 earners was released.
"At a time when Ontario families are struggling, how does this premier justify increasing the staff?"
Ford, elected in 2018 on a mantra of "respect for taxpayers," bristled at the question in a testy exchange with Fraser.
"The nerve of the member, to say about saving taxpayers' money," the premier shot back.
"Your leader, Bonnie Crombie, called the tax break that we gave on gas a gimmick," Ford added.
Crombie said last week that Ford is jeopardizing jobs of the future in Ontario with "reckless" underfunding of colleges and universities as several run deficits.
She questioned whether the gas tax cut, which costs the provincial treasury $1.2 billion a year as the government notched a $9.8-billion deficit, could be better spent on improving health care and education with 2.3 million Ontarians unable to find a family doctor.
Ford did not explain why the number of premier's office staff has grown so dramatically under his Progressive Conservatives. In 2019, his first full year in government, there were 20 staff in the premier's office earning at least $100,000 - two more than former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne. But by last year, there were 48 staff on the list, for a total of $6.9 million in annual salaries, up from $2.9 million in 2019.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Title: Ford fires back after criticism of staff payroll

Doug Ford bristles at questions about $100K salaries in his office

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-bristles-at-questions-about-100k/docview/3034823815/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+bristles+at+questions+about+%24100K+salaries+in+his+office&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford is firing back at critics after the number of premier's office staff making at least $100,000 a year has more than doubled to 48 people.
"The gravy train has pulled into the premier's office," Liberal MPP John Fraser (Ottawa South) charged Monday as the legislature resumed sitting for the first time since the annual "Sunshine List" of $100,000 earners was released.
"At a time when Ontario families are struggling, how does this premier justify increasing the staff?"
Ford, elected in 2018 on a mantra of "respect for taxpayers," bristled at the question in a testy exchange with Fraser.
"The nerve of the member, to say about saving taxpayers' money," the premier shot back.
"Your leader, Bonnie Crombie, called the tax break that we gave on gas a gimmick," Ford added in a bid to deflect attention from the issue.
Crombie said last week that Ford is jeopardizing jobs of the future in Ontario with "reckless" underfunding of colleges and universities as several run deficits.
She questioned whether the gas tax cut, which costs the provincial treasury $1.2 billion a year as the government notched a $9.8-billion deficit, could be better spent on improving health care and education with 2.3 million Ontarians unable to find a family doctor.
Ford did not explain why the number of premier's office staff has grown so dramatically under his Progressive Conservatives.
In 2019, his first full year in government, there were 20 staff in the premier's office earning at least $100,000 - two more than former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne.
But by last year, there were 48 staff on the list, for a total of $6.9 million in annual salaries, up from $2.9 million in 2019.
Fraser noted later that Ford did not try to defend or explain the increase at a time when Ontario's annual deficit has soared to $9.8 billion.
"He couldn't say it, because he knows it's wrong."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks at the Ottawa Board of Trade on March 28, 2024.

Document 189 of 500

Doug Ford forces LCBO to reinstate paper bags for customers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-forces-lcbo-reinstate-paper-bags/docview/3034468466/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+forces+LCBO+to+reinstate+paper+bags+for+customers&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The LCBO will resume giving paper bags to customers after the ban was sacked by Premier Doug Ford.
In an unusual intervention with the operations of the Crown-owned Liquor Control Board of Ontario, Ford ordered the booze giant to rethink its bag policy that took effect last September.
"I'm requesting that you take immediate steps to reverse the decision to remove paper bags from the LCBO's retail locations," the premier wrote Sunday in a terse letter to George Soleas, president and CEO of the company.
"At a time when many Ontario families are already struggling to make ends meet, every additional expense counts. That includes charging customers for reusable bags instead of the free paper bags that the LCBO previously offered," wrote Ford.
"This change has left people stuck openly carrying alcohol in public when leaving a LCBO store," added the premier, a teetotaler.
The LCBO, which first announced the phasing out of the paper bags last spring, confirmed Monday it "has received direction from the provincial government to take steps to reintroduce single-use paper bags" at its more than 680 outlets.
"While we are not able to confirm an availability date at this time, we will share more details with our valued customers in the coming weeks," the company said.
Last April 27, Soleas said the scheme would remove about 135 million paper bags from circulation, diverting 2,665 tonnes of waste from landfill.
The company maintained at the time that it would save the equivalent of more than 188,000 trees annually.
It followed the decision to stop providing customers plastic bags in 2008.
But Ford said Sunday that "the environmental merits of this decision are questionable at best."
"Paper bags are an easily recyclable alternative to single-use plastic, which is why the LCBO adopted them in the first place," he reminded Soleas.
"As a government, we are focused on making life easier, more convenient, and more affordable for the people of Ontario. The decision to remove paper bags has had the opposite effect."
CAPTION: File photo: Premier Doug Ford.

Document 190 of 500

Old elevators are foiling a plan to renovate a historic Toronto building - and leaving 6,000 people in limbo

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/old-elevators-are-foiling-plan-renovate-historic/docview/3034468438/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Old+elevators+are+foiling+a+plan+to+renovate+a+historic+Toronto+building+-+and+leaving+6%2C000+people+in+limbo&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Elevator problems are giving civil servants the shaft.
Right around now, an army of bureaucrats was to be marching back into a huge Ontario government office complex across from the legislature following a massive $1.5-billion renovation begun in 2018.
But major problems in getting elevator shafts up to modern building codes in the 53-year-old Macdonald Block have foiled plans to repatriate 6,000 government and political staff from leased offices elsewhere.
"This has been a very difficult time to build," said Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma.
She cites the age of the buildings, COVID-19, "unforeseen challenges" that led to escalating costs and post-pandemic inflation as factors in pushing the original price tag higher.
Just how much, the minister won't say as the government agency Infrastructure Ontario continues "active negotiations" with construction contractor PCL on the project, which is mostly complete except for the installation of updated security systems.
"There are cost overruns," she confirmed. "Fixing the issues that we faced is of great importance because these are taxpayer dollars."
Asked if there is an estimated completion date, Surma, who last fall hoped to have one by Christmas after discussions with PCL, replied "not at this point in time."
The talks, underway for months, will "come up with a new plan and a new timeline and schedule on how to address the challenges we face," she added.
"Through further inspections and testing is when we realized that the elevators were not to code and so we've been working very closely with them."
PCL referred a request for details on the technical challenges in completing the job to Infrastructure Ontario. PCL has also been working on major renovations at the Rogers Centre to be unveiled Monday at the Blue Jays home opener.
"During the project it was determined that significant reconstruction is required," Infrastructure Ontario media and communications manager Ian McConachie said in a statement to the Star.
"The scope of outstanding work relates to steel beam being treated with additional fireproofing material to ensure the elevator shaft enclosures are in compliance with building code requirements," he added.
With 38 elevators in the Macdonald Block, there are 439 elevator entrance assemblies that underwent fire standard testing on the original entrances and subsequent modifications.
"Both the original and modified original entrances do not meet the current requirements, and we need a full replacement of all 439 elevator entrances," said McConachie.
Surma recently donned a hard hat and took a look in-person on the Macdonald Block construction site, where standard renovation tasks like drywalling are finished and landscaping is underway behind the fences and hoarding walling it off from the street.
"I wanted to see first-hand why it's so difficult to fix these elevators. They're in the interiors of the building, they're very much part of the structural integrity of the building," she said.
"There's 38 of them and we need to make sure that they're built to code, that they're safe and functioning and operational for the workers when they get back."
The indefinite delay comes as Premier Doug Ford's government works toward a top-to-bottom renovation of the 131-year-old main legislative building estimated to cost at least $1 billion and take years.
Legislative Affairs Minister Paul Calandra acknowledged last month that the goal of beginning the work after the 2026 provincial election is "unlikely to happen."
Calandra and officials have been looking for 500,000 square feet of space near the legislative precinct as a temporary home for MPPs, political aides, legislative staff and journalists during the renovation.
The legislative precinct includes the Macdonald Block, putting civil servants within quick walking distance of their political masters instead of being located in several other locations downtown, as is the case during the construction work.
Depending on progress with the elevators, Surma said it may be possible to move staff back into one or two of the Macdonald Block buildings first instead of waiting for the entire complex to be completed.
"Those are all details of the plan and the schedule that's being negotiated now," Surma said.
The renovation to modernize the complex - bounded by Queen's Park Circle to the west, Wellesley Street to the north, Bay Street to the east and Grosvenor Street to the south - was intended to cut costs by fitting more people in and upgrading heating and cooling systems.
About 4,000 staff worked in the buildings before the renovation, but there will be room for 6,000 once the work is complete.
"The government will save $26 million annually," Surma said.
But until everything is sorted out, leases in other downtown buildings housing various ministry offices and staff may have to be extended.
CAPTION: Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma speaks in the Ontario Legislature on Oct. 19, 2023.

Subject: Infrastructure; Elevators & escalators; Building codes; Civil service; Buildings; Politics; Courthouses

Document 191 of 500

Is Doug Ford's put-down of Bonnie Crombie a smear, or just politics as usual?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-doug-fords-put-down-bonnie-crombie-smear-just/docview/3034468288/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Doug+Ford%27s+put-down+of+Bonnie+Crombie+a+smear%2C+or+just+politics+as+usual%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford has dubbed Bonnie Crombie the "queen of the carbon tax."
Does that make him king of the put-downs?
At Queen's Park and during news conferences, the snub is repeated - some 45 times in the legislature this year alone - by a dozen ministers and MPPs, and the premier himself numerous times when he's in front of the microphone making government announcements.
It's catchy, alliterative and conveys a simple message to his supporters and a wider audience of voters - Crombie supports higher taxes, and she's out of touch with Ontarians on a hot-button issue that has divided the country, experts say.
But when the Liberal leader has already ruled out introducing a carbon levy in Ontario, some wonder why Ford continues to use that line of attack, especially as it includes a gendered term.
"I think he thinks it's derogatory because he's trying to position himself in front of the anti-carbon-tax parade," said Peggy Nash, a former New Democrat MP who is now at Toronto Metropolitan University (TMU).
"I think he thinks that associating her with the carbon tax is the pejorative part of it" and not the term "queen," added Nash, who has authored a book on women running for office.
"Whether you call somebody a queen or king, that implies they're a leader. Now the irony is, didn't Bonnie Crombie say she wasn't going to be applying the carbon tax? It is a bit of an ironic tag for someone who clearly is not the queen."
Myer Siemiatycki, a retired TMU professor and long-time observer of politics, said "that kind of language is problematic and objectionable on a number of grounds ... (It's) a demeaning and mocking characterization of a position that a female politician has taken."
For Ford, the term is fair game - he said his Liberal rival did not speak out against Ottawa's carbon levy and "as a federal Liberal, Bonnie Crombie was one of the earliest supporters of a carbon tax. She supported a carbon tax before there was a carbon tax, making her the queen of the carbon tax."
Crombie was elected as a Liberal member of Parliament in 2008 and defeated in the 2011 election - four years before the Trudeau government, which introduced the federal carbon levy, came to power.
In a statement to the Star, Ford's director of communications, Caitlin Clark, said, "back then, Bonnie championed a carbon tax that would have imposed even higher costs on Ontario families, and she's never stood up against the federal carbon tax and remained silent about this week's 23 per cent increase," referring to the jump in price of carbon to $80 a tonne from $65 on April 1.
In addition, Clark added, "just days ago, she said she's considering re-implementing the cap-and-trade carbon tax and scrapping the gas tax cut that provides relief at the pumps and puts hundreds of dollars back into peoples' pockets."
For her part, Crombie said that if "I earned that crown," then the moniker would fit - but it doesn't.
"I've been in politics a long time. I have a thick skin and I can take political punches," she said in an interview. "I'm willing to stand up for myself and other women. But that comment really crosses the line, simply because they are perpetuating an untrue statement."
The "only reason we have a carbon tax in Ontario is because of Doug Ford, and that he ripped up the made-in-Ontario climate plan that we did have through the previous (Liberal) government - that's the only reason we have a carbon tax today is because they cancelled cap and trade," Crombie said. "So it's a false statement."
Crombie has created a climate action panel, headed by Beaches-East York MPP Mary Margaret McMahon, to help craft the party's 2026 election platform.
A return to a cap-and-trade system could be a part of it, she added, but all options are on the table.
Part of the appeal of repeating "queen of the carbon tax" is for the Ontario PC Party to define Crombie as a leader, said Peter Graefe, a political science professor at McMaster University.
Even though the Liberals were reduced to so few seats in the past two elections that they didn't even quality for official party status, Ford clearly sees her as more of a rival than NDP Leader Marit Stiles, said Graefe.
"As soon as Bonnie Crombie was elected, the Conservatives wanted to not give her any space to really establish herself and paint her, early on, as someone who cannot be trusted by the suburban electorate ... and I think very early on, they came up with this idea that they would try and pin her as a person who would support carbon taxes," said Graefe.
"Calling her the 'queen of the carbon taxes' ... it's something that's easy to repeat, pretty short form and on the face of it is not too derogatory, but certainly has the impact of putting the label on her, but also portraying her as a bit of a cartoony character."
Simon Kiss, a digital media and journalism professor at Wilfrid Laurier University, said "queen of the carbon tax" may play well now with some voters, but when inflation is tamed, concerns about the levy may go away.
"In which case, is it going to be that effective to label somebody 'queen of the carbon tax'?"
Ford and the Progressive Conservative party "very clearly see her as the alternative - they don't give a damn about Marit Stiles and the NDP. They've made a strategic calculation ... that voters will be choosing between (Bonnie Crombie) and the PCs," Kiss said, and so are "better off painting her negatively."
Former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne said she thinks Ford is "obsessed with (Crombie) and can't help going after her."
But "as the run-up to the election goes on, if she gains traction, Ford is going to get mad and his baser instincts will take over. In that context, we may look back on this and see it as a harbinger of things that were to come."
Graefe said part of the appeal of using "queen" feeds on an idea that Crombie is not "a woman of the people," is imperious and doesn't deal well with those who oppose her.
But given her humble roots - and Ford's upbringing in a wealthy family - Siemiatycki said "it's playing a populist card that applies, if anything, more to him than to Bonnie Crombie." He says the premier is likely trying to change the channel on controversies his government is currently facing.
The name-calling, however, "demeans our political discourse" and is adversarial, even toxic, and "queen of the carbon tax" is sexist language, Siemiatycki added.
As to whether using the term is fair, Kiss said, "What's fair in politics? There are no rules about this - voters are the ultimate judges."
CAPTION: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie speaks to reporters at Queen's Park in Toronto on March 26, 2024.

Subject: Taxes; Emissions trading; Voters; Carbon; Politics; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Taxes Emissions trading Environmental tax

Title: Is Doug Ford's put-down of Bonnie Crombie a smear, or just politics as usual?

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Apr 8, 2024

Section: politics/provincial

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Servi ces, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document type: News

ProQuest document ID: 3034468288

Document URL: https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-doug-fords-put-down-bonnie-crombie-smear-just/docview/3034468288/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 192 of 500

Elevator problems driving up project's price tag: Thousands of staff waiting to move into renovated complex

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/elevator-problems-driving-up-projects-price-tag/docview/3034134875/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Elevator+problems+driving+up+project%27s+price+tag%3A+Thousands+of+staff+waiting+to+move+into+renovated+complex&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-04-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Legislative Affairs Minister Paul Calandra acknowledged last month that the goal of beginning the work after the 2026 provincial election is "unlikely to happen."
Calandra and officials have been looking for 500,000 square feet of space near the legislative precinct as a temporary home for MPPs, political aides, legislative staff and journalists during the renovation.
The legislative precinct includes the Macdonald Block, putting civil servants within quick walking distance of their political masters instead of being located in several other locations downtown, as is the case during the construction work.
Depending on progress with the elevators, Surma said it may be possible to move staff back into one or two of the Macdonald Block buildings first instead of waiting for the entire complex to be completed.
"Those are all details of the plan and the schedule that's being negotiated now," Surma said.
The renovation to modernize the complex - bounded by Queen's Park Circle to the west, Wellesley Street to the north, Bay Street to the east and Grosvenor Street to the south - was intended to cut costs by fitting more people in and upgrading heating and cooling systems.
About 4,000 staff worked in the buildings before the renovation, but there will be room for 6,000 once the work is complete.
"The government will save $26 million annually," Surma said.
But until everything is sorted out, leases in other downtown buildings housing various ministry offices and staff may have to be extended.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Infrastructure; Elevators & escalators; Building codes; Civil service; Buildings; Politics; Courthouses

Business indexing term: Subject: Infrastructure

Location: Ontario Canada; Canada

Company / organization: Name: Infrastructure Ontario; NAICS: 926110, 926120

Document 193 of 500

Hawaii's hangin' loose - what about Ontario?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/hawaiis-hangin-loose-what-about-ontario/docview/3034134642/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hawaii%27s+hangin%27+loose+-+what+about+Ontario%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-04-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hula hips don't lie. But Hawaii has decided that its official gesture should be all in the fingers.
The Pacific Ocean archipelago recently became the first state in the U.S. to formally adopt a bit of body language. Or will do when, as expected, a pair of bills are passed through the legislature. The "Shaka": Pinky and thumb extended with the remaining digits curled downwards. "The wrist may be rotated back and forth for emphasis," as noted in the bill. But some insist that shaking the shaka is a no-no.
Also known as the "hang loose" sign, the shaka has long been associated with surf culture, conveying a range of friendly aloha spirit sentiments. It's ubiquitous in Hawaii, found even on Honolulu city buses - a digital shaka light that drivers turn on to thank motorists for letting them merge. The Central Pacific Bank calls their digital checking account Shaka Checking. And a high school football team from the Maui community devastated by last summer's deadly wildfire, invited to the Super Bowl in Las Vegas in February, flashed shakas for the cameras.
It was particularly useful during the pandemic when people were leery of shaking hands.
Take it from an Italian: Gestures speak volumes. In fact, hand gestures are considered the essence of Italian language and you're not really speaking colloquially unless hands and arms are providing semaphoring accompaniment. Most omnipresent: Fingers pinched, which means "What are you talking about?" or "Are you serious?"
Further from the signalling vernacular:
The chin flick - fingers brushed under the jaw, indicating indifference, don't care (or worse). The horns - superstitiously warding off the devil (especially when placed over the testicles) but also an insult. Hand flat across the chest - I can't stand you. The prayer clasp: Oh puhleeze. Both palms outstretched: I don't know, not my fault. Kissing the fingers - delicious, bliss. Tugging at the bottom of the eyelid: Watch out. The Umbrella - one arm bent facing upwards, the other slaps the crook of the bent arm's elbow, a common profanity.
Of course, shorthand (literally) in one culture could be exceedingly offensive in another, indicating something completely different. Even seemingly innocuous body positioning can be interpreted as an affront. I was once severely admonished in Afghanistan for stretching my legs while sitting on the floor and pointing my socks-clad feet towards a public speaker.
But back to Hawaii (I wish). Quite charming, officializing a motion. Ontario should consider following suit. After all, the province already has an official flower (white trillium), official bird (loon), official tree (white pine), official gemstone (amethyst) and official tartan (red, white, three shades of green, two shades of blue).
What gesture would best encapsulate Ontario, though?
The gimme-gimme hand outwards Because the province never tires of blaming Ottawa for its financial shortcomings. And some of the time they're actually on the money about that. Simultaneously, the outstretched palm could designate the urban problem of aggressive panhandling, an annoyance - pugnacious scrounging - given short shrift by the Supreme Court judge who this past week struck down the panhandling ban passed a quarter century ago by the Mike Harris government.
Not that cadgers had been paying the ban much mind. But Justice Robert Centa essentially gutted the Safe Streets Act, ruling most of its content as unconstitutional, knocking out prohibitions on soliciting donations in public, including from people near public toilets, pay phones, ATMs, taxi stands and public transit stops. Squeegie-ing, however, is still invasive-non-grata.
The swipe In days of old, the extended thumb and pinky to the ear meant call me later, universally understood. But hardly anyone makes phone calls anymore. It's all about the text because God forbid actual uttered words should be exchanged. Meanwhile the decline swipe left has become quotidian, whether rejecting the call or thumbs-down on the dating app hook-up seeker. The etiquette is brutal.
The swerve Denoting the Le Mons-style driving now required to navigate traffic-choked, construction-obstructed, pylon-studded, bicycle-lane-squished streets.
The up middle finger A one-gesture-fits-all to flip the bird at anybody who even mildly vexes or provokes because everybody is apparently on their last nerve.
The shrug Meh-me, meh-you. Default mime - physical version of a meme - when you've tuned out, retreated into ennui or just don't give a good damn anymore. Admittedly a condition that can wax and wane.
The evil eyeSimilar to the horns but more malevolent and specifically targeted at perceived enemies, of which I have many, you and you and you and you and ...
The twisted sphincterSymbolizing collective anal pedantry and fastidiousness. Personified by that neighbour who goes hissy-fit over the teensiest of cross-fence annoyances, shouts your kids off his lawn and sic's bylaw officers on any potential reno infringement. Admittedly difficult to portray as a PG gesture in a PC world.
On second thought, signalling - virtue or otherwise - might get you rage-clocked. We ain't no shaka sunny zone. Maybe best keep your hands in your pockets and shaddup.
CAPTION: Actor Dwayne Johnson makes Hawaii's "shaka" hand sign, sometimes known as the "hang loose" gesture often associated with surf culture. The archipelago recently became the first state in the U.S. to formally adopt body language.Chris Pizzellothe associated press file photo
CREDIT: Rosie DiManno Toronto Star OPINION

Subject: Hands

Location: Hawaii; United States--US

Title: Hawaii's hangin' loose - what about Ontario?

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: A.2

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Apr 8, 2024

Section: News

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 03190781

Source type: Newspaper

Language of publication: English

Document type: News

ProQuest document ID: 3034134642

Document URL: https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/hawaiis-hangin-loose-what-about-ontario/docview/3034134642/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 195 of 500

If the eclipse deserves a state of emergency, why not the true crises we face?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/if-eclipse-deserves-state-emergency-why-not-true/docview/3034034939/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=If+the+eclipse+deserves+a+state+of+emergency%2C+why+not+the+true+crises+we+face%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A kind of eclipse occurs at the beginning of Stanley Kubrick's cinematic masterpiece, "2001: A Space Odyssey." Then strange things start to happen: a mysterious black monolith appears; apes discover weapons; and a computer with artificial intelligence goes murderously haywire.
Eclipses have a great cosmic and psychological effect. It's nature doing a strange, wondrous thing providing cultural fodder for centuries. All that puts the current delirium around the upcoming eclipse into some perspective. It is indeed a big deal, even if a number of the reactions seem a bit over the top.
Some municipalities are anticipating and even cultivating a tourism boom, hoping to capitalize on it with eclipse events. Elsewhere, students are being sent home early, or for the entire day, lest teachers not be able to keep all the children in their charge from staring directly at it. Even knowing it's harmful, the urge to look at something as cool as an eclipse takes effort to resist. A brood of kids would be hard to control.
While the school issue seemed like overkill, the justification makes sense. The alternative might be just to keep them all indoors.
Still, the time off from school created quite a debate, perhaps only equal to Niagara region declaring a pre-emptive state of emergency as it expects more than a million people to head there on Monday. This is from a region that is used to lots of tourists arriving, so perhaps it knows something about that. For comparison, Sullivan County in neighbouring New York State declared a state of emergency during the 1969 Woodstock festival because of how many people converged there.
However, this "abundance of caution," as one Niagara official said, puts a lot of other things in perspective. If a state of emergency is declared for something as joyously fantastic as a total eclipse, why don't we do the same for the long list of other threats and problems we have? Ones that fester and cause harm to society, the economy or even death and injury.
Occasionally this happens. Just over a year ago, Niagara region declared another state of emergency, one that triggered far less debate. Enacted to get a handle on the triple threat of homelessness, mental health and opioid addiction, the move was to bring attention to the problems and seek additional support from the federal and provincial governments, though questions were raised if it would be effective.
It's not the first time this kind of thing has been discussed. In 2017, Toronto's board of health called for an emergency declaration over the surge in opioid overdoses and deaths, but then-minister of health Eric Hoskins rejected it. Today opioid carnage continues.
Some object to the eclipse state of emergency because it can open the door to curtailing civil liberties. It's not always the right move, it can be quite heavy handed and needs to be considered thoroughly before implementing.
Those specifics aside, what the eclipse emergency says to me is officials are taking it seriously, while there are an awful lot of important things we don't, issues that affect our lives much more than this celestial event.
I've likely written about the housing crisis more than any other issue, maybe our biggest next to climate change, yet even this week Doug Ford doubled down on his fourplex stand. There is no housing crisis, it seems, no emergency, no seriousness in actually trying to solve it. This isn't a unique dynamic.
Traffic gridlock, bickering over transit plans and even the epic, eclipse-worthiness of the Eglinton Crosstown LRT delays are often treated like a political squabble or even something to joke about rather than things that negatively affect hundreds of thousands of people daily. Why did it take months to get traffic wardens on King Street to keep the streetcar moving? Unseriousness.
Road conditions and behaviour that kill and injure pedestrians, cyclists and other drivers have, in Toronto, gone unenforced by our police, but also are treated with kid glove punishments and little change rather than having, say, a car impounded or shutting down the collision area to figure out why it happened and not reopening until there's a redesign.
But then even something as obviously lifesaving as a bike lane is easily objected to by politicians and residents alike. It's a useful example because, like a lot of things, if there's no direct personal benefit it's easy to object, to not take the consequences seriously, to be annoyed at how it might affect one's own lifestyle.
So maybe it's fine Niagara is declaring a state of emergency, showing they're serious about possible threats, even if they might not materialize. A cloudy Monday could make all the fuss for naught. Sometimes that's the best outcome.
We may not need a state of emergency for these other problems, but we need the same kind of seriousness.
CAPTION: Declaring a state of emergency over the solar eclipse shows that authorities are taking any potential dangers seriously. They should apply that to other, real crises.

Subject: Drug overdose; State of emergency; Narcotics

Title: If the eclipse deserves a state of emergency, why not the true crises we face?

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Apr 7, 2024

Section: opinion/contributors

Document 196 of 500

What is a fourplex, the building in the middle of a Jusin Trudeau-Doug Ford housing fight?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/what-is-fourplex-building-middle-jusin-trudeau/docview/3034034937/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+is+a+fourplex%2C+the+building+in+the+middle+of+a+Jusin+Trudeau-Doug+Ford+housing+fight%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Kyle Knoeck was in university, the Toronto planner lived in the very kind of housing that has now ensnarled Canadian politicians into a multi-billion-dollar dispute - the admired or admonished fourplex.
It's the type of housing Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is trying to lure provinces and territories into mandating, this week offering access to a $6-billion infrastructure fund if a province requires its municipalities to allow fourplexes as a right - a move Ontario Premier Doug Ford has roundly rejected.
But back then, the now-controversial housing form was simply an affordable place for a University of Toronto student to live, with Knoeck and his roommate renting a two-bedroom inside an old Edwardian house in Harbord Village.
"It was near the university. It was affordable. It was good, well-maintained housing," Knoeck said. Their street was a mixed bag of residents, he recalled, with an older couple on one side and a young family on the other.
The experience is one reason Toronto's now-director of zoning is puzzled at the current debate, particularly as Ford has suggested that allowing four-unit homes as a right would risk introducing "towers" into neighbourhood areas.
Toronto council has already legalized fourplexes across the city - after city planners like Knoeck recommended they be allowed on any residential lot to open up traditional single-family neighbourhoods to those who can't afford the million-dollar price-tags of a semi-detached or detached house.
"Different cities may face different issues, so I'm hesitant to say that everyone should adopt these," Knoeck said. But he sees them as having great potential. "I think there are lots of places that would benefit from more housing options."
What is a fourplex?
A fourplex is a residential building with four units inside. Unlike a townhouse, where units sit side-by-side, a fourplex - like other multiplexes such as triplexes and duplexes - involves some units stacked on top of the others. What that looks like can vary widely depending on age, architectural style and location.
Some fourplexes look similar to a single-family home, with four units contained within two storeys and even behind a single front door. Others can be taller, with three or more storeys, and can be identified as having four separate units from the street. Multiplexes of all types can be used as rentals, as multi-generational homes, or - as seen recently in East York - sold as separate condo units.
Under the new rules approved by Toronto council last year, builders of new multiplexes have to adhere to constraints like height limits. Unless an area was already zoned for taller buildings, Knoeck says the new rules largely mean a maximum of 10 or 11 metres, or roughly three-and-a-half storeys.
A multiplex, which is made up of separate self-contained units, is a different concept from a multi-tenant home or rooming house - where tenants rent their own bedrooms but share spaces like kitchens and bathrooms.
Why did Toronto expand its fourplex permissions?
While Toronto has some older multiplexes, the introduction of zoning laws around the 1950s and '60s stifled their development, as wide cuts of residential areas were set aside for semis and detached houses.
But in recent years, that zoning began to be re-examined, with some officials and advocates arguing allowing multiplexes could open the city's house-dominated neighbourhoods to a greater mix of housing types.
Proponents of zoning reform also argued the changes would allow more even population and housing growth - versus the jagged pattern that has areas such as Yonge and Eglinton increasingly packed with tall towers while blocks away house-dominated neighbourhoods have stayed stagnant. Some of Toronto's lowrise neighbourhoods have even seen their populations decline.
There was pushback, with residents' associations and individual Torontonians arguing more multiplexes could harm the city's tree cover, introduce more parking problems and traffic congestion, or alter their area's "character."
Some housing advocates also cautioned the new developments weren't guaranteed to be affordable to lower or moderate-income households.
But the expanded permissions were ultimately approved by council last May. Since then, city staff say they've received 74 fourplex proposals.
So why are politicians fighting over them?
As Canada's housing crisis charges on, many governments have eyed ways to increase supply and density. In Ontario, the province has introduced legislation to allow three units as a right on any residential lot.
But fourplexes have been a sticking point.
Last year, the federal government began dangling the promise of funding, via their Housing Accelerator Fund, to cities that showed progress on increasing density. In some cities, that became a de facto referendum on fourplexes.
In Mississauga, council deferred a decision on allowing more fourplex homes. But after the federal government warned the choice could jeopardize Mississauga's funding application, then-mayor Bonnie Crombie reversed course. The city received $112.9 million just 11 days after fourplexes were approved citywide.
In Windsor, Mayor Drew Dilkens accused the federal government of using fourplex legalization as a "minimum ticket" to get funding. "We have offered a fair, reasonable and sensible solution to allow more housing to be built but we won't be bullied into a solution that alters the quality of life of residents who have worked hard to buy a home in a great neighbourhood," he wrote.
Windsor's funding application was ultimately denied.
Since then, Ottawa has doubled down, with Trudeau introducing the $6-billion fund this week with explicit conditions for provinces and territories on fourplex legalization - which Ford has so far refused, telling reporters in Vaughan on Wednesday he didn't "believe in forcing municipalities" who remain opposed.
Four units or four storeys?
Ford has stated particular concern about fourplexes potentially being built to four storeys - larger than currently allowed in most of Toronto, but something his own housing affordability task force recommended in 2022.
"You have to differentiate between putting four units in an existing house or your neighbour tearing down the house and putting a four-storey tower," Ford told reporters in March.
In Toronto, Knoeck said planners did not feel the need to allow up to four above-ground storeys everywhere. Looking at Ontario's building code, he said the rules require an elevator at that height, which could be expensive enough to halt a project.
While the federal government has also urged four units, not four storeys, some activists still hope to see the 2022 task force recommendation carried out.
Among them is Eric Lombardi, a 29-year-old organizer with a group called More Neighbours that has advocated for denser housing options. Lombardi, like Knoeck, lived in a fourplex many years ago, when he moved to Toronto following his undergraduate degree. The four-unit building in the Kensington Market area was more than a century old, and not in pristine shape, he said.
But for himself and three friends, it was a reasonably priced way to live near their work and schools. If more of that housing existed - especially newer ones in better condition - he sees it as a way to keep young people in the city.
"You are actually incentivizing the creation of larger floorplans, and nicer layouts, with better sunlight," he said, "that are in ground-oriented neighbourhoods that families everywhere in Canada like to live in."
With files from Robert Benzie
CAPTION: A fourplex on Seaton Street in Toronto that resembles a detached house.A fourplex on Amelia Street in Toronto with obvious stacked units.A fourplex on Indian Road in Toronto.A fourplex on Bayview Avenue in Toronto with a single front door.

Selective caution while true crises linger: If eclipse deserves a state of emergency, why not the long list of threats and problems we face?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/selective-caution-while-true-crises-linger/docview/3034003474/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Selective+caution+while+true+crises+linger%3A+If+eclipse+deserves+a+state+of+emergency%2C+why+not+the+long+list+of+threats+and+problems+we+face%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-04-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A kind of eclipse occurs at the beginning of Stanley Kubrick's cinematic masterpiece, "2001: A Space Odyssey." Then strange things start to happen: a mysterious black monolith appears; apes discover weapons; and a computer with artificial intelligence goes murderously haywire.
Eclipses have a great cosmic and psychological effect. It's nature doing a strange, wondrous thing providing cultural fodder for centuries. All that puts the current delirium around the upcoming eclipse into some perspective. It is indeed a big deal, even if a number of the reactions seem a bit over the top.
Some municipalities are anticipating and even cultivating a tourism boom, hoping to capitalize on it with eclipse events. Elsewhere, students are being sent home early, or for the entire day, lest teachers not be able to keep all the children in their charge from staring directly at it. Even knowing it's harmful, the urge to look at something as cool as an eclipse takes effort to resist. A brood of kids would be hard to control.
While the school issue seemed like overkill, the justification makes sense. The alternative might be just to keep them all indoors.
Still, the time off from school created quite a debate, perhaps only equal to Niagara region declaring a pre-emptive state of emergency as it expects more than a million people to head there on Monday. This is from a region that is used to lots of tourists arriving, so perhaps it knows something about that. For comparison, Sullivan County in neighbouring New York state declared a state of emergency during the 1969 Woodstock festival because of how many people converged there.
However, this "abundance of caution," as one Niagara official said, puts a lot of other things in perspective.
If a state of emergency is declared for something as joyously fantastic as a total eclipse, why don't we do the same for the long list of other threats and problems we have? Ones that fester and cause harm to society, the economy or even death and injury.
Occasionally this happens. Just over a year ago, Niagara region declared another state of emergency, one that triggered far less debate. Enacted to get a handle on the triple threat of homelessness, mental health and opioid addiction, the move was to bring attention to the problems and seek additional support from the federal and provincial governments, though questions were raised if it would be effective.
It's not the first time this kind of thing has been discussed. In 2017, Toronto's board of health called for an emergency declaration over the surge in opioid overdoses and deaths, but then-minister of health Eric Hoskins rejected it. Today opioid carnage continues.
Some object to the eclipse state of emergency because it can open the door to curtailing civil liberties. It's not always the right move, it can be quite heavy-handed and needs to be considered thoroughly before implementing.
Those specifics aside, what the eclipse emergency says to me is officials are taking it seriously, while there are an awful lot of important things we don't, issues that affect our lives much more than this celestial event.
I've likely written about the housing crisis more than any other issue, maybe our biggest next to climate change, yet even this past week Doug Ford doubled down on his fourplex stand. There is no housing crisis, it seems, no emergency, no seriousness in actually trying to solve it. This isn't a unique dynamic.
Traffic gridlock, bickering over transit plans and even the epic, eclipse-worthiness of the Eglinton Crosstown LRT delays are often treated like a political squabble or even something to joke about rather than things that negatively affect hundreds of thousands of people daily. Why did it take months to get traffic wardens on King Street to keep the streetcar moving? Unseriousness.
Road conditions and behaviour that kill and injure pedestrians, cyclists and other drivers have, in Toronto, gone unenforced by our police, but also are treated with kid glove punishments and little change rather than having, say, a car impounded or shutting down the collision area to figure out why it happened and not reopening until there's a redesign.
But then even something as obviously lifesaving as a bike lane is easily objected to by politicians and residents alike. It's a useful example because, like a lot of things, if there's no direct personal benefit it's easy to object, to not take the consequences seriously, to be annoyed at how it might affect one's own lifestyle.
So maybe it's fine Niagara is declaring a state of emergency, showing they're serious about possible threats, even if they might not materialize. A cloudy Monday could make all the fuss for naught. Sometimes that's the best outcome.
We may not need a state of emergency for these other problems, but we need the same kind of seriousness.
CAPTION: Declaring a state of emergency over the solar eclipse shows that authorities are taking any potential dangers seriously. They should apply that to other, real crises, writes Shawn Micallef.ORLANDO SIERRAAFP via Getty Images file photo
CREDIT: Shawn Micallef OPINION

Subject: Drug overdose; State of emergency; Narcotics; Solar eclipses

Fire destroys band office in North Spirit Lake

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fire-destroys-band-office-north-spirit-lake/docview/3034003462/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Fire+destroys+band+office+in+North+Spirit+Lake&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-04-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Police are investigating after a fire destroyed a First Nation band office in northern Ontario.
Nishnawbe Aski Police said its crime unit is now investigating Thursday's early morning fire at the North Spirit Lake band office and a nearby abandoned home.
Police said they got a call shortly before 3:40 a.m. about a fire in the remote community, which is located about 500 kilometres northwest of Thunder Bay.
Officers found the community's band office in flames, and then also learned about the fire at the abandoned home.
Police say no injuries have been reported, but both buildings are "a total loss."
Ontario NDP legislator Sol Mamakwa, who represents the region, issued a social media post calling the fire a "huge loss" for the community.
He said most programs in the community were administered through the band office building.

Document 199 of 500

Ford's Crombie put-down a smear or just politics?: Premier's use of nickname for rival raises some eyebrows

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-crombie-put-down-smear-just-politics/docview/3034003294/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+Crombie+put-down+a+smear+or+just+politics%3F%3A+Premier%27s+use+of+nickname+for+rival+raises+some+eyebrows&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-04-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford has dubbed Bonnie Crombie the "queen of the carbon tax."
Does that make him king of the put-downs?
At Queen's Park and during news conferences, the snub is repeated - about 45 times in the legislature this year alone - by a dozen ministers and MPPs, and the premier himself numerous times when he's in front of the microphone making government announcements.
It's catchy, alliterative and conveys a simple message to his supporters and a wider audience of voters - Crombie supports higher taxes, and she's out of touch with Ontarians on a hot-button issue that has divided the country, experts say.
But when the Liberal leader has already ruled out introducing a carbon levy in Ontario, some wonder why Ford continues to use that line of attack, especially as it includes a gendered term.
"I think he thinks it's derogatory because he's trying to position himself in front of the anti-carbon-tax parade," said Peggy Nash, a former New Democrat MP who is now at Toronto Metropolitan University (TMU).
"I think he thinks that associating her with the carbon tax is the pejorative part of it" and not the term "queen," added Nash, who has authored a book on women running for office.
"Whether you call somebody a queen or king, that implies they're a leader. Now the irony is, didn't Bonnie Crombie say she wasn't going to be applying the carbon tax? It is a bit of an ironic tag for someone who clearly is not the queen."
Myer Siemiatycki, a retired TMU professor and long-time observer of politics, said "that kind of language is problematic and objectionable on a number of grounds ... (It's) a demeaning and mocking characterization of a position that a female politician has taken."
For Ford, the term is fair game - he said his Liberal rival did not speak out against Ottawa's carbon levy and "as a federal Liberal, Bonnie Crombie was one of the earliest supporters of a carbon tax. She supported a carbon tax before there was a carbon tax, making her the queen of the carbon tax."
Crombie was elected as a Liberal member of Parliament in 2008 and defeated in the 2011 election - four years before the Trudeau government, which introduced the federal carbon levy, came to power.
In a statement to the Star, Ford's director of communications, Caitlin Clark, said, "back then, Bonnie championed a carbon tax that would have imposed even higher costs on Ontario families, and she's never stood up against the federal carbon tax and remained silent about this week's 23 per cent increase," referring to the jump in price of carbon to $80 a tonne from $65 on April 1.
In addition, Clark added, "just days ago, she said she's considering re-implementing the cap-and-trade carbon tax and scrapping the gas tax cut that provides relief at the pumps and puts hundreds of dollars back into peoples' pockets."
For her part, Crombie said that if "I earned that crown," then the moniker would fit - but it doesn't.
"I've been in politics a long time. I have a thick skin and I can take political punches," she said in an interview. "I'm willing to stand up for myself and other women. But that comment really crosses the line, simply because they are perpetuating an untrue statement."
The "only reason we have a carbon tax in Ontario is because of Doug Ford, and that he ripped up the made-in-Ontario climate plan that we did have through the previous (Liberal) government - that's the only reason we have a carbon tax today is because they cancelled cap and trade," Crombie said. "So it's a false statement."
Crombie has created a climate action panel, headed by Beaches-East York MPP Mary Margaret McMahon, to help craft the party's 2026 election platform. A return to a cap-and-trade system could be a part of it, she added, but all options are on the table.
Part of the appeal of repeating "queen of the carbon tax" is for the Ontario PC party to define Crombie as a leader, said Peter Graefe, a political science professor at McMaster University.
Even though the Liberals were reduced to so few seats in the past two elections that they didn't even quality for official party status, Ford clearly sees her as more of a rival than NDP Leader Marit Stiles, said Graefe.
"As soon as Bonnie Crombie was elected, the Conservatives wanted to not give her any space to really establish herself and paint her, early on, as someone who cannot be trusted by the suburban electorate ... and I think very early on, they came up with this idea that they would try and pin her as a person who would support carbon taxes," said Graefe.
"Calling her the 'queen of the carbon taxes' ... it's something that's easy to repeat, pretty short form and on the face of it is not too derogatory, but certainly has the impact of putting the label on her, but also portraying her as a bit of a cartoony character."
Simon Kiss, a digital media and journalism professor at Wilfrid Laurier University, said "queen of the carbon tax" may play well now with some voters, but when inflation is tamed, concerns about the levy may go away.
"In which case, is it going to be that effective to label somebody 'queen of the carbon tax'?"
Ford and the Progressive Conservative party "very clearly see her as the alternative - they don't give a damn about Marit Stiles and the NDP. They've made a strategic calculation ... that voters will be choosing between (Bonnie Crombie) and the PCs," Kiss said, and so are "better off painting her negatively."
Former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne said she thinks Ford is "obsessed with (Crombie) and can't help going after her." But "as the run-up to the election goes on, if she gains traction, Ford is going to get mad and his baser instincts will take over. In that context, we may look back on this and see it as a harbinger of things that were to come."
Graefe said part of the appeal of using "queen" feeds on an idea that Crombie is not "a woman of the people," is imperious and doesn't deal well with those who oppose her.
But given her humble roots - and Ford's upbringing in a wealthy family - Siemiatycki said "it's playing a populist card that applies, if anything, more to him than to Bonnie Crombie." He says the premier is likely trying to change the channel on controversies his government is currently facing.
The name-calling, however, "demeans our political discourse" and is adversarial, even toxic, and "queen of the carbon tax" is sexist language, Siemiatycki added.
As to whether using the term is fair, Kiss said, "What's fair in politics? There are no rules about this - voters are the ultimate judges."
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, dubbed "queen of the carbon tax" by Premier Doug Ford, says she has "a thick skin and I can take political punches."Chris YoungThe Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau
