Document 211 of 500

Tech Update: Innovation gets charged up at University of Waterloo's battery research centre

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/tech-update-innovation-gets-charged-up-at/docview/3033041287/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tech+Update%3A+Innovation+gets+charged+up+at+University+of+Waterloo%27s+battery+research+centre&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Batteries are a hot commodity right now: Demand is surging as the market for electric vehicles and other battery-powered tech steadily grows. The most common rechargeable batteries, which power everything from EVs to smartphones, are made with lithium - but extracting the element exacts a huge toll on the environment. And once those batteries run out of juice, there aren't many viable answers to the question of what to do with the waste. While recycling is an option, it's costly and hard to scale.
Luckily, innovations in this realm are multiplying in tandem with increasing demand for batteries. Bolstered by nearly $5 million in combined funding from the Canadian Foundation for Innovation and the University of Waterloo, the Ontario Battery and Electrochemistry research Centre (OBEC) at the university will work to accelerate the development of innovative tech. According to Linda Nazar, the Canada Research Chair in Solid State Energy Materials and one of the centre's lead researchers, OBEC is exploring a range of options. "There's no silver bullet," she says. The ideal solution would be "low cost, inherently safe, sustainable and high in energy density with great power capabilities and long cycle life. And you can't hit all of those targets with one technology - at least not yet." That means Nazar and her colleagues are digging into everything from sodium ion batteries (popular in the '80s, this rechargeable tech uses sodium to carry a charge) to metal air technologies (where power is generated when metals react with oxygen), as well as overseeing a fabrication facility. "Literally everything is on the menu," she says. Nazar also notes that the centre's location is ideal because of its proximity to both automotive manufacturers and startups.
While OBEC homes in on the cells themselves, startups across the country are working on other innovations to help optimize batteries. For instance, Montreal-based startup FTEX is focusing on tech to help batteries power motors more efficiently. The company has developed an inverter that uses gallium nitride, a semiconductor material that conducts energy more efficiently than silicon. An algorithm controls and reduces the amount of energy used by a vehicle, preserving battery life and saving electricity. For now, FTEX's technology is used in smaller modes of transport (like e-bikes and scooters), but Ramee Mossa, FTEX's co-founder and CEO, says that there are plans to eventually scale up to motorcycles and cars.
It stands to reason that the country is seeing such promising battery-related innovation, says OBEC's Nazar: "It's an area where Canada is poised to make a tremendous contribution. We have a fair amount of the natural resources needed for a lot of these systems, a highly educated population, and the combination of that and the science and technology should be instrumental in pushing this forward."
Quebec's agtech incubator takes root
Cutting-edge labs, tech workshops, and access to drones and other infrastructure will be among the highlights of Zone Agtech's new innovation centre in the Montreal suburb of L'Assomption, which is funded by a $42-million investment from the government of Quebec. Zone Agtech is hoping to help startups accelerate their development of new solutions to agricultural problems.
New Ontario budget supports innovation centres
The government of Ontario dropped its annual budget, which contains investments to the innovation sector. An additional $100 million will be added to the Invest Ontario Fund and there are plans to create a $12-million Health Technology Accelerator Fund. Doug Ford's government will also launch a new Regional Innovation Centre in Barrie to further expand innovation and economic growth. It will be the 18th Regional Innovation Centre in the province.
IBM sets its sights on Quebec's DeepSight
A new partnership between DeepSight/Réalité Augmentée and IBM will facilitate the integration of the Quebec-based startup's AR-powered learning platform and the computing giant's AI technology to create real-time training and guidance for workers. DeepSight harnesses augmented reality - interactive tech that superimposes digital content on real-life surroundings - to provide workplace guidance by letting users engage with holographic simulations of potential scenarios and/or equipment.
By the numbers:
$4.5 billion: The amount that Ontario school boards are suing social media platforms Snapchat, TikTok and Meta for in a class-action lawsuit. The school boards allege the social platforms are deliberately harming students.
$2 billion-plus: How much big pharma firm AstraZeneca is shelling out to buy Hamilton-based biopharmaceutical precision oncology company Fusion Pharmaceuticals.
$16 million: The amount the federal and Quebec governments are investing to establish an AI computing cluster in Laval, Que.
CAPTION: Building better (and more efficient) batteries and developing new ways to manage expended ones (such as the lithium-ion supply pictured here) are top priorities for the team at the Ontario Battery and Electrochemistry research Centre at the University of Waterloo.
CREDIT: Rebecca Gao

Document 212 of 500

This Toronto multiplex has four units, including a garden suite. They're all for sale - separately

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/this-toronto-multiplex-has-four-units-including/docview/3032923103/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=This+Toronto+multiplex+has+four+units%2C+including+a+garden+suite.+They%27re+all+for+sale+-+separately&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On a tree-lined street a short walk from Pape Village, something new has risen from the footprint of a former bungalow. The property now hosts a four-unit condo multiplex, with each residence for sale separately.
From the street, a white and brown three-bedroom semi-detached home appears to float over the single parking space and shares a wall with the dark brick two-bedroom townhome. Nestled below is a one-bedroom basement suite, which looks out on a fenced-in yard adjoining the brown three-bedroom garden suite. They present a modern facade, with large windows.
Together, the four newly built units form a condominium, all for sale. It's a style of multiplex new to Toronto, and industry insiders say it could spark an increase in similar projects springing up across the city.
Armen Krikorian, the Big City Realty real estate broker for 319 Mortimer Ave., says the project was the result of a "perfect storm": the combination of the builder's unique vision and the City of Toronto's need for more housing.
The builder acquired the property for $1.02 million in October 2020, originally home to one of the many bungalows that line the street. After some delays, which Krikorian attributed to the process to secure permits, the new homes were listed for sale last week.
"It hits every demographic and every family size," said Krikorian. "Young couples, downsizers in the area, people that want to be close to downtown but yet don't want to be uptown."
The semi and the townhome each sport balconies overlooking the fenced-in backyard. Inside, the units are bright and outfitted with new appliances, including a gas stove, with wooden finishes. Each unit has its own designated outdoor space divided by fencing, and although only the semi has a parking spot the property is within walking distance to multiple transit lines and designated street parking.
The basement unit is listed for $499,900, while the townhouse is on the market for $999,900. The price of the semi-detached unit, at $1,599,900, and garden suite, listed for $1,299,900, rival listings for detached homes in the neighbourhood.
"Not a lot of people are doing it, but I think as property values are still quite high, this is going to be an attractive new option for people to look at as a sort of a pathway to home ownership," said Sean Galbraith, president of Galbraith & Associates urban planning firm in Toronto. The smaller units, Galbraith said, could be particularly appealing for first-time homebuyers.
What stood out to Galbraith is that this project obtained city approval, as he said the city has largely kept a focus on garden suites and laneway homes as rental units, not as separate homes for sale.
"If this represents a shift in the city's thinking that can be applied elsewhere in the city, I think we're definitely going to see it lead to an increase in the number of garden suites and laneway suites that are built," said Galbraith, who has 20 years of experience in the industry, including as a senior planner for the Ministry of Municipal Affairs and Housing.
In an email Wednesday, city staff said this multiplex project fell within the existing zoning provisions for the property, and was approved in 2023 as part of the city's expanded "missing middle" housing push.
Multiplexes have been a point of political contention lately as the federal government has offered provinces access to billions in infrastructure funding so long as they require cities to allow fourplexes as of right. Ontario Premier Doug Ford, however, has said overriding municipalities to force fourplex permissions is "off the table" for the province. Instead, he says his government is focused on single-family homes and townhomes.
The City of Toronto has already taken the steps the federal government is trying to compel, legalizing up to four-unit homes citywide last July, with new rules that allow duplexes, triplexes and fourplexes as a right, though the new structures have to meet certain conditions around height and setbacks that depend on the neighbourhood they're in.
Krikorian said all of the Mortimer Avenue units are part of a condominium, with snow removal, landscaping, waste removal and other maintenance and insurance overseen by a property management company. Each unit pays condo fees - $394.20 for the townhouse, $230.15 for the basement, $633.08 for the semi-detached and $491.44 for the garden suite.
He said the homes' residents will make up the condo board and can decide to do away with the property management company's oversight - although given there are an even number of units, he was not able to confirm who would have ultimate say if the owners disagreed.
The builder, HMV Homes, had worked on similar projects in Quebec, Krikorian said. After a few years working in and studying the market in Toronto, HMV Homes decided to bring this style of building here.
Krikorian said the garden suite was the hardest to determine a listing price for, alongside the builder, as there were "zero comparable" homes for sale in the area - leaving them to look at detached homes and condos nearby to determine a price point.
There are perks to this type of project, Galbraith said, both for a builder who can see higher upfront earnings than if the units were listed as rentals, and for buyers who could find a street-level condo in a residential neighbourhood. But potential buyers in these situations should be people who are comfortable working alongside others on decisions related to their home.
"Communal living to some extent is not for everybody, but it's for a lot of people," he said.
After seeing this building land on the market, Galbraith expects to see a rising interest from other builders looking to replicate the project. For him, it's already prompted discussion with colleagues about the potential opened up by this pioneering development.
"I've got a list of people that I'm going to be calling back and saying, 'Hey, when I told you that it wasn't likely before, well, things have changed. Let's have another conversation,'" he said.
CAPTION: The units all feature modern finishes and appliances. Document 213 of 500

Justin Trudeau is angering Canada's premiers with his legacy projects. Inside the fight between provinces and the prime minister

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-is-angering-canadas-premiers-with/docview/3032923033/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+is+angering+Canada%27s+premiers+with+his+legacy+projects.+Inside+the+fight+between+provinces+and+the+prime+minister&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Prime Minister Justin Trudeau came to power promising a new era of federal-provincial co-operation, but now the Liberal leader finds himself locked in battles with friends and foes alike.It's not just with "Conservative premiers" who Trudeau says are unserious about tackling climate change because they oppose the federal Liberal consumer carbon-levy-and-rebate scheme. And it's not just about the carbon price.Trudeau's battles are across the country and on a range of files, where the prime minister claims the federal Liberals are more "ambitious." They show just how much pandemic-era goodwill between him and the premiers has eroded, reveal a federal government urgently trying to win some political credit at a time when the Liberals are vulnerable, and lay bare contradictions in Trudeau's arguments.Trudeau is right that Conservative premiers have opposed his climate action plan alongside federal Conservatives who long ago won the shorthand contest, branding it a "carbon tax" even if it's technically a levy.However, the Conservative premiers are now joined by people who should be Trudeau's political allies: Newfoundland and Labrador Liberal Premier Andrew Furey, Manitoba's newly elected Premier Wab Kinew, and Ontario Liberal party leader Bonnie Crombie.All have distanced themselves from Trudeau's consumer carbon price-and-rebate program.Seven premiers, including Furey, asked Trudeau to pause the rate increase that just took effect - the April carbon price went up by $15 a tonne or about 3.3 cents on a litre of gasoline - at the very least. Some want to ditch the carbon price altogether and look at other ways to incentivize change. Only two - B.C.'s David Eby and Yukon's Ranj Pillai - are still standing behind the federal program, which neither B.C. nor Quebec are subject to because they have their own carbon cap-and-trade pricing system aimed at emissions reductions.Furey penned an open letter to Trudeau calling for a first ministers' meeting. He said Ottawa should look at the U.S. where there is no carbon tax, and the Biden administration has gone all out in using the power of the federal treasury to offer public subsidies to leverage a "green economy" transition and prime its electric vehicle industry. On top of carbon pricing concerns, the premiers are fuming over Trudeau's latest moves to stomp all over provincial jurisdiction, announcing about $25 billion in pre-budget measures in the past week to expand housing, child care, and school lunch and breakfast programs - all provincial areas.Those are on top of Ottawa's forays into health care with the Liberal government's offer of free diabetes meds and birth control as first steps toward a universal drug coverage plan, and with the rollout of a dental care plan.They are all complex programs to put in place and administer. They all require a measure of provincial buy-in, or at least co-ordination . And on none, according to two senior government sources in different provinces, did Trudeau consult with the provinces in advance. There was obviously some co-ordination with NDP premiers in B.C. and Manitoba, who were persuaded to join Trudeau for a pair of housing photo-ops in the past week for the Liberal pre-budget tour that's underway.Yet on one particularly provocative measure - Tuesday's promise of $5 billion in new infrastructure spending to boost new housing construction that came with strings attached - it was Canada's biggest city mayor, Toronto's Olivia Chow, who stood by and applauded, not Premier Doug Ford.Several premiers talked about it privately that night on a phone call. The next day, several went public, calling it an overreach.One provincial source privy to discussions among the provinces said they had never seen this level of frustration among premiers, calling it a risk for national unity.Trudeau shrugged off questions about intruding on provincial jurisdiction Thursday. He said Canadians want to see action.Ford, too, publicly downplayed jurisdictional squabbles, saying that everyone just wants to get housing built. But the Ontario premier made clear he doesn't think Ottawa should be dictating to other levels of government, including municipalities, whether they must allow fourplexes to be built without municipal permits (or "as of right").Ford has long opposed Trudeau on carbon pricing, but the reality is on many other files he has been an ally of the Trudeau government. So it seems curious Trudeau saw the need to pick the fourplex battle with him. Yet there's irony in the fact that Trudeau - who said in August "housing isn't a primary federal responsibility" - is a prime minister now prepared to dictate conditions for housing funds to the provinces or bypass them altogether. Trudeau says he will give the money directly to municipalities willing to accept those conditions and match the federal "ambition" to move faster on housing.Last week, it was "Conservative premiers" who weren't ambitious enough on child care. They were deliberately "slow-walking" the rollout of the Liberals' $10-a-day daycare plan because, Trudeau said, they were taking their cue from federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre.On Thursday, standing next to Premier Kinew, the prime minister insisted he remains "open" to working with provinces, and to hearing Manitoba's carbon tax exemption plan right after their housing photo-op."Provinces that want to price pollution in their own way can do so as long as it is equivalent in impact to what's going on elsewhere in the country, for matters of fairness and for matters of shared ambition," he said.So when he asserts federal jurisdiction, Trudeau insists the provinces have to follow his lead. And when he flexes federal spending power in areas of provincial jurisdiction, they still have to follow his lead. If it's a matter of winning political credit, it may also come at a price of losing political allies.As for that request for a first ministers' meeting to revisit carbon pricing, Trudeau seems cool to the idea.He said Thursday he met with all premiers in 2016 and decided on the "pan-Canadian" framework for addressing climate change.Still, several of those premiers have changed and a post-pandemic affordability crisis could factor into any thinking about the need for another such meeting.Although Alberta, Saskatchewan, Ontario, New Brunswick, Prince Edward Island, Nova Scotia and Newfoundland and Labrador all asked Trudeau to pause the 23-per-cent carbon price increase this month, Trudeau says it comes with a matching rebate, and he's not budging.For all of Trudeau's determination to stay the course, he is aware of how acutely Canadians are feeling the pain of inflation and high interest rates, yet appears unwilling to take his own advice for how to decrease polarization and division in the nation. He told CBC host Matt Galloway Thursday it behooves everyone "to start talking and listening to each other. And we need to start working together to build stronger communities."For sure, Trudeau's right. In an era of social media and echo chambers, complex discussions are harder to have. All the more reason to have them.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau talks to media during an affordable housing press conference at a public housing development in Winnipeg on April 4, 2024.

Subject: Child care; Carbon; Politics; Pandemics; Political alliances; Environmental tax; Jurisdiction; Affordable housing; Climate change; Prime ministers

Document 214 of 500

Watchdog clears Ford aide: Accepts chief of staff's defence that key Greenbelt email was 'overlooked'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/watchdog-clears-ford-aide/docview/3032922642/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Watchdog+clears+Ford+aide%3A+Accepts+chief+of+staff%27s+defence+that+key+Greenbelt+email+was+%27overlooked%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-04-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's ethics watchdog has cleared Premier Doug Ford's chief of staff of wrongdoing after the discovery of an "overlooked" email linked to the $8.28-billion Greenbelt scandal.
In response to a complaint from NDP Leader Marit Stiles, integrity commissioner J. David Wake said he has "accepted" Patrick Sackville's explanation of how his Oct. 17, 2022, email exchange - on private accounts instead of government servers - with Ryan Amato, at the time chief of staff to then-municipal affairs minister Steve Clark, was missed.
"I have concluded that the Oct. 17 email sent from Mr. Amato to Mr. Sackville's personal email in the circumstances I have outlined does not support a finding that Patrick Sackville knew about the specific removal criteria for lands from the Greenbelt or the properties selected for removal until he was briefed with others, as he testified, on Oct. 27," Wake wrote Thursday.
While the integrity commissioner said "it is unfortunate that this email was not retrieved in accordance with the request for documents I made to Mr. Sackville ... I have accepted his explanation as to how it was overlooked."
Ford's chief of staff told Wake it "was an oversight on my part" that the missive fell through the cracks because it was to his personal account rather than his government email.
"When you previously sought documents relevant to the issues you are investigating, I conducted a diligent search of my records that would be responsive to your request. However, I did not locate the email from Mr. Amato in making that search," said Sackville.
"In conducting some due diligence related to freedom of information requests, I later became aware of this email and, consistent with my obligations, provided it to you expeditiously," he said, referring to the message being found last December.
The opposition New Democrats, who obtained the documents through a freedom of information request, argued the email contradicted testimony given under oath to the integrity commissioner last year.
In the message from Amato to Sackville, the ministerial chief of staff shared a "list of criteria for removals" of 7,400 acres from the two-million-acre environmentally sensitive Greenbelt around the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area.
These include "infrastructure services," such as proximity to roads, gas and hydro lines and water mains, police and fire services.
The email also listed "potential offsets" such as the Paris Galt Moraine, "green space our government has decided not to move on" as well as "options to go larger depending on executive interest."
At issue is the timing of the email, which is at odds with testimony made under oath to Wake for his Aug. 30 report on the Greenbelt affair.
"Members of the premier's staff also told us they were not involved in the selection of properties," the integrity commissioner wrote last summer.
"Mr. Sackville said he did not discuss specific properties to be removed or removal criteria with Mr. Amato until the briefing that occurred on Oct. 27, 2022," he noted at the time.
However, that was 10 days after the Oct. 17 email in which criteria for removal of the lands from the Greenbelt were addressed. The changes were finally announced publicly on Nov. 4, 2022.
Sackville declined to comment when contacted by the Star on Thursday, as did Amato.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford eventually cancelled the Greenbelt land swap discussed in the email. The deal is now being investigated by the RCMP.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 215 of 500

Ottawa proposes $1.5B rental fund: Measure aimed at helping non-profits, partners acquire more units and keep prices affordable

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ottawa-proposes-1-5b-rental-fund/docview/3032922623/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+proposes+%241.5B+rental+fund%3A+Measure+aimed+at+helping+non-profits%2C+partners+acquire+more+units+and+keep+prices+affordable&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-04-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau unveiled a new $1.5-billion housing fund Thursday that he billed as helping non-profit organizations acquire more rental units across Canada and ensure they remain affordable.
The new Canada Rental Protection Fund will be part of the April 16 federal budget, which Trudeau's Liberals are already promoting aggressively as part of a long-term play to win back younger voters.
The fund will provide $1 billion in loans and $470 million in contributions to non-profits and other partners to help them acquire affordable rental units.
An existing rental protection fund in British Columbia is a good "proof of concept" for the initiative, Trudeau told a news conference in Winnipeg.
"They recognize that for every new affordable rental home that is built in their province, four more are lost to investors, to conversions, to demolition, and to rent increases," Trudeau said.
"And this is happening in communities right across the country."
Thursday's announcement was just the latest in a series of new housing measures unveiled by the Liberal government during a campaign-style pre-budget tour across the country that got underway last week. The renewed political focus on housing policy comes as experts warn significant government action is needed to spur home construction and help close the gap between supply and demand.
Home prices and activity in the housing market are expected to rebound as the Bank of Canada begins lowering interest rates, with economists widely expecting rate cuts to begin in June or July.
As Trudeau spoke, the Conservatives were quick to point to a new report from the Canada Mortgage and Housing Corp. that warns next year's home prices could match 2022's peaks and exceed them by 2026.
Thursday's housing market outlook report also said housing starts in Canada would likely decline this year before recovering in 2025 and 2026 - a reflection of the lingering impact of higher interest rates.
Protecting and expanding the country's rental stock has been a particular focus for all three levels of government in recent months as Canadians face skyrocketing rents.
Advocates in the social housing and non-profit space have been calling for a mechanism to help them buy up affordable rentals that might otherwise be sold off to investors.
The Federation of Canadian Municipalities applauded the Liberal government's announcement, noting it has advocated for a similar fund for years. The federation said it will be in talks with the federal government to ensure municipal initiatives are eligible for the fund.
Meanwhile, Conservatives panned the announcement and as they've done for months, blamed the Liberals writ large for soaring housing costs.
"Today, Justin Trudeau announced another billion-dollar photo op that will result in not one single additional home being built in Canada," Conservative Deputy Leader Melissa Lantsman said in a statement.
New Democrats, who have advocated for the creation of an acquisition fund, called the news a step forward, but long overdue and insufficient.
"The underwhelming funding commitment and delayed rollout show that the Liberals will find a way to disappoint," said Jenny Kwan, the NDP's housing critic.
So far, the measures the government has been proposing to address the housing crisis appear inspired by policy ideas from Mike Moffatt, a senior director of policy and innovation at the Smart Prosperity Institute.
Many of the recommendations made in two reports co-authored by the institute have been adopted by the Liberals since the fall, including Thursday's announcement of a new acquisition fund.
On Wednesday, Trudeau said the Liberals would add another $15 billion to an apartment construction loan program, bringing available funding to $55 billion.
The loan program was launched in 2017 and has helped create more than 48,000 homes so far. It's aimed at building at least 131,000 apartments in the next decade.
On Tuesday, the federal government announced a $6-billion infrastructure fund to support homebuilding and a $400 million top-up to the housing accelerator fund.
The Liberals say that funding for provinces and territories will come with conditions, including adopting the recently announced renters' bill of rights and allowing fourplexes to be built on residential land in municipalities.
Premiers from several provinces, including Ontario, Saskatchewan and Alberta, have slammed the federal government for overstepping into provincial jurisdiction.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau holds eight- month-old Tori at a public housing development in Winnipeg on Thursday. The new Canada Rental Protection Fund will be part of the April 16 federal budget.JOHN WOODS THE CANADIAN PRESS
CREDIT: Nojoud Al Mallees The Canadian Press

Subject: Federal budget; Interest rates; Rents; Rentals; Affordable housing; Funding; Prime ministers; Housing starts; Nonprofit organizations

Document 216 of 500

Crombie slams Ford's 'pocketbook gimmicks': Liberal leader vows to boost education, health-care funding

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/crombie-slams-fords-pocketbook-gimmicks/docview/3032922571/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crombie+slams+Ford%27s+%27pocketbook+gimmicks%27%3A+Liberal+leader+vows+to+boost+education%2C+health-care+funding&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-04-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Setting the stage for her plan to defeat Doug Ford in 2026, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie says the premier is jeopardizing jobs of the future with "reckless" underfinancing of colleges and universities.
"It is imperiling our economy," Crombie said Thursday in a 20-minute luncheon speech to the Empire Club, her first since taking the party helm in December.
"If I were premier today, I'd spend less time on pocketbook gimmicks and more time on the fundamental differentiator for our economy - education," she added in a shot at Ford measures, including a 5.7-cent-per-litre gasoline tax cut and scrapping of licence plate renewal fees.
Although Ford's Progressive Conservatives recently announced a $1-billion, three-year funding boost for post-secondary education, struggling colleges and universities warned it's not enough to put them on solid financial footing, given the government's extended tuition freeze, rising costs and a looming federal cap on international students.
Eight of Ontario's 23 universities are running deficits, and colleges are expected to lose $3 billion in revenue from international students over the next three years.
Colleges and Universities Minister Jill Dunlop has defended the $1-billion increase as the biggest "in over a decade" and noted tuition can be raised five per cent next fall for students from other provinces.
But Crombie said limiting revenue for colleges and universities will stifle their ability to research and innovate, and pointed to how University of Waterloo graduates helped spawn a "global revolution" with the BlackBerry smartphone two decades ago.
"Between 2025 and 2030, our economy will undergo a massive disruption. Technologies like (artificial intelligence) will continue to change how we live, work and learn. Retirements will soar, shrinking our workforce," she noted in a reference to baby boomers.
Crombie, whose party is a distant third in the legislature's standings with nine MPPs, did not say how much a Liberal government would boost post-secondary support other than to say "we need to fund them properly."
However, she noted in a question-and-answer session following her speech that "pocketbook gimmicks" like the gas tax break are "really reducing the fiscal capacity" of the provincial treasury.
"I keep thinking, would people really miss that or is that money that could be better invested in education and our health-care system?"
The gas tax cut, for example, costs $1.2 billion annually. Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy's budget last week included a $9.8-billion annual deficit.
Crombie, a former three-term mayor of Mississauga, said she passed on a chance to run in the upcoming May 2 byelection in nearby Milton to travel the province, meet voters and revive the Liberal party after stunning election defeats in 2018 and 2022.
"If a Mississauga seat comes open, we could have another conversation," she said.
CAPTION: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie says underfunding education is imperiling Ontario's economy.Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 217 of 500

Is this four-unit condo part of city's housing solution?: East York bungalow gives way to multiple homes, sold individually

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/is-this-four-unit-condo-part-citys-housing/docview/3032922467/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+this+four-unit+condo+part+of+city%27s+housing+solution%3F%3A+East+York+bungalow+gives+way+to+multiple+homes%2C+sold+individually&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-04-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On a tree-lined street a short walk from Pape Village, something new has risen from the footprint of a former bungalow. The property now hosts a four-unit condo multiplex, with each residence for sale separately.
From the street, a white and brown three-bedroom semi-detached home appears to float over the single parking space and shares a wall with the dark brick two-bedroom townhome. Nestled below is a one-bedroom basement suite, which looks out on a fenced-in yard adjoining the brown three-bedroom garden suite.
They present a modern façade, with large windows.
Together, the four newly built units form a condominium, all for sale. It's a style of multiplex new to Toronto, and industry insiders say it could spark an increase in similar projects springing up across the city.
Armen Krikorian, the Big City Realty broker for 319 Mortimer Ave., says the project was the result of a "perfect storm": the combination of the builder's unique vision and Toronto's need for more housing.
The builder acquired the property for $1.02 million in October 2020. It was originally home to one of the many bungalows that line the street. After some delays, which Krikorian attributed to the process to secure permits, the new homes were listed for sale last week.
"It hits every demographic and every family size," said Krikorian. "Young couples, downsizers in the area, people that want to be close to downtown but yet don't want to be uptown."
The semi and the townhome each sport balconies overlooking the fenced-in backyard. Inside, the units are bright and outfitted with new appliances, including a gas stove, with wooden finishes. Each unit has its own designated outdoor space divided by fencing, and although only the semi has a parking spot, the property is within walking distance to multiple transit lines and designated street parking.
The basement unit is listed for $499,900, while the townhouse is on the market for $999,900. The prices of the semi-detached unit, at $1,599,900, and garden suite, listed at $1,299,900, rival listings for detached homes in the neighbourhood.
"Not a lot of people are doing it, but I think as property values are still quite high, this is going to be an attractive new option for people to look at as a sort of a pathway to home ownership," said Sean Galbraith, president of Galbraith & Associates urban planning firm in Toronto. The smaller units, Galbraith said, could be particularly appealing for first-time homebuyers.
What stood out to Galbraith is that this project obtained city approval, as he said the city has largely kept a focus on garden suites and laneway homes as rental units, not as separate homes for sale.
"If this represents a shift in the city's thinking that can be applied elsewhere in the city, I think we're definitely going to see it lead to an increase in the number of garden suites and laneway suites that are built," said Galbraith, who has 20 years of experience in the industry, including as a senior planner for the Ministry of Municipal Affairs and Housing.
In an email Wednesday, city staff said this multiplex project fell within the existing zoning provisions for the property, and was approved in 2023 as part of the city's expanded "missing middle" housing push.
Multiplexes have been a point of political contention lately as the federal government has offered provinces access to billions in infrastructure funding so long as they require cities to allow fourplexes as of right. Ontario Premier Doug Ford, however, has said overriding municipalities to force fourplex permissions is "off the table" for the province. Instead, he says his government is focused on single-family homes and townhomes.
The City of Toronto has already taken the steps the federal government is trying to compel, legalizing up to four-unit homes citywide last July, with new rules that allow duplexes, triplexes and fourplexes as a right, though the new structures have to meet certain conditions around height and setbacks that depend on the neighbourhood they're in.
Krikorian said all of the Mortimer Avenue units are part of a condominium, with snow removal, landscaping, waste removal and other maintenance and insurance overseen by a property management company. Each unit pays condo fees - $394.20 for the townhouse, $230.15 for the basement, $633.08 for the semi-detached and $491.44 for the garden suite.
He said the homes' residents will make up the condo board and can decide to do away with the property management company's oversight - although given there are an even number of units, he was not able to confirm who would have ultimate say if the owners disagreed.
The builder, HMV Homes, had worked on similar projects in Quebec, Krikorian said. After a few years working in and studying the market in Toronto, HMV Homes decided to bring this style of building here.
Krikorian said the garden suite was the hardest to determine a listing price for, alongside the builder, as there were "zero comparable" homes for sale in the area - leaving them to look at detached homes and condos nearby to determine a price point.
There are perks to this type of project, Galbraith said, both for a builder who can see higher upfront earnings than if the units were listed as rentals, and for buyers who could find a street-level condo in a residential neighbourhood. But potential buyers in these situations should be people who are comfortable working alongside others on decisions related to their home.
"Communal living to some extent is not for everybody, but it's for a lot of people," he said.
After seeing this building land on the market, Galbraith expects to see a rising interest from other builders looking to replicate the project.
For him, it's already prompted discussion with colleagues about the potential opened up by this pioneering development.
"I've got a list of people that I'm going to be calling back and saying, 'Hey, when I told you that it wasn't likely before, well, things have changed. Let's have another conversation,'" he said.
CREDIT: Emily Fagan Toronto Star

Subject: Property management; Rentals; Neighborhoods; Condominiums; Real estate sales

Document 218 of 500

Three NDP MPs won't run again: Veteran maverick Angus is among those calling it quits

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/three-ndp-mps-wont-run-again/docview/3032921185/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Three+NDP+MPs+won%27t+run+again%3A+Veteran+maverick+Angus+is+among+those+calling+it+quits&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-04-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Three New Democrats, including one of the party's longest-serving MPs, have announced they won't be running for re-election in battleground regions where Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives hope to make gains.
Charlie Angus, who has represented Timmins-James Bay for two decades, said Thursday he has decided not to seek re-election in the northern Ontario riding that is of particular interest to Poilievre.
Following Angus's announcement, the NDP said British Columbia MP Rachel Blaney, who has fielded stiff competition from the Conservatives in her riding of North Island-Powell River, and Carol Hughes, who has held Angus's neighbouring riding of Algoma-Manitoulin-Kapuskasing since 2008, had also decided not to run again.
"On behalf of all New Democrats, I want to thank Rachel, Charlie and Carol for their years of advocacy and dedication to serving working people," NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh said in a written statement.
"They've all accomplished so much for Canadians and worked tirelessly to make our country a fairer, better place. They've added so much to our party and, while we'll miss their contributions around the caucus table, I want to wish my friends the very best when each of them begins their next chapter."
The departures are likely to put pressure on a party that is fighting to expand its electoral footprint at a time when the NDP is grappling with other caucus exits, its ties to an increasingly unpopular Liberal government, and a Conservative party eager to boost their seat count at the New Democrats' expense.
Thursday's announcements mean six MPs out of the NDP's 25-member caucus will have vacated their seats by the time Canadians next head to the polls. Blaney, who was first elected in 2015, is the third NDP MP in B.C. who has said they won't run again. She joins fellow longtime MPs Randall Garrison and Richard Cannings, the latter of whom also represents a blue-orange battleground.
Manitoba MP Daniel Blaikie left federal politics at the end of March to work for Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew.
Angus told the Star it was time for new blood to revitalize the party.
"We've seen great MPs step aside in the past and I didn't know how we were going to carry on, and then other great MPs step up," said Angus, who has garnered a reputation as a scrappy and outspoken New Democrat who once ran against Singh for leadership of the NDP.
"Every caucus needs renewal, and I think renewal is happening."
As the party's natural resources critic, Angus worked closely with his government counterpart Jonathan Wilkinson on Ottawa's sustainable jobs bill as part of the Liberal-NDP parliamentary alliance.
"I will continue to find ways to advocate for Indigenous and Northern issues. The climate crisis is an all-hands-on-deck moment. I started in grassroots activism and am excited to return to those roots. And yes, there will be more gigs, more books, more historical projects, and more s--t disturbing," Angus wrote on Facebook.
Angus partly pinned his decision on significant boundary changes to Canada's ridings that came into effect this month.
"The Federal Electoral Boundary Commission has ruled that this constituency, already bigger than the U.K., will undergo dramatic changes. The new riding of Mushkegowuk-Kapuskasing-Timmins will be even larger, with the addition of many new communities spread out over an additional 20,000 square kilometres of territory," Angus wrote in his post.
That redrawing of the electoral map also means Hughes's riding is set to be dissolved.
The departure of both Angus and Hughes creates a timely opening for the Conservatives, who have the region - an NDP stronghold - in their sights.
Poilievre has visited the area on at least four occasions since announcing his intent to run for the Conservative leadership in 2022, often hammering his party's tough-on-crime message to a region wrestling with public safety concerns.
CAPTION: NDP MP Charlie Angus, who has represented the Timmins- James Bay riding for two decades, said Thursday he won't seek re-election in the northern Ontario riding that has been targeted by Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre.
CREDIT: Raisa Patel Toronto Star

Document 219 of 500

Ethics watchdog clears Doug Ford's chief of staff after he 'overlooked' Greenbelt email

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ethics-watchdog-clears-doug-fords-chief-staff/docview/3033624959/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ethics+watchdog+clears+Doug+Ford%27s+chief+of+staff+after+he+%27overlooked%27+Greenbelt+email&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's ethics watchdog has cleared Premier Doug Ford's chief of staff of wrongdoing after the discovery of an "overlooked" email linked to the $8.28-billion Greenbelt scandal.
In response to a complaint from NDP Leader Marit Stiles, integrity commissioner J. David Wake said he has "accepted" Patrick Sackville's explanation of how his Oct. 17, 2022 email exchange - on private accounts instead of government servers - with Ryan Amato, at the time chief of staff to then-municipal affairs minister Steve Clark, was missed.
"I have concluded that the Oct. 17 email sent from Mr. Amato to Mr. Sackville's personal email in the circumstances I have outlined does not support a finding that Patrick Sackville knew about the specific removal criteria for lands from the Greenbelt or the properties selected for removal until he was briefed with others, as he testified, on Oct. 27," Wake wrote Thursday.
While the integrity commissioner said "it is unfortunate that this email was not retrieved in accordance with the request for documents I made to Mr. Sackville ... I have accepted his explanation as to how it was overlooked."
Ford's chief of staff told Wake it "was an oversight on my part" that the missive fell through the cracks because it was to his personal account rather than his government email.
"When you previously sought documents relevant to the issues you are investigating, I conducted a diligent search of my records that would be responsive to your request. However, I did not locate the email from Mr. Amato in making that search," said Sackville.
"In conducting some due diligence related to freedom of information requests, I later became aware of this email and, consistent with my obligations, provided it to you expeditiously," he said, referring to the message being found last December.
"I do not conduct government business on my personal email, and would not have expected or anticipated such an email."
Wake said "it is entirely plausible that he would have given primary attention to the hundreds of daily emails he receives to his government email address and that he may have neglected to be attentive to his personal emails."
The opposition New Democrats, who obtained the documents through a freedom of information request, argued the email contradicted testimony given under oath to the integrity commissioner last year.
In the message from Amato to Sackville, the ministerial chief of staff shared a "list of criteria for removals" of 7,400 acres from the two-million-acre environmentally sensitive Greenbelt around the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area.
These include "infrastructure services," such as proximity to roads, gas and hydro lines and water mains, police and fire services.
The email also listed "potential offsets" such as the Paris Galt Moraine, "green space our government has decided not to move on" as well as "options to go larger depending on executive interest."
At issue is the timing of the email, which is at odds with testimony made under oath to Wake for his Aug. 30 report on the Greenbelt affair.
"Members of the premier's staff also told us they were not involved in the selection of properties," the integrity commissioner wrote last summer.
"Mr. Sackville said he did not discuss specific properties to be removed or removal criteria with Mr. Amato until the briefing that occurred on Oct. 27, 2022," he noted at the time.
"Mr. Sackville recalled being first briefed about the Greenbelt project at a meeting facilitated by the cabinet office on Oct. 27, 2022. He told me that he recalled the intentions of the policy were discussed and that it should be 'programmatic,' meaning that it should be something that could be repeated and made standard."
However, that was 10 days after the Oct. 17 email in which criteria for removal of the lands from the Greenbelt were addressed. The changes were finally announced publicly on Nov. 4, 2022.
Sackville declined to comment when contacted by the Star on Thursday, as did Amato.
Ford has maintained he knew nothing about the removal of 15 parcels of land from the Greenbelt, which were estimated to be worth $8.28 billion to property owners.
In separate reports last summer, the auditor general and integrity commissioner found certain developers with Tory connections were "favoured."
Under pressure, Ford cancelled the land swap on Sept. 21. The deal is now being investigated by the RCMP.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks at the Ottawa Board of Trade on March 28, 2024.

Subject: Electronic mail systems; Water mains; Freedom of information

Bonnie Crombie slams Doug Ford for 'reckless' underfunding of colleges and universities

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bonnie-crombie-slams-doug-ford-reckless/docview/3033624393/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bonnie+Crombie+slams+Doug+Ford+for+%27reckless%27+underfunding+of+colleges+and+universities&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Setting the stage for her plan to defeat Doug Ford in 2026, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie says the premier is jeopardizing jobs of the future with "reckless" underfinancing of colleges and universities.
"It is imperiling our economy," Crombie said Thursday in a 20-minute luncheon speech to the Empire Club, her first since taking the party helm in December.
"If I were premier today, I'd spend less time on pocketbook gimmicks and more time on the fundamental differentiator for our economy - education," she added in a shot at Ford measures including a 5.7-cent-per-litre gasoline tax cut and scrapping of license plate renewal fees.
While Ford's Progressive Conservatives recently announced a $1-billion, three-year funding boost for post-secondary education, struggling colleges and universities warned it's not enough to put them on solid financial footing given the government's extended tuition freeze, rising costs and a looming federal cap on international students.
Eight of Ontario's 23 universities are running deficits, and colleges are expected to lose $3 billion in revenue from international students over the next three years.
Colleges and Universities Minister Jill Dunlop has defended the $1-billion increase as the biggest "in over a decade" and noted tuition can be raised five per cent next fall for students from other provinces.
But Crombie said limiting revenue for colleges and universities will stifle their ability to research and innovate, and pointed to how University of Waterloo graduates helped spawn a "global revolution" with the BlackBerry smartphone two decades ago.
"Between 2025 and 2030, our economy will undergo a massive disruption. Technologies like (artificial intelligence) will continue to change how we live, work and learn. Retirements will soar, shrinking our workforce," she noted in a reference to baby boomers.
Crombie, whose party is a distant third in the legislature's standings with nine MPPs, did not say how much a Liberal government would boost post-secondary support other than to say "we need to fund them properly."
However, she noted in a question-and-answer session following her speech that "pocketbook gimmicks" like the gas tax break are "really reducing the fiscal capacity" of the provincial treasury.
"I keep thinking, would people really miss that or is that money that could be better invested in education and our health-care system?"
The gas tax cut, for example, costs $1.2 billion annually. Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy's budget last week included a $9.8-billion annual deficit.
Crombie, a former three-term mayor of Mississauga, said she passed on a chance to run in the upcoming May 2 byelection in nearby Milton to travel the province, meet voters and revive the Liberal party after stunning election defeats in 2018 and 2022.
"If a Mississauga seat comes open, we could have another conversation," she said.
CAPTION: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie speaks to reporters at Queen's Park in Toronto on March 26, 2024.

Subject: Tuition; Foreign students; Baby boomers; Tax cuts; Colleges & universities; Gasoline taxes; Education

Man accused in Dominica killings of Canadian millionaire breaks his silence + Toronto homeowners struck with big vacant home tax

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/man-accused-dominica-killings-canadian/docview/3031740145/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Man+accused+in+Dominica+killings+of+Canadian+millionaire+breaks+his+silence+%2B+Toronto+homeowners+struck+with+big+vacant+home+tax&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Thursday, April 4 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on the case of a Canadian killed in Dominica, the demise of Ontario's anti-panhandling law and homeowners erroneously slapped with Toronto's vacant home tax.
DON'T MISSThe man accused of killing a Canadian millionaire in Dominica broke his silence
The American chocolatier accused in a Caribbean court of the brutal killings of Canadian entrepreneur Daniel Langlois and his partner Dominique Marchand says he's been unfairly portrayed by the avalanche of international news coverage on the deaths. Speaking for the first time from Dominica State Prison, through his lawyers and a public relations firm, Jonathan Lehrer told the Star's Kenyon Wallace and Sheila Wang he couldn't comment on the allegations against him. Instead, Lehrer painted an idyllic tale of how his family moved to the island of Dominica and started their successful chocolate plantation: "It is our home and our love." Check out the details.
Miss something? Canadian philanthropist and film pioneer Langlois and his partner were found dead in a bullet-riddled, burnt-out SUV on the Caribbean island last December. The same day, police arrested Lehrer and mechanic Robert Snyder Jr.Context: For years before the killings, Langlois and Lehrer were locked in a bitter dispute over the usage of a public road linking their neighbouring properties. Locals tell the Star their rivalry could have played a role in the deaths.A court has struck down most of Ontario's anti-panhandling law
A Toronto judge has struck down most of Ontario's bans on panhandling in public spaces - like on transit or near ATMs - as unconstitutional, Jacques Gallant reports. The decision by Superior Court Justice Robert Centa largely guts the Safe Streets Act, introduced in 1999 by the Progressive Conservative government of Mike Harris, to crack down on panhandling. Centa found Ontario's ban on squeegeeing and panhandling in roadways - the original impetus for the act - should be upheld. But all other prohibitions on soliciting donations in public were struck down, including from people near public toilets, payphones, ATMs, taxi stands and public transit stops. Here's how you could be affected.
Word from the courts: "The mere presence of a homeless person soliciting gifts from persons doing certain things at a prohibited site does not, on its own and without more, pose any danger or impediment to the safe use of public space," Centa wrote in his 91-page decision.Background: The constitutional challenge before Centa was first launched in 2017 by legal clinic Fair Change, which argued the act targets the most vulnerable members of society. "It increases their marginalization and aggravates their negative physical and psychological health outcomes," the clinic said.Homeowners who forgot to claim occupancy have been hit with a big vacancy tax bill
Hasan Mahbub was "shocked" to wake up to a $4,000 tax bill from Toronto for owning a "vacant home" - the one he currently lives in. "I was panicked, I was angry," he told the Star's David Rider. Mahbub was among the 125,000 homeowners who received a demand to pay the city's vacant home tax, despite many of them occupying their residences - including some who declared their residency before the March 15 deadline. Mayor Olivia Chow told the Star she's hearing the complaints. Her team is following up with a notice, in multiple languages, telling residents who occupy their homes "do not pay." What's going on?
Word from the city: Chow told the Star the city's vacant home tax notification and billing system "needs to be completely revamped," vowing: "We'll clean it up."What we know: If you've been slapped with the vacant home tax despite occupying your property, you can dispute the charges through the city's online portal - although you'll still face a $21.24 late fee.WHAT ELSE

Northrup was not hit head-on, contradicting plainclothes officers.Demand for the GED has "skyrocketed" in Ontario. Document 222 of 500

Three NDP MPs say they won't seek re-election - creating openings for Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/three-ndp-mps-say-they-wont-seek-re-election/docview/3031739600/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Three+NDP+MPs+say+they+won%27t+seek+re-election+-+creating+openings+for+Pierre+Poilievre%27s+Conservatives&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Three New Democrats, including one of the party's longest-serving MPs, have announced they won't be running for re-election in battleground regions where Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives hope to make gains.Charlie Angus, who has represented Timmins-James Bay for two decades, said Thursday he has decided not to seek re-election in the northern Ontario riding that is of particular interest to Poilievre.Following Angus's announcement, the NDP said British Columbia MP Rachel Blaney, who has fielded stiff competition from the Conservatives in her riding of North Island-Powell River, and Carol Hughes, who has held Angus's neighbouring riding of Algoma-Manitoulin-Kapuskasing since 2008, had also decided not to run again."On behalf of all New Democrats, I want to thank Rachel, Charlie and Carol for their years of advocacy and dedication to serving working people," NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh said in a written statement."They've all accomplished so much for Canadians and worked tirelessly to make our country a fairer, better place. They've added so much to our party and, while we'll miss their contributions around the caucus table, I want to wish my friends the very best when each of them begins their next chapter."The departures are likely to put pressure on a party that is fighting to expand its electoral footprint at a time when the NDP is grappling with other caucus exits, its ties to an increasingly unpopular Liberal government, and a Conservative party eager to boost their seat count at the New Democrats' expense. Thursday's announcements mean six MPs out of the NDP's 25-member caucus will have vacated their seats by the time Canadians next head to the polls. Blaney, who was first elected in 2015, is the third NDP MP in B.C. who has said they won't run again. She joins fellow longtime MPs Randall Garrison and Richard Cannings, the latter of whom also represents a blue-orange battleground.Manitoba MP Daniel Blaikie left federal politics at the end of March to work for Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew.Angus told the Star it was time for new blood to revitalize the party."We've seen great MPs step aside in the past and I didn't know how we were going to carry on, and then other great MPs step up," said Angus, who has garnered a reputation as a scrappy and outspoken New Democrat who once ran against Singh for leadership of the NDP. "Every caucus needs renewal, and I think renewal is happening."As the party's natural resources critic, Angus worked closely with his government counterpart Jonathan Wilkinson on Ottawa's sustainable jobs bill as part of the Liberal-NDP parliamentary alliance."I will continue to find ways to advocate for Indigenous and Northern issues. The climate crisis is an all-hands-on-deck moment. I started in grassroots activism and am excited to return to those roots. And yes, there will be more gigs, more books, more historical projects, and more s--t disturbing," Angus wrote on Facebook.Angus partly pinned his decision on significant boundary changes to Canada's ridings that came into effect this month."The Federal Electoral Boundary Commission has ruled that this constituency, already bigger than the U.K., will undergo dramatic changes. The new riding of Mushkegowuk-Kapuskasing-Timmins will be even larger, with the addition of many new communities spread out over an additional 20,000 square kilometres of territory," Angus wrote in his post.That redrawing of the electoral map also means Hughes's riding is set to be dissolved. The departure of both Angus and Hughes creates a timely opening for the Conservatives, who have the region - an NDP stronghold - in their sights. Poilievre has visited the area on at least four occasions since announcing his intent to run for the Conservative leadership in 2022, often hammering his party's tough-on-crime message to a region wrestling with public safety concerns.For his part, Angus said he feels the NDP's northern Ontario base is thriving and secure, but conceded Thursday the coming fight will be a "battle." He acknowledged that Poilievre is "playing to a rage machine" - a small group of vocal voters he says are receptive to the Conservative leader's rhetoric.Poilievre on Thursday accused the trio of MPs on X, formerly Twitter, of "jumping off Jagmeet's ship before going down in defeat." Hughes, who is also the House of Commons assistant deputy speaker, has criticized the riding changes in Ontario's north, arguing that a large swath of constituents will be underserved with only one MP to represent them. Hughes said in a written statement that she was proud of her tenure and looked forward to "helping elect that next generation of progressive MPs."Blaney, the NDP's whip and critic for veterans affairs, seniors and rural economic development, said in her own statement that the decision not to seek re-election was "incredibly difficult."All three MPs said they were keen to spend more time with their families after spending years toiling on Parliament Hill.
CAPTION: NDP MP Charlie Angus attends a news conference in Ottawa on Oct. 17, 2023.

Subject: Conservatism; Leadership; Grass roots movement

Long-term-care money just another example of collusion and abuse by the Ford government

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/long-term-care-money-just-another-example/docview/3031739462/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Long-term-care+money+just+another+example+of+collusion+and+abuse+by+the+Ford+government&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Why is the Ford government giving even more money to long-term-care operators facing lawsuits for negligence? April 3
Armine Yalnizyan's article epitomizes why we need independent journalists and news sources. We are now the subjects (not the constituents) of a government that is increasingly restricting access to information about how they are actually running this province.
We spend a lot of time watching our neighbours to the south, but we don't seem to learn much. The privatization of their health-care system is a disaster with most Americans one illness away from bankruptcy. This is also the case for visitors who unknowingly find themselves at the mercy of this rapacious system.
Ford's government is using every effort to privatize our health-care system and now they are in league with the worst of the worst, private equity groups who are moving to control the long-term-care sector which places our most vulnerable in great danger.
This is not a real estate "play," people's lives are at risk.
The pandemic brought this danger into clear focus but as Yalnizyan points out, the resulting class-action suits have not deterred these operators or our current government from charging ahead with more support and investment.
Without voices such as hers and the other hardworking journalists and contributors in the public media, this collusion and abuse would be out of sight, right where this government and their cronies want it.
Peter Bradley, Mississauga
Another Greenbelt fiasco?
Once again the Ford conservatives sell out the citizens of the province for profit.Hopefully, the result will unfold as did their Greenbelt fiasco.
Dale Nicol, Trenton
Fourplexes are practical, affordable and overwhelmingly successfulDoug Ford's housing plan is unravelling - and he is the reason why, March 30
Doug Ford's fourplex fumble was a dismal moment for anyone who cares about affordable housing, March 30

As a former director of engineering for the city of Brampton, I am very familiar with the fourplex model and agree that it provides a valuable addition to the affordable housing category. Fourplexes are practical, affordable and overwhelmingly successful. The fourplex was introduced in Brampton in the mid 1990s and were designed for each unit to have its own private access and blended in quite nicely with the neighbourhood. See the Brampton Brick subdivision at Hwy. 10 and Bovaird Drive on the south-east corner. This model has proliferated in other parts of the GTA such as Waterdown and is quite viable, attractive and popular. Fourplexes economize on service connections and provide four private affordable living spaces. Why in the world would our Premier all of a sudden take exception to them I don't know. I wonder who is giving the Premier this errant advice?
Richard Bino, Singhampton, ON
A fourplex made it possible for my family to own a home
It doesn't seem like Premier Doug Ford has provided a valid reason for opposing the construction of fourplexes. Perhaps my families' experience will persuade Ford that fourplexes are a good way to expand the housing inventory but also help families to own a home. In the mid 1960s, my father was the sole worker in a family of seven and on his income it was virtually impossible to feed his family and purchase a home. But my parents had the wisdom and opportunity to purchase an existing fourplex and we lived in one apartment and rented the other three. On this basis we moved forward until approximately 10 years later my parents had fully paid the mortgage on the fourplex. The neighborhood still exists. Ford can go see it for himself at Northcliffe Boulevard and Rogers Road. It's still a perfectly good family neighborhood.
Charles Campisi, Oakville
The deeper benefit of getting cell phones out of the classroom
'Nonsense': Doug Ford tells Ontario school boards to 'focus on the kids' - not sue Snapchat, TikTok or Meta,March 28
It's more than just the phones. It's about the culture of instant gratification and urgent immediacy. It's about slowing down and focusing on one thing at a time. This contrived urgency plays a role in the ever-increasing anxiety that affects children. And of course, it's about giving full attention to what might be the single most important achievement in one's life.
It's a small but important step. Letting them discover that a text or message does not need an immediate reply. Getting them to understand "What's the worst that could happen?" Teaching them that the problem lies with the person who expects an immediate response.
And of course, real emergencies can be dealt with the old-fashioned way. Call the school and actually talk to someone.
Don Mustill, Niagara on the Lake
Social media is interfering with the kids' ability to focus and learn
The addiction to social media is interfering with the kids' ability to focus and learn in the classroom. It also interferes with their ability to relate comfortably to their classmates. That is concerning to both parents and teachers. Hopefully this government will soon recognize the importance of that harm to the kids.
Bonnie Bacvar, Toronto
The cost of social media's harms is bearing its fruit
Doug Ford calls the four school boards lawsuit against social media companies nonsense and schools should focus on the kids. If the students were focused on learning in class instead of looking at their phones there wouldn't be a lawsuit. Over the last 20 years or so our politicians have failed the taxpayer once again by not paying attention and regulating social media giants. They have been allowed to create algorithms that are designed to addict students/people. The cost of this addiction to our society is now bearing its fruit.I applaud the school boards for taking this initiative to the courts and publicly taking on the social media giants.
Mike Walker, Washago, ON
Doctors try, but daily the system sets us all up to fail Cutting ER wait times? There's an app for that, March 30
We commend the Star editorial board and its health reporters for their continued and necessary focus on the issue of prolonged waits for emergency care in Ontario, be they from delayed ambulances, crowded hospitals or closed rural departments. They are dangerous, unacceptable and unnecessary. We particularly appreciate the well-deserved acknowledgment of Ontario's emergency physicians (and our nursing colleagues) for an unfaltering willingness and determination to innovate in order to provide the best possible care in a system that daily sets us all up to fail.
But here's the thing. We cannot accept or congratulate the normalization of the perversity of a health-care system that in one's worst moments makes you wait, wonder and worry. There can be no acceptable substitute for care delivered at the bedside by a well-trained, empathetic, maximally supported emergency physician, reading your body language and providing a direct human connection. Ontarians deserve so much better.
Alan Drummond, Canadian Association of Emergency Physicians, Ottawa
