Document 412 of 500

Pierre Poilievre will undo some of Justin Trudeau's legacy if he becomes prime minister. Here's what he'd target

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievre-will-undo-some-justin-trudeaus/docview/2957974144/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre+will+undo+some+of+Justin+Trudeau%27s+legacy+if+he+becomes+prime+minister.+Here%27s+what+he%27d+target&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Prime ministers always say they leave their political legacies to historians. But it's their successors who largely write that history by deciding what parts of any governing agenda survive.
So what should we glean from the reflections about Brian Mulroney's legacy? His successors left most of the former Progressive Conservative leader's important accomplishments intact, even those they once adamantly opposed.
And what might it tell us about which parts of Justin Trudeau's agenda will survive, or what lessons Pierre Poilievre has taken?
Plenty.
In Mulroney's case, his big successes and big failures shifted the national conversation and charted paths for those who followed him.
Liberal Jean Chrétien signed onto Mulroney's North American Free Trade Agreement and did not replace the GST despite campaigning against both. In Mulroney's two failed efforts to rewrite the Canadian Constitution, his successors found inspiration for how to try to politically accommodate Quebec's unique place in Canada. Quebec's demands to opt out of federal spending programs with full compensation go unchallenged now by any federal leader. And Stephen Harper, despite roots in a Reform party that fiercely opposed Mulroney's "distinct society" status for Quebec, passed a motion recognizing "the Quebecois form a nation within a united Canada."
We can already glimpse how much of Trudeau's time in government will survive a change if, as polls indicate now, the Conservatives led by Poilievre win the next election, scheduled for 2025.
In an interview with the Star's Susan Delacourt in Guelph, Trudeau would not be drawn into talk of his legacy because he insists he's not going anywhere.
Trudeau did, however, talk about what he thinks will not be undone.
"The Canada Child Benefit can't be rolled back; it lifted 400,000 kids out of poverty over the past few years," he said.
"Child care - we got Alberta, Manitoba, and Saskatchewan to sign on. And regardless of the fact that the Conservative party ran against it in the last election, and wouldn't have done it if they'd gotten elected, now that it's in place, and once it will be in place for a year or two, I don't think that'll be reversible."
Beyond that, Trudeau said, he believes some of his government's moves on reconciliation have "moved the floor to a much higher level that you can never go back. ... You'll have governments that could go far slower than we have and be less enthusiastic and supportive on it, but I think we have seen a step change on that."
The same is true of climate change, Trudeau said.
In just a half dozen years, he says the public debate has profoundly shifted. Canadians talk of climate change now in terms of how quickly or how much they need to do, rather than whether they should do anything at all. "That's something that wasn't obvious before, but is now irrevocable."
Some of that seems true.
The Conservatives under Harper brought in a child-care benefit which Trudeau overhauled to include a number of other support measures, leading to a payment for families that experts say has been transformative. The Poilievre Conservatives, with their focus on ordinary Canadians, are unlikely to kill it.
When it comes to child care, Poilievre may leave much of the Trudeau program intact. Why? Because after Harper killed the first iteration of a Liberal national child care program - the $5-billion plan prime minister Paul Martin signed with provinces - the Conservatives' effort (which amounted to $100 monthly cheques to parents and tax incentives that failed to create the promised daycare spaces) was widely panned.
Poilievre's Conservatives already voted to pass Trudeau's child-care legislation in the House of Commons, despite protesting it did not help private at-home daycare providers, or shift workers whose daycare needs don't match public non-profit daycare centre hours. If anything, the Conservatives are likely to face pressure to live up to their rhetoric to help those parents.
Poilievre has also begun to lay out his objectives on Indigenous reconciliation, chiefly framing it as encouraging Indigenous communities to take a key role in and reap the benefits of developing natural resources on their lands.
In his interview with the Star, Trudeau did not cite his reform to the way senators are appointed. The prime minister now draws from an application list vetted by an independent advisory committee, a process the Conservatives deride as partisan. He did not say that gender equity in cabinet, or on the country's top court, is the new normal. He did not mention dental care or the as-yet unfinished national pharmacare project as irreversible.
Some of that may be undone.
If Poilievre has eyes on his own legacy, his takeaway from Mulroney's record might be: do big things, and do them fast.
It's a parlour game for Ottawa's chattering classes to guess what Poilievre would do beyond his slogans of "axe the tax, fix the budget, build the houses and stop the crime."
Guess no more.
Poilievre makes no bones about his intention to swiftly kill the carbon levy on consumers, to shut down safe injection sites, to ramp up the number of mandatory minimum prison sentences for drug, gun and violent crimes, and to slash funding for the CBC.
Speaking to the Greater Vancouver Board of Trade recently, he gave the clearest indication yet of how he intends to take bold steps and damn the critics, and that he learned from Mulroney's legacy.
While "everyone praises him now," Poilievre said, Mulroney carved out an agenda that was "very controversial."
"He had to take on a lot of entrenched interests in order to achieve the, frankly, the revolution that he brought at the time: privatizing Air Canada and numerous other Crown corporations that had been very entrenched."
Poilievre admired Mulroney for "reducing the cost of government," saying that during his tenure as prime minister, Canada had "among the slowest ... spending growth of any government in history." Mulroney "balanced the operational budget. We had an ongoing deficit because of the interest on the preceding Trudeau government's debt."
Poilievre gave only passing nod to Mulroney nailing a free trade deal with the United States, the legacy centrepiece Mulroney's critics now hail, before praising at length Mulroney's role in setting a clear inflation target for monetary policy, the purview of the central bank.
"Up until then, the Bank of Canada had no mandate of any clear purpose, so they just kept printing money and leading to double-digit inflation. He brought in a two per cent inflation target," Poilievre said.
Hammering the Bank of Canada was an early and ongoing stock phrase in Poilievre's speeches. He claims the central bank enabled the Liberal government's deficit-spending through the pandemic. That, he has said, caused high inflation, which in turn led to higher interest rates that are crushing Canadians. Poilievre in the past promised to fire Bank of Canada governor Tiff Macklem, although he has not reiterated that recently. And since Macklem has taken to lamenting the pace of federal government spending, perhaps it's Trudeau who now wishes the central bank governor would get a new job.
Moving to Mulroney's international legacy, Poilievre - whose support for Ukraine has been challenged by Liberals - praised him as "firmly on the side of freedom against Soviet communism, which was a change from the previous government." And he noted Mulroney "was on the side of freedom in South Africa against apartheid, which was a lonely position for him to take in the western world at the time, but obviously he has been proven right.
"So the lesson I take from that is that he was prepared to stand alone in order to stand for what was right, and that it wasn't easy in the short run," Poilievre said, "but in the long run, he has been vindicated."
It's not clear yet what that means for Poilievre's military and foreign policy. Will he undo or bolster the Liberals' long-term security guarantees for Ukraine's fight against Russia? Will he dial back Canadian troops in Latvia until Canada's budget is on stronger footing? He says he's prepared to cut "wasteful" foreign aid, and money for the United Nations' relief agency in Gaza.
In Vancouver, Poilievre hailed Mulroney's "balanced budgets, free enterprise, privatization, better support for our resource sector," and pivoted to his now-everyday slogan, painting the former prime minister as the standard bearer for a "common-sense consensus" which he said had "unanimous" support among Conservatives, New Democrats and Liberals until Trudeau came to power in 2015.
Does it amount to a governing agenda? Possibly. Does it amount to a legacy? Poilievre's own successors will decide.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks to reporters in the foyer of the House of Commons on Feb. 29, 2024.

Document 413 of 500

The carbon levy will haunt Justin Trudeau whether he raises it or not

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/carbon-levy-will-haunt-justin-trudeau-whether-he/docview/2957974128/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+carbon+levy+will+haunt+Justin+Trudeau+whether+he+raises+it+or+not&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau acknowledged this week that he's no longer popular. Hearkening back to similar comments by former prime minister Brian Mulroney, whose funeral will be held Saturday, Trudeau said, "My job is not to be popular. ... My job is to do the right things for Canada now, and do the right things for Canadians a generation from now."
Friends and foes suggest a solution to Trudeau's popularity problem - one that may lead the Liberals towards an existential crisis in the next election - is a pause to the carbon price increase coming to Canadians' wallets on April 1.
But Trudeau has much more to lose if he amends his government's signature climate policy than if he stands his ground.
Next month, the levy will rise by 23 per cent, from $65 a tonne to $80. The renamed Canada Carbon Rebates - those quarterly payments Canadians receive to offset the carbon price - will rise to reflect the change. But everyday items will also be more expensive: gasoline for your vehicle, the cost to heat your home, food at the grocery store, airplane tickets for your summer vacation.
With Canadians feeling squeezed from inflation and higher interest rates, it's no surprise that provincial premiers - those who, unlike their peers in Quebec and British Columbia, chose not to enact their own carbon pricing regimes and rely on the federal backstop, making price hikes Ottawa's problems and not their own - now want Trudeau to pause the plan.
On Monday, Newfoundland and Labrador Premier Andrew Furey, a fellow Liberal, penned a letter politely requesting Trudeau pause the increase, "at least until inflation stabilizes, interest rates lower and related economic pressures on the cost of living sufficiently cool."
On Tuesday, New Brunswick Premier Blaine Higgs called on Trudeau to "immediately cancel his punishing April 1st carbon tax hike. Even better, cancel the carbon tax altogether."
Later that evening, Nova Scotia Premier Tim Houston wrote his own letter to the prime minister, noting the increase will add 3.3 cents per litre of gas and asking that Trudeau "cancel the carbon tax before any more financial damage is done."
On Wednesday, Ontario Premier Doug Ford told reporters he doesn't understand what the federal Liberals are thinking. "If they don't start ... putting money back into people's pockets instead of filling their pockets, guess what, they're going to get annihilated."
On Thursday, Alberta Premier Danielle Smith noted seven premiers are now united in demanding Ottawa reverse course.
On Friday, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, in New Brunswick for a "spike the hike" rally, called on the prime minister to cancel his "heartless and cruel ... tax hike."
But Trudeau and federal Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault appear unmoved.
They argue their plan is an affordability measure; that it returns more money into most people's pockets; that without the carbon price and the carbon price rebates, more people would struggle to make ends meet.
But as Ford's comments reflect, many Canadians - including the premier - don't understand how the federal program works. It's designed to make things more expensive, so you're pushed to find alternatives and adopt new habits - things like installing a heat pump, buying a hybrid or electric vehicle, or biking to work.
The program was always designed to ensure most Canadians weren't financially disadvantaged, with a significant portion of the levy collected returned to residents. A family of four in urban Ontario, for example, will receive $1,120 this year; in Alberta it's $1,800. But, as Guilbeault acknowledges, too little attention was placed on the second part of the Grits' plan.
Carbon pricing was always going to be a tough sell because it is visible. That is both its strength and its vulnerability.
It may also be the Liberals' strength and vulnerability.
Last fall, after intense lobbying from his Atlantic MPs, Trudeau announced a nationwide pause of the carbon levy on home heating oil for three years, a federal-provincial plan to rebate the costs of installing heat pumps, and a doubling of the rural climate rebate top-up. There was little impact at the polls. It may have stopped Liberal hemorrhaging in the Atlantic region, but it did not reverse the slide. Abacus Data had the Liberals polling at 32 per cent support in September compared to 43 per cent for the Conservatives. This month, the Grits are at 33 per cent, and the Tories are at 48 per cent in Atlantic Canada (with a comparable margin of error of plus or minus 2.53 per cent, 19 times out of 20.)
Why would the Liberals think any new pause to the carbon pricing regime will lead to an uptick in support for them? It may instead lead to more bleeding as climate-conscious voters decide Trudeau is willing to sacrifice the predictability of his plan for short-term political gain. That would be the quickest path to Liberal irrelevance.
With a few percentage points shaved off of Liberal support - as Abacus CEO David Coletto notes happened in November, following the heating oil announcement - the Grits could be battling the NDP for second-place territory. And that, notes Coletto, would be a death blow to the Liberals.
It's one more reason Poilievre and his allies may be putting so much pressure on Trudeau to "spike the hike."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau speaks to the media following a meeting with Quebec Premier Francois Legault in Montreal on March 15, 2024.

Subject: Rebates; Canadians; Interest rates; Environmental policy; Carbon; Prime ministers; Tax increases; Environmental tax

Document 414 of 500

An expected defeat was unexpectedly painful for Liberals

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/expected-defeat-was-unexpectedly-painful-liberals/docview/2957844473/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=An+expected+defeat+was+unexpectedly+painful+for+Liberals&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: MG I know better than to make predictions in politics. I'm not paid enough to risk that kind of embarrassment, and we live in such bizarrely turbulent times that I'm happy if any given day goes roughly as I expected. But I can't help but note that the Durham byelection result earlier this month is bad news for the Liberals.
I'm mostly firmly in the "byelections don't matter" camp, but this specific byelection isn't just sending back a new Tory to replace another Tory in a safely CPC seat. The vote totals in this election are a real-world validation of months of polling that is just brutal for PM Trudeau and his government. I'll agree to all the usual caveats about campaigns mattering and there being a lot of time left, but I don't see how anyone at Liberal HQ who was trying to hold onto much hope is going to be able to do that after Durham. The result doesn't matter, but getting confirmation that the polls are broadly accurate should.
SD We promised to disagree last time we wrote, so let me get my two points of agreement out of the way quickly. Yes, that was a brutal byelection for the Liberals and I, too, am cautious about reading too much into the results of them. You are correct, too - this confirms what all those polls have been fairly screaming to the Liberals over this past year. Here is where we may disagree, though: I still think there's a path for the Liberals to recover before the next election, though it's a faint and narrow one.
MG I mean, I'll let you make the case, but I don't reject the notion entirely. I think we're just getting into the kind of territory where the only man who can stop Pierre Poilievre is Pierre Poilievre, and he may well try. What's your sense of the path?
SD First, the path comes from taking the Durham byelection seriously - as seriously as Stephen Harper did way back in 2003 after a byelection in the southwestern Ontario riding of Perth-Middlesex. It was the disastrous showing for the Canadian Alliance in Perth-Middlesex that convinced Harper it was time to get serious about uniting the right in Canada.
Now, I'm not saying Justin Trudeau needs to go out and merge with the New Democrats (some say that's already happened). But I am saying the progressive centre in Canadian politics is as fragmented as the conservative centre was in the early 2000s and Liberals seem to be losing their hold on the mainstream middle of the road.
MG This won't be much of a reply. It's the inverse of that annoying thing people do: more of a question than a comment. But do you think there's a path back for them that's less dramatic than some kind of fundamental realignment of Canadian federal politics? I say this with no snark, but if this is their path back, they're in more trouble than I thought!
SD They are in serious trouble. But I had been watching Durham for echoes of what we've seen in the U.S. Even though Republicans and Trump are supposed to be on a romp back to power in the White House, the Democrats keep doing better than predicted in midterms and special votes, such as the recent one to fill George Santos' seat in Congress. David Brooks wrote about this phenomenon in the New York Times late last year, saying: "Americans increasingly use polls to vent, not vote." The idea is that it's all well and good to vent right now, whether that's in a poll or byelection, but when it comes to the actual ballot-box choice, the moderate centrist voters, especially those frustrated with political turbulence as you put it, will lean progressive, not conservative.
The trick for the Liberals, as it is for (President Joe) Biden and the Democrats, is to make very sure that Canadians stare down that choice here.
MG That's one of the reasons I won't make a firm prediction. It'll be easier for the two of us (and everyone else!) to turn their minds to this the day after we know the outcome of the next U.S. presidential election (which I do not assume will be the day after the election - we should be prepared for some chaos, if only to manage our expectations properly). So sure. I can sketch out a scenario along those broad lines, too: a VERY nasty U.S. election campaign, a re-elected Donald Trump, a few months in which Trump says and does things that horrify a big chunk of the population. I dare say that to some extent the Liberals are already campaigning along those lines. It could work. But I also am skeptical it'll work in the way they think. If Trump goes Full Trump on the world again, I'm not convinced attempts to link him to Poilievre succeed in alarming Canadians. It could easily just make Poilievre look vastly more palatable by comparison.
SD Well that's a sobering view of the future. All too plausible, too, I'm sorry to say. (And apologies, here we are agreeing again.) I'll say this: I think Liberals believe they did a good job handling Trump once and voters will remember that when it counts. I'm not sure voters do. But I also think we're not paying enough attention to the polls showing some discomfort/unease with the idea of Prime Minister Poilievre.
MGYeah, I get that. But he's got some room to manoeuvre. The polling aggregators have him somewhere in line for 200-210 seats. Which means he can lose 30-40 seats to that discomfort and still have a majority for up to five years. I do agree, as noted above, that he could definitely alienate a lot of voters. He seems to have a problem controlling his acid tongue at times, and that could sour people. But as I said, he could alienate 30 seats worth of people and still win handily. So I do pay attention to those polls. But he definitely has some margin for error, no pun intended.
SDAgreed, and Trudeau, unlike the 2019 and 2021 elections, does not. Moreover, if Liberals want the next election to be framed as a stark choice for the moderate middle in Canada, they need voters to come to that conclusion themselves, not jam it down their throats. One senior Liberal admitted to me a while back that they probably can't run a campaign of hope the next time, but they have to be very careful with a campaign of fear, too.
MG They are in a tough spot. No doubt about it. They need to wait until the U.S. election shakes out, to some extent. But they won't have much time after that. I don't envy them their problems, that's for sure.
CREDIT: Matt Gurney OPINION SUSAN DELACOURT OPINION;

Subject: Political campaigns; Polls & surveys; Election results; Presidential elections; Voters

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Title: An expected defeat was unexpectedly painful for Liberals

Document 415 of 500

An expected defeat was unexpectedly painful for Justin Trudeau's Liberals. That should set off alarm bells

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/expected-defeat-was-unexpectedly-painful-justin/docview/2957844453/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=An+expected+defeat+was+unexpectedly+painful+for+Justin+Trudeau%27s+Liberals.+That+should+set+off+alarm+bells&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a recurring feature, Susan Delacourt, a small-l liberal, and Matt Gurney, a small-c conservative, bring their different perspectives - and shared commitment to civil disagreement - to the political debates of the moment.
MG: I know better than to make predictions in politics. I'm not paid enough to risk that kind of embarrassment, and we live in such bizarrely turbulent times that I'm happy if any given day goes roughly as I expected. But I can't help but note that the Durham byelection result earlier this month is bad news for the Liberals.
I'm mostly firmly in the "byelections don't matter" camp, but this specific byelection isn't just sending back a new Tory to replace another Tory in a safely CPC seat. I'll agree to all the usual caveats about campaigns mattering and there being a lot of time left, but I don't see how anyone at Liberal HQ who was trying to hold onto much hope is going to be able to do that after Durham. The result doesn't matter, but getting confirmation that the polls are broadly accurate should.
SD: We promised to disagree last time we wrote, so let me get my two points of agreement out of the way quickly. Yes, that was a brutal byelection for the Liberals and I, too, am cautious about reading too much into the results of them. You are correct, too - this confirms what all those polls have been fairly screaming to the Liberals over this past year. Here is where we may disagree, though: I still think there's a path for the Liberals to recover before the next election, though it's a faint and narrow one.
MG: I mean, I'll let you make the case, but I don't reject the notion entirely. I think we're just getting into the kind of territory where the only man who can stop Pierre Poilievre is Pierre Poilievre, and he may well try. What's your sense of the path?
SD: First, the path comes from taking the Durham byelection seriously - as seriously as Stephen Harper did way back in 2003 after a byelection in the southwestern Ontario riding of Perth-Middlesex. It was the disastrous showing for the Canadian Alliance in Perth-Middlesex that convinced Harper it was time to get serious about uniting the right in Canada.
Now, I'm not saying Justin Trudeau needs to go out and merge with the New Democrats (some say that's already happened). But I am saying the progressive centre in Canadian politics is as fragmented as the conservative centre was in the early 2000s and Liberals seem to be losing their hold on the mainstream middle of the road.
MG: This won't be much of a reply. It's the inverse of that annoying thing people do: more of a question than a comment. But do you think there's a path back for them that's less dramatic than some kind of fundamental realignment of Canadian federal politics? I say this with no snark, but if this is their path back, they're in more trouble than I thought!
SD: They are in serious trouble. But I had been watching Durham for echoes of what we've seen in the U.S. Even though Republicans and Trump are supposed to be on a romp back to power in the White House, the Democrats keep doing better than predicted in midterms and special votes, such as the recent one to fill George Santos' seat in Congress. David Brooks wrote about this phenomenon in the New York Times late last year, saying: "Americans increasingly use polls to vent, not vote." The idea is that it's all well and good to vent right now, whether that's in a poll or byelection, but when it comes to the actual ballot-box choice, the moderate centrist voters, especially those frustrated with political turbulence as you put it, will lean progressive, not conservative.
The trick for the Liberals, as it is for (President Joe) Biden and the Democrats, is to make very sure that Canadians stare down that choice here.
MG: That's one of the reasons I won't make a firm prediction. It'll be easier for the two of us (and everyone else!) to turn their minds to this the day after we know the outcome of the next U.S. presidential election (which I do not assume will be the day after the election - we should be prepared for some chaos, if only to manage our expectations properly). So sure. I can sketch out a scenario along those broad lines, too: a VERY nasty U.S. election campaign, a re-elected Donald Trump, a few months in which Trump says and does things that horrify a big chunk of the population. I dare say that to some extent the Liberals are already campaigning along those lines. It could work. But I also am skeptical it'll work in the way they think. If Trump goes Full Trump on the world again, I'm not convinced attempts to link him to Poilievre succeed in alarming Canadians. It could easily just make Poilievre look vastly more palatable by comparison.
SD: Well that's a sobering view of the future. All too plausible, too, I'm sorry to say. (And apologies, here we are agreeing again.) I'll say this: I think Liberals believe they did a good job handling Trump once and voters will remember that when it counts. I'm not sure voters do. But I also think we're not paying enough attention to the polls showing some discomfort/unease with the idea of Prime Minister Poilievre.
MG: Yeah, I get that. But he's got some room to manoeuvre. The polling aggregators have him somewhere in line for 200-210 seats. Which means he can lose 30-40 seats to that discomfort and still have a majority for up to five years. I do agree, as noted above, that he could definitely alienate a lot of voters. He seems to have a problem controlling his acid tongue at times, and that could sour people. But as I said, he could alienate 30 seats worth of people and still win handily. So I do pay attention to those polls. But he definitely has some margin for error, no pun intended.
SD: Agreed, and Trudeau, unlike the 2019 and 2021 elections, does not. Moreover, if Liberals want the next election to be framed as a stark choice for the moderate middle in Canada, they need voters to come to that conclusion themselves, not jam it down their throats. One senior Liberal admitted to me a while back that they probably can't run a campaign of hope the next time, but they have to be very careful with a campaign of fear, too.
MG: They are in a tough spot. No doubt about it. They need to wait until the U.S. election shakes out, to some extent. But they won't have much time after that. I don't envy them their problems, that's for sure.
CAPTION: Conservative candidate Jamil Jivani won the Durham byelection on March 4, 2024.na-onthecontrary-mar16 printna-onthecontrary-mar16 web

Subject: Polls & surveys; Political campaigns; Election results; Presidential elections; Voters; Political parties

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Document 416 of 500

Carbon levy pause bigger risk for PM

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/carbon-levy-pause-bigger-risk-pm/docview/2957844345/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Carbon+levy+pause+bigger+risk+for+PM&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau acknowledged this past week that he's no longer popular. Hearkening back to similar comments by former prime minister Brian Mulroney, whose funeral will be held Saturday, Trudeau said, "My job is not to be popular. ... My job is to do the right things for Canada now, and do the right things for Canadians a generation from now."
Friends and foes suggest a solution to Trudeau's popularity problem - one that may lead the Liberals towards an existential crisis in the next election - is a pause to the carbon price increase coming to Canadians' wallets on April 1.
But Trudeau has much more to lose if he amends his government's signature climate policy than if he stands his ground.
Next month, the levy will rise by 23 per cent, from $65 a tonne to $80. The renamed Canada Carbon Rebates - those quarterly payments Canadians receive to offset the carbon price - will rise to reflect the change. But everyday items will also be more expensive: gasoline for your vehicle, the cost to heat your home, food at the grocery store, airplane tickets for your summer vacation.
With Canadians feeling squeezed from inflation and higher interest rates, it's no surprise that provincial premiers - those who, unlike their peers in Quebec and British Columbia, chose not to enact their own carbon pricing regimes and rely on the federal backstop, making price hikes Ottawa's problems and not their own - now want Trudeau to pause the plan.
On Monday, Newfoundland and Labrador Premier Andrew Furey, a fellow Liberal, penned a letter politely requesting Trudeau pause the increase, "at least until inflation stabilizes, interest rates lower and related economic pressures on the cost of living sufficiently cool."
On Tuesday, New Brunswick Premier Blaine Higgs called on Trudeau to "immediately cancel his punishing April 1st carbon tax hike. Even better, cancel the carbon tax altogether."
Later that evening, Nova Scotia Premier Tim Houston wrote his own letter to the prime minister, noting the increase will add 3.3 cents per litre of gas and asking that Trudeau "cancel the carbon tax before any more financial damage is done."
On Wednesday, Premier Doug Ford told reporters he doesn't understand what the federal Liberals are thinking. "If they don't start ... putting money back into people's pockets instead of filling their pockets, guess what, they're going to get annihilated."
On Thursday, Alberta Premier Danielle Smith noted seven premiers are now united in demanding Ottawa reverse course.
On Friday, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, in New Brunswick for a "spike the hike" rally, called on the prime minister to cancel his "heartless and cruel ... tax hike."
But Trudeau and federal Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault appear unmoved.
They argue their plan is an affordability measure; that it returns more money into most people's pockets; that without the carbon price and the carbon price rebates, more people would struggle to make ends meet.
But as Ford's comments reflect, many Canadians - including the premier - don't understand how the federal program works. It's designed to make things more expensive, so you're pushed to find alternatives and adopt new habits - things like installing a heat pump, buying a hybrid or electric vehicle, or biking to work.
The program was always designed to ensure most Canadians weren't financially disadvantaged, with a significant portion of the levy collected returned to residents. A family of four in urban Ontario, for example, will receive $1,120 this year; in Alberta it's $1,800. But, as Guilbeault acknowledges, too little attention was placed on the second part of the Grits' plan.
Carbon pricing was always going to be a tough sell because it is visible. That is both its strength and its vulnerability.
It may also be the Liberals' strength and vulnerability.
Last fall, after intense lobbying from his Atlantic MPs, Trudeau announced a nationwide pause of the carbon levy on home heating oil for three years, a federal-provincial plan to rebate the costs of installing heat pumps, and a doubling of the rural climate rebate top-up. There was little impact at the polls. It may have stopped Liberal hemorrhaging in the Atlantic region, but it did not reverse the slide. Abacus Data had the Liberals polling at 32 per cent support in September compared to 43 per cent for the Conservatives. This month, the Grits are at 33 per cent, and the Tories are at 48 per cent in Atlantic Canada (with a comparable margin of error of plus or minus 2.53 per cent, 19 times out of 20.)
Why would the Liberals think any new pause to the carbon pricing regime will lead to an uptick in support for them? It may instead lead to more bleeding as climate-conscious voters decide Trudeau is willing to sacrifice the predictability of his plan for short-term political gain. That would be the quickest path to Liberal irrelevance.
With a few percentage points shaved off of Liberal support - as Abacus CEO David Coletto notes happened in November, following the heating oil announcement - the Grits could be battling the NDP for second-place territory. And that, notes Coletto, would be a death blow to the Liberals.
It's one more reason Poilievre and his allies may be putting so much pressure on Trudeau to "spike the hike."
CAPTION: Any pause to the carbon pricing policy may lead to more problems for the Liberals, as climate- conscious voters decide if Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is willing to sacrifice the predictabilityof his planfor short-term political gain, Althia Raj writes.Adrian WyldThe Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: Althia raj OPINION

Which Trudeau legacies will survive?: Although Poilievre vows to take bold steps as PM, many Liberal policies appear safe

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/which-trudeau-legacies-will-survive/docview/2957844206/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Which+Trudeau+legacies+will+survive%3F%3A+Although+Poilievre+vows+to+take+bold+steps+as+PM%2C+many+Liberal+policies+appear+safe&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime ministers always say they leave their political legacies to historians. But it's their successors who largely write that history by deciding what parts of any governing agenda survive.
So what should we glean from the reflections about Brian Mulroney's legacy? His successors left most of the former Progressive Conservative leader's important accomplishments intact, even those they once adamantly opposed.
And what might it tell us about which parts of Justin Trudeau's agenda will survive, or what lessons Pierre Poilievre has taken?
Plenty.
In Mulroney's case, his big successes and big failures shifted the national conversation and charted paths for those who followed him.
Liberal Jean Chrétien signed onto Mulroney's North American Free Trade Agreement and did not replace the GST, despite campaigning against both. In Mulroney's two failed efforts to rewrite the Canadian Constitution, his successors found inspiration for how to try to politically accommodate Quebec's unique place in Canada. Quebec's demands to opt out of federal spending programs with full compensation go unchallenged now by any federal leader. And Stephen Harper, despite roots in a Reform party that fiercely opposed Mulroney's "distinct society" status for Quebec, passed a motion recognizing "the Quebecois form a nation within a united Canada."
We can already glimpse how much of Trudeau's time in government will survive a change if, as polls indicate now, the Conservatives led by Poilievre win the next election, scheduled for 2025.
In an interview with the Star's Susan Delacourt in Guelph, Trudeau would not be drawn into talk of his legacy because he insists he's not going anywhere.
Trudeau did, however, talk about what he thinks will not be undone.
"The Canada Child Benefit can't be rolled back; it lifted 400,000 kids out of poverty over the past few years," he said.
"Child care - we got Alberta, Manitoba and Saskatchewan to sign on. And regardless of the fact that the Conservative party ran against it in the last election, and wouldn't have done it if they'd gotten elected, now that it's in place, and once it will be in place for a year or two, I don't think that'll be reversible."
Beyond that, Trudeau said, he believes some of his government's moves on reconciliation have "moved the floor to a much higher level that you can never go back. ... You'll have governments that could go far slower than we have and be less enthusiastic and supportive on it, but I think we have seen a step change on that."
The same is true of climate change, Trudeau said.
In just a half dozen years, he says the public debate has profoundly shifted. Canadians talk of climate change now in terms of how quickly or how much they need to do, rather than whether they should do anything at all. "That's something that wasn't obvious before, but is now irrevocable."
Some of that seems true.
The Conservatives under Harper brought in a child-care benefit that Trudeau overhauled to include a number of other support measures, leading to a payment for families that experts say has been transformative. The Poilievre Conservatives, with their focus on ordinary Canadians, are unlikely to kill it.
When it comes to child care, Poilievre may leave much of the Trudeau program intact. Why? Because after Harper killed the first iteration of a Liberal national child-care program - the $5-billion plan prime minister Paul Martin signed with provinces - the Conservatives' effort (which amounted to $100 monthly cheques to parents and tax incentives that failed to create the promised daycare spaces) was widely panned.
Poilievre's Conservatives already voted to pass Trudeau's child-care legislation in the House of Commons, despite protesting it did not help private at-home daycare providers, or shift workers whose daycare needs don't match public non-profit daycare centre hours. If anything, the Conservatives are likely to face pressure to live up to their rhetoric to help those parents.
Poilievre has also begun to lay out his objectives on Indigenous reconciliation, chiefly framing it as encouraging Indigenous communities to take a key role in and reap the benefits of developing natural resources on their lands.
In his interview with the Star, Trudeau did not cite his reform to the way senators are appointed. The prime minister now draws from an application list vetted by an independent advisory committee, a process the Conservatives deride as partisan. He did not say that gender equity in cabinet, or on the country's top court, is the new normal. He did not mention dental care or the as-yet unfinished national pharmacare project as irreversible.
Some of that may be undone.
If Poilievre has eyes on his own legacy, his takeaway from Mulroney's record might be: do big things, and do them fast.
It's a parlour game for Ottawa's chattering classes to guess what Poilievre would do beyond his slogans of "axe the tax, fix the budget, build the houses and stop the crime."
Guess no more.
Poilievre makes no bones about his intention to swiftly kill the carbon levy on consumers, to shut down safe injection sites, to ramp up the number of mandatory minimum prison sentences for drug, gun and violent crimes, and to slash funding for the CBC.
Speaking to the Greater Vancouver Board of Trade recently, he gave the clearest indication yet of how he intends to take bold steps and damn the critics, and that he learned from Mulroney's legacy.
While "everyone praises him now," Poilievre said, Mulroney carved out an agenda that was "very controversial."
"He had to take on a lot of entrenched interests in order to achieve the, frankly, the revolution that he brought at the time: privatizing Air Canada and numerous other Crown corporations that had been very entrenched."
Poilievre admired Mulroney for "reducing the cost of government," saying that during his tenure as prime minister, Canada had "among the slowest ... spending growth of any government in history." Mulroney "balanced the operational budget. We had an ongoing deficit because of the interest on the preceding Trudeau government's debt."
Poilievre gave only passing nod to Mulroney nailing a free trade deal with the United States, the legacy centrepiece Mulroney's critics now hail, before praising at length Mulroney's role in setting a clear inflation target for monetary policy, the purview of the central bank.
"Up until then, the Bank of Canada had no mandate of any clear purpose, so they just kept printing money and leading to double-digit inflation. He brought in a two-per-cent inflation target," Poilievre said.
Hammering the Bank of Canada was an early and ongoing stock phrase in Poilievre's speeches. He claims the central bank enabled the Liberal government's deficit-spending through the pandemic. That, he has said, caused high inflation, which in turn led to higher interest rates that are crushing Canadians. Poilievre in the past promised to fire Bank of Canada governor Tiff Macklem, although he has not reiterated that recently. And since Macklem has taken to lamenting the pace of federal government spending, perhaps it's Trudeau who now wishes the central bank governor would get a new job.
Moving to Mulroney's international legacy, Poilievre - whose support for Ukraine has been challenged by Liberals - praised him as "firmly on the side of freedom against Soviet communism, which was a change from the previous government." And he noted Mulroney "was on the side of freedom in South Africa against apartheid, which was a lonely position for him to take in the western world at the time, but obviously he has been proven right.
"So the lesson I take from that is that he was prepared to stand alone in order to stand for what was right, and that it wasn't easy in the short run," Poilievre said, "but in the long run, he has been vindicated."
It's not clear yet what that means for Poilievre's military and foreign policy. Will he undo or bolster the Liberals' long-term security guarantees for Ukraine's fight against Russia? Will he dial back Canadian troops in Latvia until Canada's budget is on stronger footing? He says he's prepared to cut "wasteful" foreign aid, and money for the United Nations' relief agency in Gaza.
In Vancouver, Poilievre hailed Mulroney's "balanced budgets, free enterprise, privatization, better support for our resource sector," and pivoted to his now-everyday slogan, painting the former prime minister as the standard bearer for a "common-sense consensus" which he said had "unanimous" support among Conservatives, New Democrats and Liberals until Trudeau came to power in 2015.
Does it amount to a governing agenda? Possibly. Does it amount to a legacy? Poilievre's own successors will decide.
CAPTION: If elected prime minister next year, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre plans to kill the carbon levy, shut down safe injection sites, ramp up mandatory minimum prison sentences for drug, gun and violent crimes, and slash funding for the CBC. But as Jean Chrétien proved when succeeding Brian Mulroney, even controversial policies like free trade and the GST take on a different light when you're in power.The Canadian Pressfile photos

CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star

Document 418 of 500

'My time to give back:' Ach v CEO Tonie Chaltas goes from the Bluenose II to helping new Canadians navigate job market

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/my-time-give-back-ach-v-ceo-tonie-chaltas-goes/docview/2957743717/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27My+time+to+give+back%3A%27+Ach+v+CEO+Tonie+Chaltas+goes+from+the+Bluenose+II+to+helping+new+Canadians+navigate+job+market&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Long before she was named one of Canada's most powerful women by Women's Executive Network, Ach v CEO Tonie Chaltas was a sailor.
The year was 1987, and Chaltas was studying commerce at Dalhousie University in Halifax. She took a summer job working on the Bluenose II tourist ship, the heritage vessel featured on the back of the Canadian dime. Its goal was to promote tourism in Nova Scotia by sailing around the province, and over to Boston.
It was a landmark year for the Bluenose; for the first time ever, women would be allowed to join the crew.
Chaltas didn't think much of it at the time. She was used to the idea of women taking charge, because she'd grown up without a father; he had passed away when she was young. So it was just Chaltas, her sister, and her mother at home.
They took care of one another. "You don't know what you don't know when you're a child," she said. "The lack of a father was just life."
But crewing on the Bluenose was a wake-up call for her. She met people who hated the idea of a woman stepping into a traditionally male role. Some even thought it was bad luck to have women on board. These people felt that tradition, heritage, and women didn't mix.
"I had never thought about the male-female dynamic in a work environment before," Chaltas said. "It had never really crossed my mind." It occurred to her that crewing the Bluenose was not just a job, but an "incredible opportunity to be a part of history."
For the rest of her career, Chaltas would be drawn to women's rights charities and initiatives. She would go on to volunteer with the International Women's Forum, Women's College Foundation Hospital, Verity Mastermind, and the BlackNorth Initiative. She was also a founding member of Women Against Multiple Sclerosis and currently serves as a mentor for the Women's Executive Network.
Community activism has always come naturally to Chaltas.
"This phrase that we often hear - 'It takes a village' - I absolutely lived that growing up." She was 'raised' by her community in the little town of Cannington, southeast of Orillia, where her family moved shortly after her father died. "The community really stepped up in ways that I probably didn't even see and understand at that time," she said.
When she left Dalhousie, Chaltas worked as a staffer for conservative Mike Harris before he became premier. She was one of the founders of 'Women In Politics,' a group which aims to support and promote Conservative women to be candidates and take leadership roles in the PC party.
Mike Coates, then-Canadian CEO of strategic communications consultancy Hill & Knowlton Strategies, took notice of Chaltas' expertise in advocacy and recruited her to join the company as National Practice Leader for International Affairs.
Coates was "immediately impressed" by Chaltas, he said. She was especially skilled at "bringing people along." In other words, she was able to carefully think through a situation "and then (was) able to bring people along with her to execute whatever plan she (came) up with ... as opposed to ordering people what to do."
This way staff are "engaged because they believe in it, not because they report to you and they have to follow you," Chaltas said. Leaders should set the direction but avoid micromanaging, she added. If they don't, "(they'll) fail," she said. "(The task) will never get done, and if it does, it won't get done to the best of its potential."
She thinks leaders should work to equip staff to "do what they're really great at ... their 'best and highest use.'"
When Coates became CEO of the Americas for Hill & Knowlton, he appointed Chaltas COO. There, Chaltas helped him put together maternity and equal-pay policies for the U.S. branch of the organization. In the world of PR, where there is often significant turnover, Coates and Chaltas worked to create an environment where staff would be compelled to stay. "Tonie was a master at that."
But Chaltas is not one to call herself a 'master,' or trumpet her business acumen. "She believes that her actions should speak louder than her words about herself," Coates said. "It isn't me, myself, and I, it's about the organization, and that's Tonie."
After 20 years at Hill & Knowlton, Chaltas felt it was time to move on to something more purpose-driven.
"I felt like it was my time to give back. I wanted to do something that was good for my soul," she said, "Ach v came along and it was love at first sight, and it's turned out to be one of the best jobs I've ever had."
Ach v, a nonprofit with nine offices in the GTA, helps Canadians and newcomers navigate the job market. As CEO, Chaltas manages 450 staff and more than 100,000 clients.
"In many cases, newcomers come with an awful lot of experience and education and then struggle to get jobs in the area they were trained in," Chaltas said. "We help them overcome some of those barriers."
Newcomers are often forced to take "survival jobs" - well below their qualifications and pay-grade - just to make ends meet.
Employers are part of the problem. "There's no question... There's unconscious bias that comes into play," Chaltas said. "We try and help (employers) understand the skills and competencies they're looking for, versus the experiences."
The role differs significantly from those Chaltas has held in the past, said Sylvia Chrominska, Chaltas' close friend of nearly 20 years. Yet Chaltas has "survived and thrived" in the job, said Chrominska. "She has a lot to be proud of." Her expertise, for one, but also her "genuine warmth," she added. "She's generous of spirit and of heart... and quite willing to lend her expertise to those causes that are important to her."
Chaltas thinks everyone should champion a cause if they are able to. "Learning to articulate a point of view about something you care about helps to expand your horizons and allows you to grow as a leader," she said.
"Not everybody is going to necessarily gravitate to a particular issue," she said, in which case she suggests people take the time to go out into the world and discover one.
Engaging in the issues you believe in is an intentional effort, she said. "Find a point of view and use your voice."
CAPTION: After a summer job 37 years ago as one of the first female crew aboard the Bluenose II, Ach v CEO Tonie Chaltas would be drawn to women's rights charities and initiatives.
CREDIT: Marisa Coulton Special to The Star

Subject: Womens rights; Leadership; Seasonal employment; Personal profiles; Employment; Labor market

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership Seasonal employment Employment Labor market

Title: 'My time to give back:' Ach v CEO Tonie Chaltas goes from the Bluenose II to helping new Canadians navigate job market

Document 419 of 500

Why Bob Rae is correct on Israel-Hamas conflict and how Manitoba sets an example for provincial politics

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-bob-rae-is-correct-on-israel-hamas-conflict/docview/2957743614/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+Bob+Rae+is+correct+on+Israel-Hamas+conflict+and+how+Manitoba+sets+an+example+for+provincial+politics&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Supporting a two-state solution
How Canada's most fiery envoy found himself in the line of fire: Cohn, March 8
Ambassador Bob Rae is absolutely correct when he says that "the ultimate responsibility for the conduct of war lies with the parties who are fighting." Equally, if not more important, is for all the demonstrators around the world to recognize that to have peace both parties must want it. Demonstrators can demand peace until the end of time but there will be no peace until all sides want peace. These demonstrators need to understand that they cannot force or wish people to get along.
Charles Campisi, Oakville, Ont.
Kudos to columnist Martin Regg Cohn for shining a light on Canada's position and votes at the UN. Thanks to Bob Rae for holding the UN position with dignity and clearly stating that Canada supports a two state solution, the terrible situation in Gaza right now is the ultimate responsibility of both fighting sides, and the hatred and anger of demonstrators on both sides here in Canada is often without knowing what the conflict is even about. Rae helps us see what it is to be committed to peace and the immense difficulties faced in working for it.
Susan Turner, Guelph, Ont.
Manitoba setting the example
Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew has a winning formula. Progressive leaders should listen: Phillips, March 12
Tuesday was NDP Appreciation Day on the Opinion page of The Star. The third article, titled "Studies show that rent controls work" only sealed the deal: the NDP make excellent provincial governments, being socially progressive and rarely if ever mired in scandal. The contrast between governments run by populists like Alberta's Danielle's Smith, Saskatchewan's Scott Moe and Ontario's Doug Ford couldn't be greater. Yet, if Bonnie Crombie's leadership takes off, Ontarians may once again see the popular vote split between the NDP and the Liberals, allowing Ford to achieve yet another majority government. And lest you object by pointing to Bob Rae's NDP government, just remember that Rae increased social spending during tough economic times, and made some government workers go on periods of unpaid leave, colloquially known as "Rae Days." That move seems far from Draconian when one considers the actions of subsequent provincial governments. I've always been a Liberal Democrat. If New Democrats and Liberals don't want to see progressive issues woefully underserved, they should amalgamate.
Ron Charach, Toronto
Trudeau could save himself
Trudeau ally joins Poilievre to demand a halt to the April 1 carbon levy increase, March 12
The Conservative Party of Canada and its leader Pierre Poilievre have relentlessly attacked the carbon tax, and held Prime Minister Justin Trudeau responsible. Other provincial governments have joined the anti-carbon tax train, along with millions of voters. A conservative government will kill the tax, so why not suspend it now, develop a new climate plan to present to Canadians at the next election? It would ease some cost of living pain, and force a more productive policy debate on the type of climate plan that's best for Canada. The current situation is far too divisive and truly unhelpful, except to the CPC and their leader. Why not puncture their balloon and force their hand to develop a plan.
Desmond Pouyat, Toronto
City hall needs a map
This week in the Star, the City of Toronto published a notice of public meeting pertaining to property at Islington and Bloor in Etobicoke. The meeting is in Scarborough. I emailed the City Registrar and the Clerk to ask for a change of venue. Only those who make an oral presentation have a right to appeal and Scarborough is a long way from Etobicoke. Though I received an apology, it is clear Toronto needs to give their employees a map.
Gail Rutherford, Etobicoke
Stand up for Picasso
Why I think Picasso's masterpiece 'Guernica' is ultimately a failure: Mallick, March 11
I was deeply moved by columnist Heather Mallick's article, but I do not think Picasso failed. He didn't have a cellphone and used his art to brilliantly capture the injustice and horror. Today, with technology, we witness the horror inflicted on Gaza daily, but it is the powerful governments that support the genocide that are failing. I note that Gaza and Guernica share the first and last letters.
Elizabeth Hill, Toronto
CAPTION: Canadian Ambassador to the United Nations Bob Rae makes his way to speak to media at the United Nations in New York on Tuesday, Sept. 20, 2022.

Subject: Leadership; Peace; Carbon; Government; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership Environmental tax

Location: Manitoba Canada; Gaza Strip; Canada

People: Rae, Bob

Company / organization: Name: United Nations--UN; NAICS: 928120

Document 420 of 500

Time for Ford government to rein in spending and deliver tax relief

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/time-ford-government-rein-spending-deliver-tax/docview/2957743608/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Time+for+Ford+government+to+rein+in+spending+and+deliver+tax+relief&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has often criticized his predecessor's fiscal approach, yet the Ford government has consistently employed the same approach while in office. When the government tables its next budget March 26, it should finally back up the tough talk and change its approach to fiscal policy.
First, some quick history. From 2008/09 to 2017/18 (the last year before Ford was elected), Ontario governments had run 10 consecutive deficits totalling $103.3 billion, due in part to a substantial increase in spending - during this period, annual program spending (total spending minus debt interest) increased by 41.6 per cent. In other words, by 2017/18 the Ontario government was spending $1,207 more per person than in 2008/09 (after adjusting for inflation).
Meanwhile, the government refused to enact broad-based tax relief. Despite a minor tax cut for low-income residents in 2010, the government raised taxes on upper-income earners in 2012 and 2014 while middle-income earners saw no reduction to their personal income tax rates.
This approach has hurt the province's economy. Growing government debt can increase the cost of borrowing in the private sector, which reduces capital investment, while high and increasing tax rates reduce the incentive for people to work and invest by lowering the rewards for these activities. Both of these effects limit economic growth and produce lower living standards than there otherwise would be. Indeed, in recent years growth in per-person GDP, a common measure of living standards, has slowed in Ontario relative to the rest of Canada.
In 2018, on the campaign trail, then-candidate Ford criticized the Wynne government's "reckless spending" and continued deficits, and promised if elected to cut taxes and find "efficiencies" in the provincial budget. In its first budget in 2019, the Ford government promised to "fix Ontario's inherited fiscal mess ... before it hurts the economy" by balancing the budget, reducing the debt burden and cutting taxes.
Yet, while the Ford government has talked a big game, it's employed the same approach to government finances as its predecessors - increased spending, deficits and no broad-based tax relief.
By the end of this fiscal year, deficits under the Ford government will have totalled a projected $41.7 billion over six years. Although the Ford government did run a $2.0 billion surplus in 2021/22, this was due to an unexpected surge in revenues as opposed to spending restraint. The following year the government promptly increased spending and returned to deficits. Due to this unwillingness to restrain spending, the government has been unable to keep its promises to reduce debt and lower taxes.
It didn't need to be this way. For example, had the Ford government simply maintained the Wynne government's level of per-person spending, rather than increase spending, it would have accumulated $27.8 billion less debt (which equals roughly $2,000 for every Ontarian). The government could have used these savings to reduce income taxes by an average of $888 per tax filer in 2023.
With Ontario's economy (and subsequent living standards) falling behind the rest of Canada, in its upcoming budget the Ford government should quickly change course and finally keep its promise to restrain spending and deliver meaningful tax relief for Ontarians.
CAPTION: When Doug Ford's government unveils its budget later this month, it shouldchange its approach to fiscal policy, write contributors Grady Munro and Jake Fuss.
CREDIT: Grady Munro and Jake Fuss Contributors

Subject: Provincial budget; Fiscal policy; Standard of living; Taxes; Tax rates; Economic development; Relief provisions; Budgets; Debt restructuring

Business indexing term: Subject: Provincial budget Fiscal policy Standard of living Taxes Tax rates Economic development Relief provisions Budgets Debt restructuring

Location: Canada

Title: Time for Ford government to rein in spending and deliver tax relief

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Section: opinion/contributors

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Document 421 of 500

Prime Minister Pierre Poilievre? That's bad news for Doug Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/prime-minister-pierre-poilievre-thats-bad-news/docview/2957743561/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Prime+Minister+Pierre+Poilievre%3F+That%27s+bad+news+for+Doug+Ford&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: By rights, these two right-wingers should be soul mates.
Yet they are anything but.
Premier Doug Ford and federal Opposition Leader Pierre Poilievre barely know each other. Nor are they in a hurry to get better acquainted.
They have no private conversations to speak of. Nor any public interactions to take stock of.
Helming the Progressive Conservative government at Queen's Park, Ford is Canada's most powerful Tory. As leader of the federal Conservatives, Poilievre is putatively Canada's prime minister in waiting.
By tradition, these two top Tories should be as one. Yet it is hard to fathom two fellow travellers moving in such different directions - keeping their political distance when in close proximity.
The best evidence of that avoidance came last weekend, when Poilievre convened a mass political rally in Ford's home riding of Etobicoke North. Poilievre's call went out to all true believers to stand together against the federal carbon tax, but Ford sent word out to caucus that he'd sit it out.
Despite their shared antipathy to taxes and hostility to government meddling, they clearly feel no mutual felicity. In truth, they have no time for each other, never having attempted a meeting of minds in the 18 months since Poilievre became federal party leader and the six years since Ford took over the provincial Tories.
What's keeping these two conservatives so far apart? Why can't they be friends and allies, if not comrades-in-arms?
From talking to those in the know, it's apparent that the differences are as much personal as political, more stylistic than substantive.
Poilievre is a lifetime politician who never went to charm school. Ford had a life before politics, capable of turning on the charm to make a sale for the family business.
Where Poilievre is constantly chippy, Ford is alternately chipper to get his way. Where the federal leader is cantankerous, the premier can be gregarious when the mood moves him.
While Poilievre disdains Ottawa, he embodies the bubble of the federal capital - right down to the riding he represents. By contrast, Ford projects an everyman persona, cultivating the salty language of working folks while downplaying his privileged upbringing.
But there is a bigger divide between these two personalities, for Ford already wields power while Poilievre is merely on the cusp of it.
The federal opposition leader is ever the ideologue because he can afford to be a poseur rather than a policymaker. Ford is forever the populist, more mindful of the realities of governing and the early mistakes of harsh restraint.
Canada's Conservatives are anchored in Western regional alienation, oblivious to the instinct for accommodation in Ontario. As premier, Ford is more mindful of the province's propensity for centrism and compromise, no matter his initial impulse for conflict and upheaval.
Paradoxically, the premier sees his true counterpart as Liberal Prime Minister Justin Trudeau. While the two leaders are far from soul mates, they are at least simpatico - breezily cutting cheques and cutting ribbons for new factories or shared programs.
If Ford is more seasoned than six years ago, Poilievre remains utterly untested after his 18 months in the spotlight - reflexively riding the anti-Trudeau bandwagon rather than steering his own course. Ford and Trudeau are not just partners in governing, but co-dependent in their politics.
While voting alliances can shift, the premier and prime minister have long shared an overlapping coalition of supporters across Ontario that crosses party lines. Voters in this province have historically sent opposing party leaders to Queen's Park and Parliament Hill.
That tradition of electoral contradiction is top of mind for Ford's Tories, who fear a Poilievre triumph in the next federal election (expected in late 2025) could set the stage for a similar alternance in power for the next provincial election (set for mid-2026). Put another way, Poilievre as PM would be bad for Ford's political prospects, whereas Trudeau remains a convenient foil and occasional friend.
Six years ago, the two counterparts had a counterproductive relationship, with Ford picking fights and Trudeau using the premier as an electoral punching bag. But COVID brought their two governments together, as Ford became close to Deputy PM Chrystia Freeland and Intergovernmental Affairs Minister Dominic LeBlanc; Ford's gushing praise for Freeland when she became finance minister exasperated the federal Conservatives, undercutting their hostile rhetoric.
The rapprochement between provincial PCs and federal Liberals has persisted even as Trudeau's popularity has slumped. While Ford has dialed down the public backslapping - dashing off finger-wagging open letters to the federal Liberals condemning high interest rates and the carbon tax - the private backchannels and political collegiality remain undiminished.
While Ford may not always be in Trudeau's corner publicly, he still whispers in his ear privately - both backstage and on the phone. That's more than can be said for the premier and Poilievre, where there is no discernible affection or connection - in public or private.
That could change as the political winds shift, forcing Canada's two top Tories to behave as bedfellows instead of strangers. But that would be an accident of electoral history, not solidarity.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford's Tories fear a Pierre Poilievre triumph in the next federal election could set the stage for a change of power in Ontario, Martin Regg Cohn writes.

Subject: Mindfulness; Provincial elections; Scandals; Power; Politics; Prime ministers; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Environmental tax

Location: Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Title: Prime Minister Pierre Poilievre? That's bad news for Doug Ford

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Mar 16, 2024

Section: politics

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document URL: https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/prime-minister-pierre-poilievre-thats-bad-news/docview/2957743561/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 422 of 500

Poilievre as PM is bad news for Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievre-as-pm-is-bad-news-ford/docview/2957707418/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+as+PM+is+bad+news+for+Ford&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford was nowhere to be seen at last weekend's rally in his home riding by federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre. By tradition, the two Tory leaders should be as one. Yet they barely know each other and are in no hurry to get acquainted, Martin Regg Cohn writes.Steve RussellToronto Star

Document 423 of 500

Rae, Kinew and axing taxes: Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew has a winning formula. Progressive leaders should listen: Phillips, March 12 Why I think Picasso's masterpiece 'Guernica' is ultimately a failure: Mallick, March 11

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/rae-kinew-axing-taxes/docview/2957706339/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Rae%2C+Kinew+and+axing+taxes%3A+Manitoba+Premier+Wab+Kinew+has+a+winning+formula.+Progressive+leaders+should+listen%3A+Phillips%2C+March+12+Why+I+think+Picasso%27s+masterpiece+%27Guernica%27+is+ultimately+a+failure%3A+Mallick%2C+March+11&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: To achieve peace, all sides of a war must really want it
How Canada's most fiery envoy
found himself in the line of fire:
Cohn, March 8
Canadian UN ambassador Bob Rae is correct when he says "the ultimate responsibility for the conduct of war lies with the parties who are fighting." Equally, if not more important, is for all the demonstrators around the world to recognize that to have peace, both parties must want it. Demonstrators can demand peace until the end of time but there will be no peace until all sides want peace. They need to understand that they cannot force or wish people to get along.
Charles Campisi, Oakville

Kudos to columnist Martin Regg Cohn for shining a light on Canada's position and votes at the UN. Thanks to Bob Rae for holding the UN position with dignity and clearly stating that Canada supports a two state solution. The terrible situation in Gaza right now is the ultimate responsibility of both fighting sides, and the hatred and anger of demonstrators on both sides here in Canada is often without knowing what the conflict is even about. Rae helps us see what it is to be committed to peace and the immense difficulties faced in working for it.
Susan Turner, Guelph
Manitoba just the latest example that NDP ideas work
Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew
has a winning formula.
Progressive leaders should listen: Phillips, March 12
Tuesday was NDP Appreciation Day on the Opinion page of The Star. The third column, titled "Studies show that rent controls work" only sealed the deal: The NDP make excellent provincial governments, being socially progressive and rarely if ever mired in scandal.
The contrast between governments run by populists like Alberta's Danielle's Smith, Saskatchewan's Scott Moe and Ontario's Doug Ford couldn't be greater. Yet, if Bonnie Crombie's leadership takes off, Ontarians may once again see the popular vote split between the NDP and the Liberals, allowing Ford to achieve yet another majority government.
And lest you object by pointing to Bob Rae's NDP government, just remember that Rae increased social spending during tough economic times, and made some government workers go on periods of unpaid leave, colloquially known as "Rae Days." That move seems far from Draconian when one considers the actions of subsequent provincial governments.
I've always been a Liberal Democrat. If New Democrats and Liberals don't want to see progressive issues woefully underserved, they should amalgamate.
Ron Charach, Toronto
PM should axe tax to force an honest debate on climate
Trudeau ally joins Poilievre to demand a halt to the April 1 carbon levy increase, March 12
The Conservative Party of Canada and its Leader Pierre Poilievre have relentlessly attacked the carbon tax, and held Prime Minister Justin Trudeau responsible. Other provincial governments have joined the anti-carbon tax train, along with millions of voters. A conservative government will kill the tax, so why not suspend it now, develop a new climate plan to present to Canadians at the next election? It would ease some cost-of-living pain, and force a more productive policy debate on the type of climate plan that's best for Canada. The current situation is far too divisive and truly unhelpful, except to the CPC and their leader. Why not puncture their balloon and force their hand to develop a plan.
Desmond Pouyat, Toronto
Officials behind planning meeting need map of city
This week in the Star, the City of Toronto published a notice of public meeting pertaining to property at Islington and Bloor in Etobicoke. The meeting is in Scarborough. I emailed the city registrar and the clerk to ask for a change of venue. Only those who make an oral presentation have a right to appeal and Scarborough is a long way from Etobicoke. Although I received an apology, it is clear Toronto needs to give their employees a map.
Gail Rutherford, Etobicoke
Picasso used his art to truly capture the injustice and horror
Why I think Picasso's masterpiece 'Guernica' is ultimately a failure: Mallick, March 11
I was deeply moved by Heather Mallick's column, but I do not think Picasso failed. He didn't have a cellphone and used his art to brilliantly capture the injustice and horror. Today, with technology, we witness the horror inflicted on Gaza daily, but it is the powerful governments that support the genocide that are failing. I note that Gaza and Guernica share the first and last letters.
Elizabeth Hill, Toronto

Subject: Leadership; Peace; Carbon; Government; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership Environmental tax

Location: Manitoba Canada; Gaza Strip; Canada

People: Rae, Bob
