Here's how Canadians can pay their final respects to Brian Mulroney

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/heres-how-canadians-can-pay-their-final-respects/docview/2956231114/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here%27s+how+Canadians+can+pay+their+final+respects+to+Brian+Mulroney&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Canadians wishing to pay respects to former prime minister Brian Mulroney may do so when a four-day period of public mourning begins next week, starting in the nation's capital before moving to Quebec.
Amid ongoing security concerns about how protests around the war in Gaza are affecting public events, the federal government has scheduled two days for Mulroney's lying in state in Ottawa, beginning March 19.
Mulroney would have turned 85 on March 20.
The former prime minister's casket is to then be transported to Montreal, where another two days has been set aside, starting March 21, for Mulroney's lying in repose - the name given when the solemn formal event takes place outside of Ottawa.
Mulroney's state funeral will take place March 23 at the majestic Notre-Dame Basilica in old Montreal.
Parliament is currently adjourned and scheduled to resume on Monday, when MPs and party leaders are expected to deliver formal tributes.
The former Progressive Conservative party leader, who was treated for prostate cancer last year, died on Feb. 29 after a fall in Palm Beach, Florida, where he and his wife Mila had a vacation home.
His death occurred just before the start of a two-week constituency break, and so only brief tributes were offered on the morning after the news broke.
Further details about the state funeral, expected guests and speakers will be disclosed by the federal Heritage Department on Friday. Those details have been worked out with the family.
In Pickering, Premier Doug Ford said he and his Progressive Conservative caucus are working to ensure MPPs are able to attend the lying in state next week even though the legislature is sitting at Queen's Park.
The late prime minister's eldest child, Caroline Mulroney, is Ford's Treasury Board president and a key cabinet minister.
"We're going to work that out through our chair of caucus," the premier said Wednesday, adding he will be at Mulroney's funeral in Montreal.
"I'm going to be attending, our finance minister (Peter Bethlenfalvy) is going to be attending," he said.
"Our heart goes out to Caroline and the Mulroney family. He was a tremendous leader."
CAPTION: A framed portrait of former prime minister Brian Mulroney leans against the Centennial Flame on Parliament Hill on March 1, 2024.

Doug Ford claims union chief is 'lying' about him selling off the LCBO

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-claims-union-chief-is-lying-about-him/docview/2956230824/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+claims+union+chief+is+%27lying%27+about+him+selling+off+the+LCBO&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is accusing a major union leader of "lying" to members at the LCBO - who are in contract negotiations - that the government is planning to sell the liquor store to private interests.
But JP Hornick, president of the powerful Ontario Public Service Employees Union, says the premier is trying to paper over a gradual privatization of the Crown-owned booze retailer that has already seen warehousing contracted out and jobs lost.
"Doug Ford can't be trusted," Hornick said Wednesday. "Remember, these are the same guys that were trying to sell off the Greenbelt."
Ford's accusation came after unionized LCBO workers held rallies outside his Etobicoke North constituency office and in 10 other cities on Tuesday to draw attention to their concerns.
"We're going to stand together and do whatever it takes to stop Doug Ford from selling off the LCBO and to protect good union jobs in every community across Ontario," said Hornick.
That is not true, Ford insisted in Pickering as LCBO contract talks began.
"Their leader is actually misleading and, let me be more blunt, lying to their members about us closing the LCBO," the premier told reporters, noting that Hornick is "up for re-election in April."
"We will never, ever sell the LCBO. Matter of fact, they're going to be the exclusive wholesaler for when we do the Beer Store," the premier said in reference to the expiry of the province's master agreement with the Beer Store next year.
That will clear the way for beer, wine, cider and ready-to-drink cocktails to be sold in convenience stores in addition to grocery and big box stores no later than Jan. 1, 2026, with all the products to be distributed by the LCBO.
"They're always going to be selling the spirits," he added, referring to liquor such as whisky, gin, rum and vodka on LCBO shelves that cannot be sold in supermarkets, big box or convenience stores even as Ontario eases restrictions on the sale of beverage alcohol.
"You have a stable job as long as we're in office," Ford said to LCBO workers.
Hornick said the government's long-term strategy for the liquor retailer is "death by 1,000 cuts," which is why the union is trying to protect members' jobs and the $2.5 billion in profits the LCBO puts into provincial coffers annually to help pay the cost of public services like health care and education.
"LCBO workers are organized like never before, and they're ready to fight for the future of the LCBO."
Liquor store workers won an arbitration award last weekend for what their union calls "three years of lost wages" after Ford's ill-fated public sector wage restraint legislation, Bill 124, was twice struck down by Ontario courts as unconstitutional for restricting collective bargaining.
The award adds 6.5 per cent to the wage grid.
Ford's government announced last month it would abandon the legislation and not appeal to the Supreme Court of Canada.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks during a press conference in Milton, Ont., on March 8, 2024.

Subject: Liquor stores; Beer; Convenience stores; Collective bargaining; Contract negotiations

Document 452 of 500

The partisan fury over prison transfers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/partisan-fury-over-prison-transfers/docview/2956230518/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+partisan+fury+over+prison+transfers&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ottawa's controversial transfers of high-profile inmates raises plenty of legitimate concerns but the federal Conservatives' chief criticism isn't one of them.
The issue first arose last year, when notorious murderer and rapist Paul Bernardo was quietly transferred from the maximum security Millhaven penitentiary to the medium security La Macaza Institution.
Ottawa's secretive approach to the transfer was particularly galling: Although the law permits the Correctional Service of Canada to inform victims of the reasons for the transfer, the service refused to provide them to Tim Danson, who represents the families of the girls murdered by Bernardo.
Danson was instead only informed of the transfer after it happened, which suggests that the service values offenders' privacy rights over the rights of victims and their families.
Last week, the issue was back in the news, with the Toronto Sun reporting that Bernardo wasn't the first high-profile inmate subject to a clandestine transfer. Luka Magnotta, who recorded and posted online his brutal murder of international student Jun Lin, was transferred to La Macaza nine months before Bernardo, yet we're only learning about it nearly two years after the fact.
The Lin family supposedly learned of the transfer earlier, as the Correctional Service told Global News that victims "receive timely notification of matters such as transfers." But there's no evidence they were treated any better than the families of Bernardo's victims.
Fortunately, the Bernardo affair led to a review of the transfer decision, which concluded that the service could engage in "more proactive and meaningful discussions with victims," and it recommended establishing a committee to facilitate that engagement.
Marco Mendicino, who was public safety minister at the time, added that engagement should occur before transfers are made, and to that end he directed the service to consider an updated victim impact statement as part of the transfer decision-making process.
These are positive developments, but with the Magnotta case reigniting the controversy, it's time for a broader discussion of the transfer process, including how we can better balance offenders' and victims' rights and restore the public's confidence in the justice system.
The federal Conservatives could and should be part of this conversation, but they have instead chosen to foment outrage by claiming that prisons have become carefree country clubs under Liberal policies, especially Bill C-83. The bill, which was passed in 2019 and designed to end solitary confinement, mandated that inmates be housed in the "least restrictive environment" consistent with protecting the public and prison staff and inmates.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre charged that Bill C-83 was responsible for the Bernardo transfer. That charges was directly contradicted by a spokesperson for Public Safety Canada who told the Canadian Press that the transfer would have occurred under the pre-Bill C-83 version of the law, which permitted the Correctional Service to impose "necessary restrictions" on inmates.
If that weren't enough, the "least restrictive" standard wasn't dreamt up by the Liberals. It was, in fact, included when the law -- the Corrections and Conditional Release Act -- was first passed in 1992 by Brian Mulroney's Progressive Conservative government.
In 2012, Stephen Harper's Conservative government amended the law with the "necessary restrictions" wording, which means Bill C-83 merely restored the language introduced by a conservative government.
And if that still weren't enough, consider this: Inmates don't suddenly lose their rights when they pass through the prison entrance. Certainly, imprisonment results in limitations on certain rights, but the Constitution places the onus on the government to prove those limitations impair -- or restrict -- the rights as little as possible.
The "least restrictive" principle is therefore derived from the Constitution, and is firmly embedded in Canadian law, including in roughly 70 statutes and regulations. That alone is good reason to maintain the standard, especially when there's no evidence it was responsible for the Bernardo or Magnotta transfers.
Nonetheless, much as he did with the Bernardo transfer, Poilievre was quick to blame the Magnotta transfer on Bill C-83. That might improve his political fortunes, but it won't help victims or anyone else.
The only thing that will is an honest, sincere discussion about improving the transfer process. And all parties, including the Conservatives, owe it to Canadians to participate.
CAPTION: The issue of prison transfers was back in the spotlight after Conservative MP Frank Caputo complained that notorious inmates are allowed play "taxpayer funded serial killer pick-up hockey."

Subject: Conservatism; Prisons; Public safety; Prisoners; Murders & murder attempts; Constitutional amendments

Title: The partisan fury over prison transfers

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Mar 13, 2024

Section: opinion/editorials

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Fixing prison transfer process

So why is Toronto set to spend less on making streets safer? Is it OK to pull your kid out of school to travel? Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Pedestrian+and+cyclist+injuries+and+deaths+are+up.+So+why+is+Toronto+set+to+spend+less+on+making+streets+safer%3F+Pedestrian+and+cyclist+injuries+and+deaths+are+up.+So+why+is+Toronto+set+to+spend+less+on+making+streets+safer%3F+Is+it+OK+to+pull+your+kid+out+of+school+to+travel%3F+Here%27s+what+parents+and+teachers+have+to+say%2C+Is+it+OK+to+pull+your+kid+out+of+school+to+travel%3F+Here%27s+what+parents+and+teachers+have+to+say%2C&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Cyclists must
be seen by drivers in order to be safe
Pedestrian and cyclist injuries and deaths are up. So why is Toronto set to spend less on making streets safer? March 12
I believe that road safety measures must include a campaign to change the behaviour of cyclists and pedestrians, specifically what they wear. Whenever I drive in the city I am struck by the fact that most cyclists and pedestrians, day and night, do not wear highly visible clothing or gear. They are virtually invisible to drivers at night. This should change.
Jacques Soucie, Newmarket
Poor planning leaves many on the road unsafe
Pedestrian and cyclist injuries and deaths are up. So why is Toronto set to spend less on making streets safer? March 12
Columnist Matt Elliott is right that cyclists need to be protected. However, inept planning has created dangerous situations for everyone. We all need to demand better.
First is the congestion. In Etobicoke, a representative from a fire hall says that they were not consulted on the bike lanes. On their first run after installation, a short run took almost 30 minutes because cars couldn't pull over. Now they must weave in and out of the narrow sidewalkless residential Etobicoke streets. They now file reports on inadequate response times on a regular basis. Bloor is a major arterial road, and now much more limited for vehicle traffic. Cars use the narrow side streets, putting school kids and seniors are at particular risk. People with disabilities can't park near shops and restaurants. Business is down. Café TO is at risk.
Yes, we must consider cyclists, but we must factor the interests of all into planning.
Gail Rutherford, Etobicoke
The City of Toronto found that spending $29 million this year on "projects designed to make streets safer" was too much and plans to reduce it down to $5 million a year after 2028. Meanwhile Premier Doug Ford plans to spend at least $6 billion to $10 billion on Highway 413, which is arguably unnecessary and will cut through the Greenbelt. When Ford was a city councillor, he was clear that he wanted to stop the "war on the cars." It is going to be hard to consider cyclist infrastructure and safety with him in Queen's Park. To quote Leonard Cohen: "Everybody knows the war is over, everybody knows the good guys lost."
Moses Shuldiner, Toronto
Kids have lost enough school
to the pandemic
Is it OK to pull your kid out of school to travel? Here's what parents and teachers have to say, March 10
While the ultimate choice rests with parents, the expectation is that students attend school, especially on the heels of a pandemic that hindered so much learning. When students miss school or are unable to make up for the lost time, the students who are struggling the most will be impacted, their marks will decline both on their tests and the EQAO scores and the onus might fall on the teacher for not doing enough to help students overcome their challenges and improve their marks.
Robert Ariano, Scarborough
Travelling can teach you things that school can't
Is it OK to pull your kid out of school to travel? Here's what parents and teachers have to say, March 10
It was 1964 and my parents were planning to take a Toronto to California road trip to visit friends over the Christmas holidays. They asked Mr. Tompson, my then Grade 12 home room chemistry teacher at York Memorial Collegiate Institute, if he thought it would be OK for me to be absent from school for an extended week to ten days. He agreed and assured that I would learn more on that trip than I would at school. He was so right! The memories of that trip have been everlasting, including seeing the Chicago skyline, sharing the driving of our 1950 Chevy down Route 66 from the start to the end, Meramec Caverns (Jesse James' hideout) in Missouri, buying cowboy hats at a roadside trading post in the Texas panhandle, eating grits for the first time, crossing the Continental Divide, coming closer and closer to Arizona's more than 12,000-ft.-high snow-topped San Francisco Peaks which we could already first see from 100 miles away, the Petrified Forest and Meteor Crater in Arizona, the Grand Canyon, the engineering marvel of Hoover Dam, Las Vegas, and finally the Santa Monica Pier and Pacific Ocean. The memories are as fresh now as though we just took the trip. Clearly, I learned a lot more than I would have at school.
Hans Zander, Toronto
With Poilievre's tactics, Trudeau must step aside
One thing that former U.S. president Donald Trump has proven is that lying to the public and uttering outrageous statements has worked in his favour. The TV news stations, newspapers, streaming outlets all report every word, every syllable and in the end, they have promoted Trump's profile to the extent that he is the most (in)famous politician in the world. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has learned that lesson very well and following Trump, he has promoted his name throughout the country relying on outrageous statements and exaggeration. He blames Prime Minister Justin Trudeau for everything and polls show his strategy is working.
Despite a reasonable record on many policies, Trudeau is receiving little credit from the majority of Canadians. Former premier Kathleen Wynne faced a similar situation here in Ontario and instead of listening to those who recommended a different leader, she faced the electorate one more time and the rest is history. Trudeau should step down and give the Liberal party a new look, different policies and an opportunity to refresh their message and hopefully get a sizable minority in Canada despite Poilievre and his attack dog tactics. Let's hope that Trudeau has the political courage to step aside for the good of Canada and our future.
David Gladstone, Toronto
In a recent rally, Pierre Poilievre described life under his government as including children skipping to school without fearing for their safety, seniors waving at friendly shopkeepers, parents calling their road-hockey-playing kids to bed, a young couple sitting on the porch "soaking in the warm evening air, with a Canadian flag gently hanging from the front of their brand new home." Perhaps he should change his name to Pollyanna.
David Greenberg, Toronto
Current ads by the federal Conservative party tells me that "Canada is broken." Meanwhile, ads by the Ontario Conservative party tells me that this is the place to live where all dreams can come true. Who to believe? I'm truly confused. Or are they both disingenuous?
Stephen Bloom, Toronto
CREDIT: ;

Document 455 of 500

Poilievre launches attack on carbon tax hike: N.L. Liberal premier joins calls to pause scheduled increase

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievre-launches-attack-on-carbon-tax-hike/docview/2956057050/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+launches+attack+on+carbon+tax+hike%3A+N.L.+Liberal+premier+joins+calls+to+pause+scheduled+increase&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives are launching a full-court political press against the Trudeau Liberals' April 1 carbon price increase - and there's already a Liberal premier signing on.
Newfoundland and Labrador's Andrew Furey - an ally of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau - added his voice Tuesday to Poilievre's demand that the Liberals pause the planned increase, a request also made in the last week by Ontario's Doug Ford, P.E.I.'s Dennis King and New Brunswick's Blaine Higgs, all conservatives.
They all argue going ahead with the scheduled increase to the levy asks too much of Canadians already feeling the pinch from higher living costs.
"In Newfoundland and Labrador, for the most vulnerable, it has been a particularly difficult time," Furey wrote in a letter to Trudeau released Tuesday. "After years of increased living costs, there is a need to fully grasp and address the magnitude of inflation's effect on citizens, particularly those most in need."
Poilievre's current push began at a massive rally in Etobicoke on Sunday under the banner of "Spike the Hike." Coming next: a new ad campaign on radio and television, events outside Liberal and New Democrat MP offices, numerous rallies across the country and debates in the House of Commons to press their case between now and April 1, a Conservative source told the Star, speaking confidentially to discuss strategy.
Poilievre urged supporters to join the cause, calling on them to sign his party's petition, organize protests at constituency offices and blast their own email and social media networks to get the word out.
"Many of you will say, 'Oh, that'll never make a difference,' " he said at the rally.
"Well, if you think that you're too small to be effective, you've never been in a tent with a mosquito."
As of April 1, the federal levy placed on gasoline and natural gas will rise by $15 per tonne, adding 3.3 cents to a litre of gasoline and about 2.9 cents to a cubic metre of natural gas.
The Liberals say placing a consumer price on carbon encourages Canadians to limit their use of fossil fuels to save money, and in turn help reduce the emissions that cause climate change.
The Liberal government promised to reduce emissions by 40 per cent to 45 per cent below 2005 levels by 2030, and has said that carbon pollution pricing will contribute to as much as one-third of that goal.
The Canadian Climate Institute has estimated that removing the fuel charge altogether would result in an emissions increase of 13.5 megatonnes in 2030, further hampering the country's ability to meet the 2030 targets, which the CCI and others have already said Canada is not on track to do.
Residents of B.C., Quebec and the Northwest Territories are exempt from the federal levy, as those regions have their own programs.
For residents elsewhere, the federal government cuts a cheque to cover the additional costs of the levy, but the debate continues to rage over whether those payments - also going up in April - are sufficient to cover the costs of the additional charge.
The latest political skirmish comes in the wake of the Liberals making recent changes to the program, driven in part by Poilievre's pressure. Those changes include removing the carbon price on home heating oil and increasing the rebate rural Canadians receive.
The changes also flowed from Liberals, many from Atlantic Canada who were getting earfuls from constituents over their high bills.
Newfoundland and Labrador's Furey, who endorsed Trudeau in the last election, had also asked the prime minister to remove the carbon price on heating oil.
Those twin changes to the program were then followed by a third: a rebrand of the rebate, an acknowledgment that many Canadians don't realize what it is.
In response to Furey on Tuesday, a spokesperson for Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland did not directly answer the Star's question about whether they would entertain a pause, instead pointing out the carbon tax rebate helps Canadians with affordability issues.
Her office also did not offer a response to Poilievre's call for protests and other actions to fight the increase.
Conservatives and Liberals have sparred over carbon pricing for more than a decade.
While the Liberals have since made slashing greenhouse gas emissions a signature piece of their policy agenda, Poilievre in turn has made a promise to end consumer carbon pricing a signature piece of his, making it a centrepiece of his leadership bid for the party.
He has said he believes in climate change, and that emissions cause it, pledging he'd reduce them by focusing on technology. He's not said exactly by how much he'd reduce emissions.
Now, as he prepares to hit 18 months in the job, the new anti-carbon-tax campaign marks a fresh phase of his leadership: moving from introducing himself to voters to finding ways to mobilize them to stick by his side on the path to the next election.
With some $35 million raised last year alone, the Tories are pouring money into voter identification and motivation efforts, buying ads, billboards and hosting events to help drive the Poilievre brand, and a connection to it - in addition to tossing quips into the audience in Etobicoke, Poilievre also tossed an "Axe the tax" T-shirt into the crowd.
By encouraging his supporters in Etobicoke to sign petitions and engage on social media, for example - a tactic likely to be repeated at rallies over the next several days - he creates the opportunity to harvest the names and social media profiles of an untold number of potential new voters who can be targeted in the future.
"Politics is not a spectator sport," he told them. "It is a participation sport. If you're not at the table, you're on the menu. So you need to be at the table."
CREDIT: Stephanie Levitz Deputy Toronto Star

Subject: Canadians; Voters; Emissions; Carbon; Prime ministers; Climate change; Social networks; Natural gas; Tax increases; Environmental tax

Document 456 of 500

Ford's finance minister hints at extension of gas tax break: Province's temporary cut of 5.7 cents per litre set to expire on June 30

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-finance-minister-hints-at-extension-gas-tax/docview/2956057045/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+finance+minister+hints+at+extension+of+gas+tax+break%3A+Province%27s+temporary+cut+of+5.7+cents+per+litre+set+to+expire+on+June+30&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Read his lips: no new taxes.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy is signalling an extension of the Ford government's temporary cut to gas and fuel taxes is looming in the March 26 budget.
In a wide-ranging discussion with the Star editorial board on Tuesday, Bethlenfalvy said the impact of inflation is top of mind as he prepares the provincial spending plan.
"I'm not going to betray what may or may not be in the budget. I know we've been very focused on helping people with the cost of living, which includes the gas tax cut," the treasurer said.
"Clearly, that's not just driving kids to school or to work, but it's part of the whole supply chain," he said, emphasizing how the savings can impact a variety of goods and services.
First announced by Premier Doug Ford before the 2022 election - and twice extended - the 5.7 cents per litre gasoline tax cut is currently supposed to expire on June 30.
But Bethlenfalvy acknowledged "it would be tough" to hike the provincial fuel tax just three months after the federal carbon levy rises on April 1.
"I'm not looking to increase any taxes or fees," he said.
The tax break costs the treasury about $1.2 billion annually and saves the average family around $260 a year.
While bullish on Ontario's prospects, Bethlenfalvy stressed, "there's no question that our economy has slowed down."
"I hope that we don't go into recession technically," the finance minister said, noting that is why the province maintains significant contingency funds that opposition parties have criticized.
"It has an impact on revenue so there's no question - notwithstanding that the technical definition of a recession is two back-to-back quarters (of a shrinking economy), which we haven't seen," he said, pointing to Ontario's close ties to the booming U.S.
"People really feel like it's a recession everywhere I go. The U.K. is in recession. Japan is in recession ... (but) south of the border, of course, they keep beating expectations on the upside."
In Pollara Strategic Insights' annual economic outlook published in the Star in January, four in five respondents said they think Canada had already been plunged into a recession.
Bethlenfalvy said he understood why that sentiment is prevalent even though the province's economy is performing better than most jurisdictions, thanks to encouraging job growth and more investment from businesses.
"You talk to a lot of people and they're hurting. The price of food - I think we'd all would agree, wow, when you go to the grocery store, you're getting hit hard," he said.
The finance minister said he is also worried about the Bank of Canada's interest rates because of the toll that takes on people's mortgages.
With more than two years before the June 2026 election, Bethlenfalvy was mum on when the Progressive Conservatives would finally balance the budget.
In his fall economic statement last November, he had said the 2023-24 deficit would be $5.6 billion with the shortfall projected to be $5.3 billion in 2024-25.
At that time, the minister said Ontario would be back in the black with a $500-million surplus in 2025-26, in time for the next provincial vote.
That was a more gloomy picture than last March's budget, which had forecast a $200-million surplus this year and for the treasury to have a $4.4-billion surplus in 2025-26.
The change was largely due to tax revenues being $2.6 billion lower than expected - a sign the economy was slowing after it rebounded from the COVID-19 pandemic.
CAPTION: Ontario Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy told the Star's editorial board Tuesday that the impact of inflation is top of mind as he prepares the provincial spending plan.Andrew Francis WallaceToronto Star
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Justin Trudeau ally joins Pierre Poilievre to demand a halt to the April 1 carbon levy increase

Newfoundland and Labrador's Andrew Furey - an ally of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau - added his voice Tuesday to Poilievre's demand the Liberals pause the planned increase, a request also made in the last week by Ontario's Doug Ford, P.E.I.'s Dennis King and New Brunswick's Blaine Higgs, all conservatives.

Document 458 of 500

Ford's finance minister hints at extension of gas tax break

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/fords-finance-minister-hints-at-extension-gas-tax/docview/2956403679/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+finance+minister+hints+at+extension+of+gas+tax+break&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Read his lips: no new taxes.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy is signalling an extension of the Ford government's temporary cut to gas and fuel taxes is looming in the March 26 budget.

"People really feel like it's a recession everywhere I go. The U.K. is in recession. Japan is in recession ... (but) south of the border, of course, they keep beating expectations on the upside."
In Pollara Strategic Insights' annual economic outlook published in the Star in January, four in five respondents said they think Canada had already been plunged into a recession.
Bethlenfalvy said he understood why that sentiment is prevalent even though the province's economy is performing better than most jurisdictions, thanks to encouraging job growth and more investment from businesses.
"You talk to a lot of people and they're hurting. The price of food - I think we'd all would agree, wow, when you go to the grocery store, you're getting hit hard," he said.
The finance minister said he is also worried about the Bank of Canada's interest rates because of the toll that takes on people's mortgages.
With more than two years before the June 2026 election, Bethlenfalvy was mum on when the Progressive Conservatives would finally balance the budget.
In his fall economic statement last November, he had said the 2023-24 deficit would be $5.6 billion with the shortfall projected to be $5.3 billion in 2024-25.
At that time, the minister said Ontario would be back in the black with a $500-million surplus in 2025-26, in time for the next provincial vote.
That was a more gloomy picture than last March's budget, which had forecast a $200-million surplus this year and for the treasury to have a $4.4-billion surplus in 2025-26.
The change was largely due to tax revenues being $2.6 billion lower than expected - a sign the economy was slowing after it rebounded from the COVID-19 pandemic.
CAPTION: Ontario Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy meets with the Toronto Star editorial board on March 12, 2024.

Subject: Budgets; Tax cuts; Gasoline taxes; Economic conditions; Recessions

Business indexing term: Subject: Budgets Tax cuts Gasoline taxes Economic conditions Recessions

Document 459 of 500

Kate Middleton's photoshop flop can't be retouched. We asked the Star's expert photo team to weigh in

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/kate-middletons-photoshop-flop-cant-be-retouched/docview/2956403601/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Kate+Middleton%27s+photoshop+flop+can%27t+be+retouched.+We+asked+the+Star%27s+expert+photo+team+to+weigh+in&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A disappearing sweater cuff. A blurry right hand. A misaligned zipper.
The list of inconsistencies in a Mother's Day photo released by Kensington Palace of Kate Middleton with children George, Charlotte and Louis is endless. The photo, since pulled by the Associated Press, Reuters and others for apparent manipulation, has consumed the world as Middleton has not made an official public appearance since Christmas.
We asked the Star's visuals team about the Royal Family photo. Here's what they said.
What are the inconsistencies?
The most noticeable alteration is the cuff of Princess Charlotte's red sweater, which disappears above her hand. Charlotte's skirt also juts out from under her sweater. Additionally, Middleton's right hand is blurry while Prince Louis' green sweater, where her hand rests, is in focus.
Steve Russell, a staff photographer at the Star, identified other inconsistencies.
When the image is brightened, a line appears on Middleton's upper body at the same point where the zipper of her jacket doesn't line up. The right side of Middleton's face, including her eye and hair, is in focus, while the hair on the left side is out of focus.
"Looking at the way this picture was shot, it should be all one thing," Russell said.
Why was the photo manipulated?
Taras Slawnych, the Star's visuals editor, suspects the photo is a composite of multiple photos taken at the same time.
"My best guess ... (is) it was a composite shot of several different layers, several different photos," Slawnych said. "I don't think it was done ... maliciously to hide something. It was more like to make everyone look good."
Slawnych also suspects the photos weren't taken with a tripod, helping explain why some parts don't line up.
Did Kate actually edit the photo?
In a post to X, Middleton said she experiments with photo editing and apologized for "any confusion the family photograph we shared yesterday caused."
"It's such a bad Photoshop that I think she did do it," Slawnych said. "If they would've hired a Photoshop expert, there's no way you would have seen these."
Is killing a photo rare?
The Associated Press kills photos at least once a month, Slawnych said - but not because they've been manipulated. Instead, most kills are because sources remove ownership of the photos or other similar issues.
"Kills because of photo manipulation are very rare," he explained.
Manipulation violates the policy of AP, the Star and other publications. Only simple modifications - like cropping, brightening, darkening, changing contrast and correcting colour - are permitted under Star guidelines, Slawnych said.
"If a Star photographer did something like this, this would be a firing offence," he said. "If all of a sudden Steve went out there and took a picture of a (Doug) Ford and added something to that, then who would believe us the next time? It's just that serious of a problem for us."
Can people still trust provided photos?
Publications, including the Star, already try to avoid provided photos, Russell said.
"If I go out and shoot an image, you know, the Star has trust in me," he explained. "They're getting it directly from the source. When stuff gets shot for governments and the royals and all that, we can't track what happened to the image."
The Middleton photo scandal only adds to that, and speculation of a manipulated family photo from Christmas has kicked up again.
It all feeds the flame of conspiracy theories around the Princess.
"While journalism gets a black eye from this," Russell said, "Kensington Palace is getting two black eyes and a bloody nose."
CAPTION: The Middleton Mother's Day photo scandal is fuelling speculation of a manipulated family photo from Christmas.The photo, since pulled by the Associated Press, Reuters and others for apparent manipulation, has consumed the world as Middleton has not made an official public appearance since Christmas.

Document 460 of 500

'It makes no sense': Trudeau government promised rural residents a carbon rebate top up - so why aren't these people getting it?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/makes-no-sense-trudeau-government-promised-rural/docview/2955212676/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27It+makes+no+sense%27%3A+Trudeau+government+promised+rural+residents+a+carbon+rebate+top+up+-+so+why+aren%27t+these+people+getting+it%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: MONO, Ont.-It might be the two provincial parks, the ski resort and the 278 square kilometres of rivers, hills and agricultural land. Or the fact it has more cell-service dead zones than public transit options or hospitals.
To Mayor John Creelman, however, the constant debate over whether to pave the roads of Mono, Ont. or keep them gravel is the biggest indicator of just how rural the town of 9,000 is.
But when it comes to the federal government's carbon levy and rebates, it's not rural enough. Mono, about an hour's drive from Toronto on a good day and even longer on the bus from nearby Orangeville - gets grouped into the Toronto area, making its residents ineligible for the cash Ottawa has set aside to help rural Canadians with the costs of the carbon levy.
Faced with heavy criticism over their carbon pricing program, the federal Liberals have been putting more emphasis on the quarterly rebates the program gives to Canadians - and the extra cash doled out to those living in rural areas. It's all part of an effort to repaint the price on pollution as an affordability measure that leaves Canadians better off. But not all rural Canadians impacted by the carbon levy, which aims to disincentivize people from emitting harmful greenhouse gases, are eligible for the rural top-up.
That's because since the introduction of the rural top-up, the federal government has used the boundaries of census metropolitan areas (CMA) - a tool created by Statistics Canada that groups densely populated centres, like the Greater Toronto Area, with closely-linked nearby municipalities - to define which Canadians are "rural." Even StatCan acknowledges you can still be living in a rural area if you are within CMA boundaries.
Mono just makes it into the Toronto CMA. But its neighbours and other Dufferin County municipalities, except for Orangeville and its 34,000 residents, fall outside of the CMA and are eligible to receive the extra cash.
"I can see Mono out my window, and we can get it and they can't," said Diana Morris, the executive director of the Dufferin Board of Trade. "There's no difference between the needs of my family living in Mulmur or the needs of a family living in Mono."
Six million Canadians live outside of CMAs and qualify for the rural top-up, with about 2.5 million in Ontario. But just over 500,000 Ontarians live in rural areas within CMAs, like Mono, and are denied the extra cash. For a family of four in the province, it adds up to an extra $224 this year.
"That's $224 less that you have to spend on anything your family needs," Creelman said.
Government officials defended the use of CMAs, arguing it's a "clearly defined and well-understood criterion" and that the rebates put more money back into the pockets of most Canadians.
"It also guarantees that Canadians are treated in a consistent manner across the country," said Katherine Cuplinskas, press secretary for Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland.
But looking at his propane bill, Creelman is not so sure. The last time he filled up his tank, he got charged an extra $131.49 for a "carbon tax," nearly the same amount as the HST charge. Since the carbon fee applies to all polluting fuels, the cost to producers gets passed down to consumers when they fill up their cars with gas or fill a propane tank. Until recently, Creelman said he didn't even know rural Canadians should be getting extra cash, and it has weighed on his mind ever since he found out, he said.
He said Mono's geography and infrastructure makes its residents car-dependent, forcing many people to drive to the Toronto area for work. It's that relationship that sees it lumped into the Toronto CMA.
"It disturbs me to think that somebody in an office in Ottawa just decided that this was the best way to distribute the rural top-up, with no consultation and no discussion with us," he told the Star. "It's completely arbitrary. It makes no sense."
Because of these inconsistencies, Scot Davidson, the Conservative MP for nearby York-Simcoe, says the Liberals' approach to carbon pricing has been politically charged. While Liberal strongholds in the Atlantic Provinces, like the cities of Fredericton and Charlottetown, classify as rural and get the top-ups, his constituents get the same rebates those in downtown Toronto do. (The rebate amount given to residents of Ontario still exceeds any of the Atlantic Provinces.)
His riding contains the Chippewas of Georgina Island First Nation, an Indigenous reserve accessible to land by ferry only. But when it comes to the carbon rebates, it too counts as part of Toronto.
After a heavy push from Liberal Atlantic MPs last fall, the federal government announced a temporary exemption of home-heating oil from the carbon price, while doubling the rural top-up to 20 per cent from 10. It drew fury from across the country with premiers and First Nations demanding fairness, and gave ammo to a Conservative campaign to "axe the tax." Now, the program as a whole is under threat by Saskatchewan, which is threatening to stop gathering the federal levy on all fossil fuels.
The doubling of the rural top-up also meant the Liberals had to decrease the amount of money set aside for other groups, like small businesses. The Canadian Federation of Independent Business says Ottawa owes businesses $2.5 billion. To find out now that the money is not actually going to all rural communities is a "double whammy," and a "kick in the teeth," said Greg Wildeboer, a landscaper working in Dufferin County.
For the Tories, it's just another reason why the federal price on carbon needs to go.
"As Conservatives, our plan is to axe the carbon tax," Davidson told the Star. "But in the meantime, I want my constituents to get everything owed to them."
Speaking about the inconsistencies in the rural rebates, the conversation among Creelman and other professionals in Mono and the surrounding areas quickly turns to questioning the premise of carbon pricing at a time of high inflation. Bill McCutcheon, a local farmer and chairperson of the Dufferin Federation of Agriculture, spoke candidly about his attempts to invest in more climate friendly business practices. The money to invest, he said, is simply not there.
"My hands are tied," he said. "We have no alternatives."
Nearby, Creelman sat quietly, thinking about the outrage the people of Mono will feel when they find out why they've been missing out on the extra cash. Maybe, he wonders out loud, if they were acknowledged as the rural town they know they are, some of the economic pressures on the town could ease.
CAPTION: John Creelman, mayor of the town of Mono, stands along Hockley Road. His town is not considered rural under the rules used to decide who gets extra carbon rebate money.

Subject: Rebates; Canadians; Rural areas; Carbon; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Rebates Environmental tax

Title: 'It makes no sense': Trudeau government promised rural residents a carbon rebate top up - so why aren't these people getting it?

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Mar 12, 2024

Section: politics/federal

Rural, but not rural enough: Town questions exclusion from federal rebate scheme

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/rural-not-enough/docview/2955212648/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Rural%2C+but+not+rural+enough%3A+Town+questions+exclusion+from+federal+rebate+scheme&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 462 of 500

Nenshi could help lift Alberta NDP

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/nenshi-could-help-lift-alberta-ndp/docview/2955212593/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nenshi+could+help+lift+Alberta+NDP&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Alberta NDP leadership race got a much-needed shot of adrenalin today when former Calgary mayor, Naheed Nenshi, confirmed all the rumours and announced that he is in to win.
A three-term mayor and a formidable opponent to anyone who takes him on, Nenshi hasn't exactly been known as a leftist, in fact he used to pride himself on being non-partisan. That's why purple was always his campaign colour: a mix of red and blue. But not a hint of orange, the Alberta NDP's preferred hue.
This has left traditional NDP supporters and leadership candidates in a bit of a huff because they see him as an outsider who has yet to earn his wings in the trenches of party politics.
But sometimes outsiders are just what a political party needs to take flight.
And there is no better example than Ralph Klein one of the most popular premiers in Alberta history.
Klein was also a three-term mayor of Calgary before he won a Calgary constituency for the ruling Alberta Progressive Conservatives in 1989 and immediately landed in cabinet. Three years later he won the PC leadership and the key to the premier's office by beating prominent PC insiders.
But before Klein joined the PCs, who at the time had lost their Lougheed lustre, he was not known as a conservative. He was a big spender as mayor. By his own admission he had changed his party colours many times: he had held Social Credit, Liberal and PC party cards.
He had been touted as the man to harvest southern Alberta for the Liberals.
And yet Klein introduced a ruthless neo-conservative agenda that slashed health care and post-secondary education by 20 per cent. Thousands of public sector works were laid off. Welfare rolls were cut in half over one year.The Calgary General Hospital was completely demolished in order to cut spending on health-care costs.
Despite the hardships suffered by many Albertans, Klein remained popular. And he became even more popular when oilsands development lured billions of dollars of investment to the province.
He certainly proved that outsiders aren't necessarily destined to fail at party politics.
Whether he wins or loses, there's no question that Nenshi will shake up the NDP leadership campaign. So far it's been rather a placid affair. Four of the candidates are prominent NDP MLAs: Kathleen Ganley, Sarah Hoffman, Rakhi Pancholi, and Jodi Calahoo Stonehouse. Gil McGowan, the outspoken, combative president of the Alberta Federation of Labour is also running.
At this point, it's doubtful any of these candidates could return the NDP to power. But Rachel Notley is a hard act to follow. She's dynamic, smart and a strong campaigner who seems to feel at home anywhere in the province. She won the 2015 election and came awfully close to winning again last year.
Nenshi could be a valuable asset for the party, particularly in Calgary and smaller cities where the NDP need to make gains if they are to form government again. He also has the kind of bravado needed to take on Premier Danielle Smith and her radical agenda.
He was on fire when he spoke at a recent rally protesting Smith's proposed legislation that would compel the outing of transgender students and forbid certain medical treatments that they can currently access.
"I hate that what we heard for seven minutes was cruelty ... lies," he told hundreds of people outside Calgary City Hall. He called Smith's stance "inhumane," "un-Albertan," and dangerous.
Just before last year's election Nenshi announced that he would be "lending" his vote to the NDP because Smith is an "existential threat" to the future of the province.
Given that the Alberta NDP is more centrist than leftist this wasn't a huge leap for someone who brags about preferring purple - a mix of conservative and liberal values.
Now he just has to convince other Albertans that an NDP membership and a vote for him could eventually turn the whole province purple.
Gillian Steward is a
Calgary-based writer and
freelance contributing columnist for the Star. Follow her on Twitter: @GillianSteward.
CAPTION: While former Calgary mayor Naheed Nenshi hasn't been known as a leftist, sometimes outsiders are just what a political party needs to take flight, writes Gillian Steward.Todd Korol photo
CREDIT: Gillian Steward OPINION Gillian Steward is a Calgary-based writer and freelance contributing columnist for the Star. Follow her on Twitter: @GillianSteward.;

Document 463 of 500

Letters: Poilievre's 'Axe the Tax' rally draws thousands of supporters to Toronto Congress Centre, March 10 Why it's time to end the pointless and dangerous charade of daylight saving time: Menon, March 7 Votto says he has agreed to minor league contract with hometown Toronto Blue Jays, March 8

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/2955212555/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Poilievre%27s+%27Axe+the+Tax%27+rally+draws+thousands+of+supporters+to+Toronto+Congress+Centre%2C+March+10+Why+it%27s+time+to+end+the+pointless+and+dangerous+charade+of+daylight+saving+time%3A+Menon%2C+March+7+Votto+says+he+has+agreed+to+minor+league+contract+with+hometown+Toronto+Blue+Jays%2C+March+8&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Unnecessary noise hurts CFL game experience
We have stopped going to CFL games for good after paying for seats and having some sort of giant drum fans pounding headaches into your brain in the same section.
After paying lots of cash, perhaps I should bring in an air horn and sit behind these drum majors. No wonder the attendance of professional sporting events in Canada is declining when all sorts of extra uncalled for noise is making watching a game or listening to play by play on your headphones hopeless.
Wayne Robertson, Chatham, Ont.
Axe the tax?
Axe the smoke and mirrors
Poilievre's 'Axe the Tax' rally draws thousands of supporters to Toronto Congress Centre, March 10
Reading coverage of federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's weekend rally and seeing comments from readers, I see there seemed to be a general consensus that Poilievre is "one of them" and understands what the working class and retirees in Canada are facing. I think some clarification is in order. Poilievre is not one of us. He's a career politician who has never ever worked in the private sector. His only work resume after completing university was working for Stockwell Day and then running for public office. Poilievre has received over twenty years of government pay cheques along with the usual perks of office. His current salary is over $270,000 per year. On top of this, he has taxpayer paid housing, transportation, medical coverage and a pension we can only dream of in private sector employment. If he retired right now, he would receive a fully indexed pension totalling hundreds of thousands of dollars per year.
One of us? I think not. Entitled? Hell yes.
Malcolm Lowe, Unionville
Like his alter ego, former U.S. president Donald Trump, Pierre Poilievre wants his country to return to the Norman Rockwell era. There may be many elements of yesterday's society that his followers admire and wish for but the reality is that the Canada of today is not the Canada of 1958 and never will be again.
Poilievre has no magic wand and this pied piper who sows seeds of anger and conflict has no vision of future Canada and therefore no road map to help us get there. No country can stand still, let alone regress to its past, no matter how green and pleasant it was. Unfortunately we have to deal with the problems of today and so far Poilievre has nothing to offer.
Peter Bradley, Mississauga
It is much easier to fit "Axe The Tax" on a sign than "I Don't Care About Climate Change But I Do Care About Saving A Few Bucks Even Though Most Of What I Pay Comes Back In Refunds."
T.C. Wilkins, Toronto
Can we please stop using daylight savings?
Why it's time to end the pointless and dangerous charade of daylight saving time: Menon, March 7
Two times a year, we are subjected to numerous articles in the popular press advising us that changing the clocks by an hour causes numerous additional traffic accidents, and has certain physiological disadvantages. The original reason for introducing daylight savings was to give farmers an additional hour of productivity each day. In the modern world, this hardly seems necessary. The majority of people that I talk to about this agree that it is an unneeded and outdated idea. I understand that the Ontario government is supportive of the idea of dropping the time change but will not do so until Quebec and/or New York does the same. Why on earth do we have to wait for other areas to agree? Lets take the initiative and show some leadership in getting rid of this time waster once and for all.
Maurice Lovelock, Toronto
Perhaps our astute provincial MPPs, MLAs and their premiers can address why on earth all of Canada has not gone to standard time, ditching daylight saving time and keeping in step with both science and 60 per cent of all the world's countries.
A casual search of international peer-reviewed studies has shown that twice a year when we change our clocks heart attacks increase by 24 per cent. Other health issues, including strokes and depression, as well as workplace and traffic incidents, also increase. The only thing that seems to decrease is office productivity.
W. R. Thuma, Toronto
There's currently an online petition to get rid of daylight saving time and keep standard time all year round. That would be a mistake. If we don't want the clocks to change, let's stop the changing of the clocks, but let's do it right and keep Daylight Saving Time all year round.
If the pro-standard time petition is adopted, it'll be dark by 8 p.m. every night in the summer. We want more sunshine, not less sunshine. Keep the Daylight Saving Time all year around, and stop the useless standard time clock change.
Max Moore, Toronto
When Premier Doug Ford was elected, he promised to end the time change. What happened? Why are we yet again being subjected to this dangerous and unnecessary foolishness? Does he really want more heart attacks and car accidents when our hospitals are overcrowded and understaffed?
Kate Chung, Toronto
Signing Votto
is a home run
for the Blue Jays
Votto says he has agreed to minor league contract with hometown Toronto Blue Jays, March 8
After a 17-year brilliant career with the Cincinnati Reds, Joey Votto has come home. I hope Joey's bat revives in Buffalo and he puts up monster offensive numbers making it impossible the Jays' management to ignore. Imagine Joey getting called up to the Jays as designated hitter and igniting the offence to first place and a third World Series triumph. This is the stuff of Hollywood movies and why not. Miracles happen. Go for it, Joey! Jays fans are pulling for you.
David Maki, Toronto
I think those who complain about his age or recent performance are underestimating Joey Votto's value.
He's a fan favourite, not just to Reds' fans but all over. He's a great teammate by many accounts, is funny, and is humble enough to say he's willing to work in the minors to earn a spot on the Jays' roster despite having a first base career which will probably land him in the Hall of Fame.
I'd love to see him reach 400 total home runs this year and next, even at the expense of his high career on-base percentage. He'll provide a veteran presence if they return to the playoffs and playing back home can only be a good thing.
Adam Silbert, New York
CREDIT: ;

Subject: Daylight saving time; Careers; Heart attacks

Business indexing term: Subject: Careers

Location: New York; United States--US; Canada

Document 465 of 500

Ottawa commits $1M for victims of sexual violence: Move comes after repeated calls from high-profile women

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ottawa-commits-1m-victims-sexual-violence/docview/2955212165/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+commits+%241M+for+victims+of+sexual+violence%3A+Move+comes+after+repeated+calls+from+high-profile+women&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly says Ottawa will commit $1 million to support survivors of sexually based attacks in Israel on Oct. 7.
"We believe Israeli women. And we stand with them in their fight for justice," Joly announced on social media, adding support from the RCMP has also been offered to assist in investigations of the reported crimes.
Joly's decision to provide funding follows repeated pressure from a coalition of high-profile Canadian women who urged the Liberal government to act in response to reports that Hamas assailants sexually assaulted people during their unprecedented attack on Israel on Oct. 7.
Former Ontario premier Kathleen Wynne, former deputy Conservative party leader Lisa Raitt, and others wrote letters and opinion pieces urging Ottawa to take action and push back against those who were downplaying or denying that Hamas used sexual violence.
Wynne, who helped spearhead the push, said Monday she was "very glad this decision has been made."
"I'm sorry it took so long," Wynne said.
"There's nothing partisan about helping people who have suffered - through no fault of their own - in a war," she said.
Others involved include former B.C. Liberal premier Christy Clark, former interim federal Tory leader Rona Ambrose, former Ontario education minister Liz Sandals, and Laureen Harper, a prominent federal Tory and the wife of former prime minister Stephen Harper.
Last week, the UN envoy focusing on sexual violence in conflict said there are "reasonable grounds" to believe Hamas committed rape, "sexualized torture" and other cruel and inhumane treatment of women during its attack.
Pramila Pratten, who visited Israel and the West Bank from Jan. 29 to Feb. 14 with a nine-member technical team, presented her report to the United Nations' Security Council on Monday.
She said their findings included determining some victims were subjected to rape and then killed, and there was also a pattern of women found partially or totally naked, tied up and shot, which indicates sexualized violence, among other things.
Pratten urged Israel to allow a fuller investigation by other UN agencies into what happened on Oct. 7, saying truth and recognition of all violations are critical to helping victims overcome their trauma and regain a sense of agency.
"It is also essential to prevent any denial of the atrocities committed on the seventh of October," she told the UN.
"Truth is the only path toward peace."
Joly made Canada's pledge during her latest tour of the Middle East, largely focused on increased humanitarian support for Gaza, where more than 30,000 Palestinians are believed to have been killed in Israel's response to the Hamas attacks.
Around 250 people were taken hostage and 1,200 were killed when Hamas militants stormed into Israel on Oct. 7.
Ariella Kimmel, a former Conservative staffer who was involved in the effort to press Ottawa for funding, said she was grateful the women of Israel are getting the recognition they deserve.
"For months, Israelis have dealt with skepticism and denialism," she said.
"If you advocate for women, then that advocacy cannot be dependent on whether you agree with the government of the country from which the victims come."
CAPTION: Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly says the funds will support survivors of sexually based attacks in Israel on Oct. 7.
CREDIT: Stephanie Levitz Deputy Toronto Star Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Violence; Sex crimes; Rape; Victims of crime

Location: Israel

People: Joly, Melanie

Document 466 of 500

For shady landlords in Ontario, the punishment must fit the crime

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/shady-landlords-ontario-punishment-must-fit-crime/docview/2955210965/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=For+shady+landlords+in+Ontario%2C+the+punishment+must+fit+the+crime&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The saying goes that the punishment must fit the crime. And yet, when it comes to shady landlords in Ontario, it would seem the punishment inadvertently rewards the crime.
This is the takeaway from a recent analysis by the Star's Victoria Gibson which determined that even when landlords break the rules by improperly evicting tenants, they often face "far milder punishments than are available." Even more damning, landlords caught breaking the rules and fined as a result are often able to quickly recoup their losses by raising the rents on their properties.
Indeed, between November 2022 and November 2023, the Star identified 11 rulings at the Landlord and Tenant Board where a landlord was caught giving an eviction notice in bad faith. In six of those cases where the rent was raised after tenants were inappropriately evicted, the landlord "could have recouped the cost of their punishment by the hearing date."
What makes this trend particularly troubling, beyond the fact that Ontario faces a growing housing crisis, is that the province has already vowed tougher consequences for scofflaw landlords.
In 2020, the provincial government introduced new legislation that increased the maximum fines for housing law offences from $25,000 to $50,000. Yet, according to the Star's reporting, none of the aforementioned landlords caught giving bad faith eviction notices were slapped with an LTB fine of more than $10,000. And even when tenants took their concerns to the supposedly more discerning Rental Housing Enforcement Unit - the steepest fine that unit levied in the 2022-23 cycle was $20,000.
It is unacceptable and unconscionable that amid a severe housing shortage, many landlords who defy the rules for their own gain do not feel anything resembling the full extent of the law. The provincial government is keen to remind the people of Ontario how committed it is to building housing; Premier Doug Ford recently handed out millions to cities that met their provincially defined housing targets.
But he has an equal responsibility to do right by renters in the province, a growing demographic typically underserved and ignored by the political class.
It should go without saying that the glaring lack of affordable rental housing in our society is not a problem that can be solved nor even slightly mitigated by way of steeper fines. However, bad actors must know that legitimate consequences exist for their actions; there is little point introducing punitive measures if authorities are hesitant to use them.
It is our view that if it is at all serious about improving the housing situation for every Ontarian, the provincial government must take a firmer stance with landlords who evict tenants for no good reason. And that stance must include giving landlords a very good reason to abstain from bad behaviour, such as a financial penalty they can't immediately recoup.
Additionally, we urge municipalities to pitch in to curb the problem wherever they can: more specifically, they can follow Hamilton's lead, which will become the first municipality in Ontario to implement a bylaw whose stated goal is to prevent so-called "renovictions."
The bylaw will require that all landlords in the city apply for and obtain a "renovation license" prior to beginning any renovation work that would require the delivery of an N-13 eviction notice. Such a license would include a building permit and an engineer's report, the latter to determine that the landlord is telling the truth -- that the unit will actually be unlivable during construction.
The good news for Torontonians is that last month four Toronto councillors formally asked the city to investigate what it would take to implement a similar policy in Toronto. We hope the city can move quickly to follow suit.
In the end a Hamilton style bylaw is not a silver bullet. Nor are hefty fines for exploitative landlords. But taken together both measures are an added layer of protection against the flagrant mistreatment of Ontario tenants and a small yet consequential step toward a fair housing future for all.
CAPTION: A Star analysis shows landlords who evict in bad faith are routinely given fines below the provincial maximums.

Subject: Bad faith; Rentals; Evictions; Tenants

Business indexing term: Subject: Bad faith

Title: For shady landlords in Ontario, the punishment must fit the crime

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Mar 12, 2024

Section: opinion/editorials

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document type: News

ProQuest document ID: 2955210965

Document URL: https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/shady-landlords-ontario-punishment-must-fit-crime/docview/2955210965/se-2?accountid=15090

Copyright: Copyright 2024 Toronto Star Newspapers Limited. All Rights Reserved.

Last updated: 2024-03-12

Database: Canadian Newsstream Collection

Document 467 of 500

Cracking down on shady landlords

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/cracking-down-on-shady-landlords/docview/2955210546/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Cracking+down+on+shady+landlords&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

It should go without saying that the glaring lack of affordable rental housing in our society is not a problem that can be solved nor even slightly mitigated by way of steeper fines. However, bad actors must know that legitimate consequences exist for their actions; there is little point introducing punitive measures if authorities are hesitant to use them.
It is our view that if it is at all serious about improving the housing situation for every Ontarian, the provincial government must take a firmer stance with landlords who evict tenants for no good reason. And that stance must include giving landlords a very good reason to abstain from bad behaviour, such as a financial penalty they can't immediately recoup.
Additionally, we urge municipalities to pitch in to curb the problem wherever they can: more specifically, they can follow Hamilton's lead, which will become the first municipality in Ontario to implement a bylaw whose stated goal is to prevent so-called "renovictions."
The bylaw will require that all landlords in the city apply for and obtain a "renovation license" prior to beginning any renovation work that would require the delivery of an N-13 eviction notice. Such a license would include a building permit and an engineer's report, the latter to determine that the landlord is telling the truth - that the unit will actually be unlivable during construction.
The good news for Torontonians is that last month four Toronto councillors formally asked the city to investigate what it would take to implement a similar policy in Toronto. We hope the city can move quickly to follow suit.
In the end a Hamilton style bylaw is not a silver bullet. Nor are hefty fines for exploitative landlords. But taken together both measures are an added layer of protection against the flagrant mistreatment of Ontario tenants and a small yet consequential step toward a fair housing future for all.
CREDIT: ;

Subject: Bad faith; Evictions; Rentals; Tenants

Business indexing term: Subject: Bad faith

Title: Cracking down on shady landlords
