Document 468 of 500

When Ford earned a judge's rebuke: Comments during jury selection in 2014 raised concern over presumed innocence

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/when-ford-earned-judges-rebuke/docview/2955209971/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=When+Ford+earned+a+judge%27s+rebuke%3A+Comments+during+jury+selection+in+2014+raised+concern+over+presumed+innocence&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ten years ago, then Toronto city councillor Doug Ford arrived at the Superior Court for jury duty.
He didn't want to be there. He said as much in a Toronto Sun interview, saying he wanted to be excused - after all, he wasn't anonymous like his fellow prospective jurors.
"I believe I should be a jury member, but I don't think the person I convict should know my address, where I work, how to get hold of my kids," he told the newspaper in an article on Feb. 4, 2014.
"The person I convict" - the next day in court, those four words were enough to earn Ford and the whole jury panel a lecture from the judge on a basic but crucial legal principle: that every person accused of a crime is presumed innocent until proven guilty.
The episode is revealed in a court recording recently uncovered by the Star at a time when the now premier of Ontario has sparked a constitutional crisis over his desire to appoint "like-minded" judges.
Just as in 2014, the presumption of innocence is at the heart of the issue, with Ford "quintupling" down on wanting judges who will lock up even more people - including by denying bail to individuals who, as the judge reminded him a decade ago, are legally innocent.
Back in 2014, Ford's statements to the Sun had raised concerns for the defence in a firearms possession case that was picking its jury. The defence was worried that his public comments suggested he would find someone guilty without hearing any evidence, and that this might taint the other potential jurors and could throw the case off the rails.
"I do wish to remind all of you, at this particular juncture, of a very fundamental - a fundamental - rule of the law of this land," Superior Court Justice Michael Quigley told Ford and the others. "That fundamental rule, ladies and gentlemen, is the presumption of innocence.
"That's the foundation of the law of this land, and it protects each and every one of us."
A decade later, legal organizations have decried Ford's call for tough "like-minded" judges as a "substantial threat" to the independence of the courts; former chief justices have called out his comments as a "political litmus test" for judges. His stance was first revealed last month in response to a Star exclusive that his government had placed two of his ex-staffers on the arm's-length committee that recommends judges for the Ontario Court of Justice. "I have a message to all these criminals: We're coming for you," Ford said at an unrelated announcement Friday.
"We're going to catch you and you're going to jail, no matter what it takes ... including making sure we have judges that do not let these people out on bail."
There's also a wider context to Ford's comments. In recent years, his government has taken more control over what was a non-partisan appointment process, once described as one of the best in the world. The Tories have also placed a heavy focus on policing and prosecution while underresourcing legal aid and failing to properly staff courthouses, resulting in delays that have led to the collapse of serious criminal cases.
Most of the people in Ontario's jails are legally innocent. About 80 per cent of inmates are on remand - meaning they've been denied bail and are awaiting trial or sentencing - a number that has only increased over the years. As The Canadian Press recently reported, the majority of jails were over capacity last year; Ford said Friday his government would build as many jails as they need.
For the premier's growing chorus of critics, his words betray a fundamental misunderstanding of the rule of law - the same kind of thinking that led to Quigley's remedial-law rebuke a decade ago. Ford's actions, meanwhile, risk damaging the public's confidence in a vital democratic institution, while likely failing to do much of anything to actually keep Ontarians safe.
Ontario is "detaining more people in custody than we ever have before, and crime is still happening," noted Ottawa criminal defence lawyer Michael Spratt, a frequent critic of the government on the judicial appointments process.
"The unfortunate thing that is apparent about Ford is that slogans and simplicity rule the mind," but "simple solutions" won't do what Ford says they will, Spratt said.
The premier's office did not return the Star's request for comment about his jury duty experience and the judge's lecture.
Back in February 2014, the lawyers and the judge had a pressing question: What to do about Doug Ford?
His statements to the Sun were one issue, but the defence was also concerned by comments Ford had made in court that again suggested he already thought the accused were guilty. Ford had told the judge during jury selection: "Your Honour, I don't know the accused, but they know who I am, and I don't feel comfortable with them knowing who I am."
Quigley told Ford he was aware of his concerns reported in the Sun, but wasn't prepared to excuse him from jury duty just yet.
Then, over the lunch break, the defence saw a tweet from a Sun reporter saying Ford had taken members of the jury pool to visit the nearby office of his brother, then mayor Rob Ford; they were seen leaving with "Ford Nation" T-shirts.
After lunch, with just the lawyers and the judge in the courtroom, the defence expressed concern about Ford's sentiments and what might have been communicated to others in the jury pool. The accused persons hadn't been convicted of anything, but Ford had spoken as though they were already guilty. What else might he have said to the other prospective jurors?
A number of options were floated: Do they bring Ford into the courtroom alone to ask him questions? Do they discharge the whole jury panel? Declare a mistrial?
"Insofar as Coun. Ford seems to have created a little bit of difficulty with comments that he has made so far, we may not be able to eliminate his ability to make those comments; it's a public nation, it's a free country, he can say what he wants," Quigley said. "Unfortunately, the press actually wants to quote what these people say."
Ford could, however, be removed from the jury panel. Quigley told the lawyers if Ford's name did get picked to potentially sit on the jury, he was unlikely to permit it, "for the very simple reason that the jurors won't focus on what their job is; they'll focus on him. And I don't mind recording that that's exactly what I just said."
But Ford would have to stay a while longer, the judge said, "because he doesn't get the entitlement, unlike other people, to just be able to walk out. He has a civic duty to serve just like everybody else does, and he can wait here until that jury is picked, and that's exactly what's going on here."
And so Quigley decided he would "admonish" the whole jury panel on the presumption of innocence - without mentioning Ford and his comments - before resuming jury selection.
Provincial and federal governments appointing judges who broadly share their world view is hardly new or surprising, but one of the concerns with Ford's stance is that he's made clear he wants a specific ruling from judges who are meant to be, and invariably take pride in being, independent.
"The way in which the premier is expressing himself - and sort of bombastically, as one might expect - it gives the impression that yes, he wants this outcome: regardless of the law, regardless of the facts, he always wants them to come down hard on crime," said Troy Riddell, chair of the political science department at the University of Guelph, who researches judicial politics.
"I don't mind the needle being shifted a little bit, but that has to be done with care and sensitivity and nuance, and that's not what you're seeing in this case."
It's also not clear that denying bail ultimately lowers crime and keeps people safe; research more often points to the need for mental health and substance-use supports, affordable housing and a livable income - largely provincial responsibilities.
If the government wants to increase public safety and ensure the justice system is sustainable and jails aren't overcrowded, "the evidence is very clear that many of the policies that the premier seems to favour are just not good policies," Osgoode Hall law professor Palma Paciocco said. "They may seem intuitive or appealing to many, but they're just not evidence-based."
In the end, a mistrial was declared in the firearms case for reasons unrelated to Ford, and he never did end up on a jury. But he nevertheless walked away with an important lesson that day about the criminal justice system.
"Accused persons start out trials with a clean slate," Quigley told the prospective jurors, future premier included. "It is only when the Crown has satisfied those 12 people (on the jury) that the accused person is guilty of one or more offences that the presumption of innocence leaves."
Everyone engages in "lunch hour talk, breakfast table talk, street talk, about people and about guilt and innocence," Quigley said. But, he emphasized, jurors are asked to try a case based solely on the evidence, and not on any outside influence, bias or partiality.
"That's what it's all about; that's the foundation of the law of this land and it protects each and every one of us, OK? So I admonish you and remind you of that presumption of innocence," the judge said.
"And we'll just leave the matter there."
CREDIT: Jacques Gallant Courts and Justice Reporter

Subject: Bail; Juries; Court hearings & proceedings; Law; Defense; Attorneys; Firearms; Judges & magistrates

Document 469 of 500

When Doug Ford tried to get out of jury duty - and earned himself a lecture on the rule of law

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/when-doug-ford-tried-get-out-jury-duty-earned/docview/2955208216/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=When+Doug+Ford+tried+to+get+out+of+jury+duty+-+and+earned+himself+a+lecture+on+the+rule+of+law&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ten years ago, then-Toronto city councillor Doug Ford arrived at the Superior Court for jury duty.He didn't want to be there.He said as much in an interview with the Toronto Sun, saying he wanted to be excused - after all, he wasn't anonymous like his fellow prospective jurors."I believe I should be a jury member, but I don't think the person I convict should know my address, where I work, how to get hold of my kids," he told the newspaper in a Feb. 4, 2014 article. His stance was first revealed last month in response to a Star exclusive that his government had placed two of his ex-staffers on the arm's-length committee that recommends judges for the Ontario Court of Justice."I have a message to all these criminals: We're coming for you," Ford said at an unrelated announcement Friday."We're going to catch you and you're going to jail, no matter what it takes ... including making sure we have judges that do not let these people out on bail." The Tories have also placed a heavy focus on policing and prosecution while underresourcing legal aid and failing to properly staff courthouses, resulting in delays that have led to the collapse of serious criminal cases.Most of the people in Ontario's jails are legally innocent. About 80 per cent of inmates are on remand - meaning they've been denied bail and are awaiting trial or sentencing - a number that has only increased over the years. As The Canadian Press recently reported, the majority of jails were over capacity last year; Ford said Friday his government would build as many jails as they need. For the premier's growing chorus of critics, his words betray a fundamental misunderstanding of the rule of law - the same kind of thinking that led to Quigley's remedial-law rebuke a decade ago. Ford's actions, meanwhile, risk damaging the public's confidence in a vital democratic institution, while likely failing to do much of anything to actually keep Ontarians safe.Ontario is "detaining more people in custody than we ever have before, and crime is still happening," noted Ottawa criminal defence lawyer Michael Spratt, a frequent critic of the government on the judicial appointments process."The unfortunate thing that is apparent about Ford is that slogans and simplicity rule the mind," but "simple solutions" won't do what Ford says they will, Spratt said.The premier's office did not return the Star's request for comment about his jury duty experience and the judge's lecture. 'He doesn't get ... to just be able to walk out'Back in February 2014, the lawyers and the judge had a pressing question: What to do about Doug Ford?His statements to the Sun were one issue, but the defence was also concerned by comments Ford had made in court that again suggested he already thought the accused were guilty. Ford had told the judge during jury selection: "Your Honour, I don't know the accused, but they know who I am, and I don't feel comfortable with them knowing who I am."Quigley told Ford he was aware of his concerns reported in the Sun, but wasn't prepared to excuse him from jury duty just yet. Then, over the lunch break, the defence saw a tweet from a Sun reporter saying Ford had taken members of the jury pool to visit the nearby office of his brother, Mayor Rob Ford; they were seen leaving with "Ford Nation" t-shirts.After lunch, with just the lawyers and the judge in the courtroom, the defence expressed concern about Ford's sentiments and what might have been communicated to others in the jury pool. The accused persons hadn't been convicted of anything, but Ford had spoken like they were already guilty. What else might he have said to the other prospective jurors?A number of options were floated: Do they bring Ford into the courtroom alone to ask him questions? Do they discharge the whole jury panel? Declare a mistrial?"Insofar as Coun. Ford seems to have created a little bit of difficulty with comments that he has made so far, we may not be able to eliminate his ability to make those comments; it's a public nation, it's a free country, he can say what he wants," Quigley said. "Unfortunately, the press actually wants to quote what these people say."Ford could, however, be removed from the jury panel. Quigley told the lawyers that if Ford's name did get picked to potentially sit on the jury, he was unlikely to permit it, "for the very simple reason that the jurors won't focus on what their job is, they'll focus on him. And I don't mind recording that that's exactly what I just said."But Ford would have to stay awhile longer, the judge said, "because he doesn't get the entitlement, unlike other people, to just be able to walk out. He has a civic duty to serve just like everybody else does, and he can wait here until that jury is picked, and that's exactly what's going on here."And so Quigley decided he would "admonish" the whole jury panel on the presumption of innocence - without mentioning Ford and his comments - before resuming jury selection.'An instrument of partisanship and ideology'Provincial and federal governments appointing judges who broadly share their world view is hardly new or surprising, but one of the concerns with Ford's stance is that he's made clear he wants a specific ruling from judges who are meant to be, and invariably take pride in being, independent. "The way in which the premier is expressing himself - and sort of bombastically, as one might expect - it gives the impression that yes, he wants this outcome: regardless of the law, regardless of the facts, he always wants them to come down hard on crime," said Troy Riddell, chair of the political science department at the University of Guelph, who researches judicial politics. "I don't mind the needle being shifted a little bit, but that has to be done with care and sensitivity and nuance, and that's not what you're seeing in this case."It's also not clear that denying bail ultimately lowers crime and keeps people safe; research more often points to the need for mental health and substance-use supports, affordable housing and a livable income - largely provincial responsibilities. If the government wants to increase public safety, and ensure the justice system is sustainable and jails aren't overcrowded, "the evidence is very clear that many of the policies that the premier seems to favour are just not good policies," Osgoode Hall law professor Palma Paciocco said. "They may seem intuitive or appealing to many, but they're just not evidence-based." Ford's "like-minded" comments come on the heels of changes his government has made to the 13-person Judicial Appointments Advisory Committee (JAAC), which is made up of judges, lawyers, and members of the public and was created in the 1980s to ensure an independent, non-partisan appointment process. It was "widely considered to be one of the best judicial appointments processes in North America" at the time of its creation, three former chief justices of the Ontario Court of Justice said in a statement this month. And until recently, they said, the basic structure and function of the committee "remained largely unchanged and has been honoured by successive Liberal, NDP and Conservative governments."The committee was also seen as superior to the system that vets candidates for federally-appointed judicial positions, which has faced its own accusations of politicization. In 2021, Ford's attorney general, Doug Downey, implemented major changes that legal groups warned could lead to patronage appointments to the bench.Those included taking away the power of the three legal organizations on the committee - the Law Society of Ontario, the Federation of Ontario Law Associations and the Ontario Bar Association - to name their own representatives; instead, they must now submit lists of candidates for his selection, allowing Downey to appoint almost all of the committee's members. As the Star previously reported, Downey has still not selected a Law Society representative, despite the regulator sending him a list of candidates.Downey also increased the number of judicial candidates on the shortlist submitted to him by the committee; previously, a ranked shortlist of at least two candidates was submitted. It's now six, with critics warning that giving the government access to the wider application pool could lead to the appointment of lower-quality candidates.And now, Ford's former deputy chief of staff Matthew Bondy chairs the JAAC, while Ford's ex-director of stakeholder relations Brock Vandrick has been a public member of the committee since December.A process that was once a model for the world "has degenerated into an instrument of partisanship and ideology," said University of Toronto law professor Kent Roach."The public will and should have less confidence in the administration of justice in this province because of this change."'The foundation of the law of this land'In the end, a mistrial was declared in the firearms case for reasons unrelated to Ford, and he never did end up on a jury. But he nevertheless walked away with an important lesson that day about the criminal justice system."Accused persons start out trials with a clean slate," Quigley told the prospective jurors, future-premier included. "It is only when the Crown has satisfied those 12 people (on the jury) that the accused person is guilty of one or more offences that the presumption of innocence leaves."Everyone engages in "lunch hour talk, breakfast table talk, street talk, about people and about guilt and innocence," Quigley said. But, he emphasized, jurors are asked to try a case based solely on the evidence, and not on any outside influence, bias, or partiality."That's what it's all about; that's the foundation of the law of this land and it protects each and every one of us, OK? So I admonish you and remind you of that presumption of innocence," the judge said."And we'll just leave the matter there."

Title: When Doug Ford tried to get out of jury duty - and earned himself a lecture on the rule of law

Document 470 of 500

Naheed Nenshi is sure to shake up the Alberta NDP leadership race

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/naheed-nenshi-is-sure-shake-up-alberta-ndp/docview/2955703972/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Naheed+Nenshi+is+sure+to+shake+up+the+Alberta+NDP+leadership+race&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Alberta NDP leadership race got a much-needed shot of adrenalin today when former Calgary mayor, Naheed Nenshi, confirmed all the rumours and announced that he is in to win.
A three-term mayor and a formidable opponent to anyone who takes him on, Nenshi hasn't exactly been known as a leftist, in fact he used to pride himself on being non-partisan. That's why purple was always his campaign colour: a mix of red and blue. But not a hint of orange, the Alberta NDP's preferred hue.
This has left traditional NDP supporters and leadership candidates in a bit of a huff because they see him as an outsider who has yet to earn his wings in the trenches of party politics.
But sometimes outsiders are just what a political party needs to take flight.
And there is no better example than Ralph Klein one of the most popular premiers in Alberta history.
Klein was also a three-term mayor of Calgary before he won a Calgary constituency for the ruling Alberta Progressive Conservatives in 1989 and immediately landed in cabinet. Three years later he won the PC leadership and the key to the premier's office by beating prominent PC insiders.
But before Klein joined the PCs, who at the time had lost their Lougheed lustre, he was not known as a conservative. He was a big spender as mayor. By his own admission he had changed his party colours many times: he had held Social Credit, Liberal and PC party cards. He had been touted as the man to harvest southern Alberta for the Liberals.
And yet Klein introduced a ruthless neo-conservative agenda that slashed health care and post-secondary education by 20 per cent. Thousands of public sector works were laid off. Welfare rolls were cut in half over one year.The Calgary General Hospital was completely demolished in order to cut spending on health-care costs.
Despite the hardships suffered by many Albertans, Klein remained popular. And he became even more popular when oilsands development lured billions of dollars of investment to the province.
He certainly proved that outsiders aren't necessarily destined to fail at party politics.
Whether he wins or loses, there's no question that Nenshi will shake up the NDP leadership campaign. So far it's been rather a placid affair. Four of the candidates are prominent NDP MLAs: Kathleen Ganley, Sarah Hoffman, Rakhi Pancholi, and Jodi Calahoo Stonehouse. Gil McGowan, the outspoken, combative president of the Alberta Federation of Labour is also running.
At this point, it's doubtful any of these candidates could return the NDP to power. But Rachel Notley is a hard act to follow. She's dynamic, smart and a strong campaigner who seems to feel at home anywhere in the province. She won the 2015 election and came awfully close to winning again last year.
Nenshi could be a valuable asset for the party, particularly in Calgary and smaller cities where the NDP need to make gains if they are to form government again. He also has the kind of bravado needed to take on Premier Danielle Smith and her radical agenda.
He was on fire when he spoke at a recent rally protesting Smith's proposed legislation that would compel the outing of transgender students and forbid certain medical treatments that they can currently access.
"I hate that what we heard for seven minutes was cruelty ... lies," he told hundreds of people outside Calgary City Hall. He called Smith's stance "inhumane," "un-Albertan," and dangerous.
Just before last year's election Nenshi announced that he would be "lending" his vote to the NDP because Smith is an "existential threat" to the future of the province.
Given that the Alberta NDP is more centrist than leftist this wasn't a huge leap for someone who brags about preferring purple - a mix of conservative and liberal values.
Now he just has to convince other Albertans that an NDP membership and a vote for him could eventually turn the whole province purple.
CAPTION: Former Calgary mayor Naheed Nenshi endorses Rachel Notley, leader of Alberta's New Democrats in a May 2023 photo. Notley lost the provincial election. On March 11, Nenshi announced he will try to succeed her as NDP leader.Mayor Naheed Nenshi at city hall in Calgary, Alta, on June 24, 2014. The three-term Calgary mayor announced March 11 he will run for the Alberta NDP leadership.
CREDIT: Gillian Steward Contributor

Subject: Provincial elections; Leadership

Document 471 of 500

Ottawa commits funding to help survivors of sexually-based attacks in Israel

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ottawa-commits-funding-help-survivors-sexually/docview/2955703796/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+commits+funding+to+help+survivors+of+sexually-based+attacks+in+Israel&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly says Ottawa will commit $1 million to support survivors of sexually-based attacks in Israel on Oct. 7.
"We believe Israeli women. And we stand with them in their fight for justice," Joly announced on social media, adding support from the RCMP has also been offered to assist in investigations of the reported crimes.
Joly's decision to provide funding follows repeated pressure from a coalition of high-profile Canadian women who urged the Liberal government to act in response to reports that Hamas assailants sexually assaulted people during their unprecedented attack on Israel on Oct. 7.
Former Ontario premier Kathleen Wynne, former deputy Conservative party leader Lisa Raitt, and others wrote letters and opinion pieces urging Ottawa to take action and push back against those who were downplaying or denying that Hamas used sexual violence.
Wynne, who helped spearhead the push, said Monday she was "very glad this decision has been made."
"I'm sorry it took so long," Wynne said.
"There's nothing partisan about helping people who have suffered - through no fault of their own - in a war," she said.
Others involved include former B.C. Liberal premier Christy Clark, former interim federal Tory leader Rona Ambrose, former Ontario education minister Liz Sandals, and Laureen Harper, a prominent federal Tory and the wife of former prime minister Stephen Harper.
Last week, the UN envoy focusing on sexual violence in conflict said there are "reasonable grounds" to believe Hamas committed rape, "sexualized torture" and other cruel and inhumane treatment of women during its attack.
Pramila Pratten, who visited Israel and the West Bank from Jan. 29 to Feb. 14 with a nine-member technical team, presented her report to the United Nations' Security Council on Monday.
She said their findings included determining some victims were subjected to rape and then killed, and there was also a pattern of women found partially or totally naked, tied up and shot, which indicates sexualized violence, among other things.
Pratten urged Israel to allow a fuller investigation by other UN agencies into what happened on Oct. 7, saying truth and recognition of all violations are critical to helping victims overcome their trauma and regain a sense of agency.
"It is also essential to prevent any denial of the atrocities committed on the seventh of October," she told the UN.
"Truth is the only path toward peace."
Joly made Canada's pledge during her latest tour of the Middle East, largely focused on increased humanitarian support for Gaza, where more than 30,000 Palestinians are believed to have been killed in Israel's response to the Hamas attacks.
Around 250 people were taken hostage and 1,200 were killed when Hamas militants stormed into Israel on Oct. 7.
Ariella Kimmel, a former Conservative staffer who was involved in the effort to press Ottawa for funding, said she was grateful the women of Israel are getting the recognition they deserve.
"For months, Israelis have dealt with skepticism and denialism," she said.
"If you advocate for women, then that advocacy cannot be dependent on whether you agree with the government of the country from which the victims come."
Next week, a delegation of families of Hamas' victims, including those with Canadian roots, will travel to Parliament Hill in a bid to raise further awareness about violence against women.
CAPTION: Minister of Foreign Affairs Melanie Joly listens to questions from reporters in the Foyer of the House of Commons on Parliament Hill in Ottawa on on Feb. 29, 2024.

Document 472 of 500

Sprawl for nothing? As it turns 50, Mississauga finds itself at crossroads

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sprawl-nothing-as-turns-50-mississauga-finds/docview/2954715755/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sprawl+for+nothing%3F+As+it+turns+50%2C+Mississauga+finds+itself+at+crossroads&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Michelle Dias looks out the window of her Mississauga office tower, she sees a sky full of cranes. She can't remember the last time she has seen so many.
Every morning as she drives in and out of the "downtown" of the city, she faces heavy traffic due to lane closures around the LRT construction on Hurontario Street.
Dias, who has grown up in Mississauga, says the change across the city has become more noticeable: more people, more traffic and more development.
All across the city, she can see the tension between what the city wants to be and what it has been.
"We're going through growing pains," she said, optimistically. "The feeling ... is that we are trying some new ideas that might be uncomfortable now but will be better for all of us in the long run."
For decades, Mississauga has largely been defined by two things: its proximity to Toronto making it a bedroom community for the big city, and the 36-year tenure of Hazel McCallion, known as the Queen of Sprawl for her push for low-density development.
But there is no more land in the city left to sprawl on.
Now, as the province's third-largest city turns 50, and is about to elect only its third mayor since 1978, it faces a choice between two futures: forge ahead to truly become a city - with transit, mixed housing and a vibrant arts scene - or cling to a past that has made it the province's second-largest economy but created a sprawling community totally reliant on the car.
Mayoral election will set the path
Residents believe the mayoral election - which kicked off last week with the vote taking place in June - will determine which path the city takes.
"A lot of people choose to live in Mississauga because they like the suburban lifestyle and it makes sense for them and their families," said lifelong resident Brandon Wiedemann, who works as a paramedic in the region. "But I think about all the things that make cities flourish: the arts, entertainment, transit and walkable communities - and we are really missing that here."
But he said unlike previous elections, where former mayors McCallion or Bonnie Crombie won handily, voters now realize they have a say in choosing a mayor who aligns with how they want their city to be.
"There's a conversation people in Mississauga are starting to have: how do we make housing more affordable, how do we get more neighbours - and then the other flip side, how do I ensure I get maximum value for my property," he said. "There's this constant push and pull between these two sides.
"We are now at this stage where we are trying to figure out who we are."
Shauna Brail, director of the Institute of Management and Innovation at University of Toronto, said that by most definitions Mississauga has already achieved city status.
Brail said suburbs become cities by focusing on urban elements such as building denser housing, building a variety of affordable housing, improving transit and transit-oriented development and improving economic development to avoid long commutes for work.
"It's not that simple to create a vibrant space from scratch," said Brail, adding that urban planners have long discussed how to build the perfect city. "And they mostly fail."
Mississauga's quest to be recognized as an independent stand-alone city has been several years in the making. Anchored by the airport and more than 75 Fortune 500 companies, it is a net importer of jobs into the municipality. Last year, Mississauga was within striking distance of independence from Peel Region - but for now, remains financially tethered to both Brampton and Caledon.
Mississauga tries to tackle the housing crisis
Mississauga has been looking for solutions to the housing crisis for the past decade. In 2017, council passed a housing strategy to increase choice by focusing on the "missing middle." At the time, the city identified the average price of a home to be $1.3 million while rent for a two bedroom was between $1,600 to $2,800.
"These prices are out of reach for 80 per cent of households," the staff report said. Suggestions to improve housing availability included making land development-ready and supporting affordable housing proposals. In 2023, it launched another plan to boost affordable housing and purpose-built rental units in the city.
But in council, the suburban mindset is ever-present. This term, city council has grappled with accusations of NIMBY-ism or bowing to the pressure of housing gatekeepers by both upper levels of government and housing advocates on key housing policies.
Kelly Singh is the acting executive director of More Homes Mississauga (MHM), an organization that represents young people who want to live in Mississauga but have been pushed out by cost or scarcity. She said there is a growing frustration with how the city has handled the housing file.
"There's a lot of people in Mississauga feeling that pinch and that frustration - not only with seeing city council not taking action or if they do take action through the public consultation process is overly dominated by the NIMBY voices," said Singh.
"The vast majority of Mississauga's neighbourhoods with suburban sprawl are locked in time because no other zoning is allowed," she added. "That has to change, but can't until council pass zoning orders (like inclusionary zoning) to allow for it."
The costs of inaction on housing
Not taking action has consequence. In 2021, Mississauga saw a population decline, and was the only outer suburb around Toronto to do so. As boomers have aged, but stayed in their homes, several parts of the city have seen population decline, leading to negative growth - rare for a suburb.
Just last month, the province told the city it was deemed ineligible for provincial funds due to insufficient housing starts. Mississauga said it had 3,470 housing starts in 2023, and that it has tens of thousands of homes in the approval pipeline but it "can't put shovels in the ground."
Singh said one example of the city's failure to accept progressive housing policy came to light in October, when council failed on a tie vote to pass a policy allowing fourplexes - a residential building with four distinct housing units - "as of right, in principle."
Eventually, faced with the risk of losing $120 million from the federal government through Housing Accelerator Fund, then-mayor Crombie used her "strong mayor" powers to push the motion through.
"It's not that fourplexes alone would solve the housing crisis in Mississauga. They would simply be a step in the right direction," said Singh. "The next person in the mayor's chair will have to find a way to work productively with a sometimes deadlocked council to achieve meaningful progress on housing reform."
And take a firmer stand against strong NIMBY voices in the community, she added. Since last year, residents have contested several projects such as bike lanes, and more recently, a 50-bed supportive housing development in the city's east end atop an empty lot.
"Nobody is at fault for owning a suburban sprawl house ... and putting their roots down in the suburbs ... but nobody else is at fault for wanting those same things," said Singh. "We have to see ourselves as part of a broader community and not just as individual homeowners."
Getting beyond 'windshield' communities
For long-time resident David Poopalapillai, community building starts by getting people out of their cars. Poopalapillai, who has lived in the city for 30 years, said Mississauga's future city building should not just be centred around bricks and mortars.
"We built houses and buildings, but we didn't build livable communities," he said. "We have built windshield communities: we only see our neighbours through the windshield."
He believes better transit options in the city, a more developed and vibrant lakeshore, and a bustling and mixed-use downtown beyond the current "concrete jungle" are all steps in making the city more livable.
In January, more than 800 people registered to listen to urban planner Brent Toderian's conversation with Mississauga city manager Shari Lichterman on the municipality's next 50 years. The venue moved twice to accommodate the demand.
Toderian, who was the chief planner for Vancouver and Calgary, and now runs his own urban planning practice, said the immense interest in this topic shows the city is at a "turning point" and the residents want to be part of it.
Toderian, who is helping Mississauga develop a strategic plan, said the key question Mississauga is trying to answer is if it "is still a suburb or is it a city?"
He believes it will remain both for a long time.
But he said the city needs to do a better job by making the "urban places more urban" instead of simply putting up high-density housing built with cars in minds. And it will also need its suburban places to also become less focused on the car.
In one example, he cited sidewalks as a key part of any city, but said the space is often designed with the driver in mind. "Everything is scaled to the car, not to the human being," he said.
Lichterman said the increased interest in city planning is likely driven by the housing crisis, and the realization that "the decisions being made by city government will affect their lives for decades to come."
A sense of belonging
Michelle Dias can rhyme off all the reasons why she loves living in Mississauga in a heartbeat: great schools, safe community, thriving community centres, and a burgeoning entertainment and food scene.
But mostly, the sense of belonging. She said that her parents who immigrated from Kenya in 1988 to Mississauga never even felt the need to go back home after settling in the city. "This became their home."
"Mississauga is incredibly inclusive," said Dias. "People feel like they can celebrate their unique community with open arms."
As an example, she points to the fact that the city's Celebration Square holds ethnic festivals and events every weekend in the summer to appeal to diverse communities.
According to the 2021 census, Mississauga took in around 15 per cent of all recent immigrants who came to the Toronto region. More than half of its residents were born outside of Canada.
Dias said that many students she teaches at a local community college dream of settling in Mississauga. She worries, given the current housing crisis in the city, whether that will ever be a possibility for them.
"What will be their living situation look like?" said Dias. "Is Mississauga even an option for them?"
Lichterman concedes the housing crisis has made it harder for immigrants and young Canadians to seek or achieve the dream of home ownership in Mississauga.
"It's attainable ... but it is becoming out of reach for many, and that's why things need to change," she said. "And it's on the city and city council to make sure it does."
CAPTION: Michelle Dias has grown up in Mississauga and says the change across the city has become more noticeable: more people, more traffic and more development: "We're going through growing pains." For some, creating a Mississauga that's more of a city means getting more people out of their cars and on to transit.Andrew Francis Wallacetoronto starGraeme FrisqueMetroland file photo

CREDIT: Noor Javed Toronto Star

Document 473 of 500

Feds had option to allow 1M new residents: Proposal presented to cabinet for 2023 immigration target

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/feds-had-option-allow-1m-new-residents/docview/2954715672/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Feds+had+option+to+allow+1M+new+residents%3A+Proposal+presented+to+cabinet+for+2023+immigration+target&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Before Justin Trudeau's government landed on a plan to bring in about 400,000 permanent residents per year, cabinet considered a proposal that would have opened the door significantly wider in a single year, the Star has learned.
Amid growing scrutiny of Ottawa's immigration rates, internal government documents show the Liberal government rejected an option to raise the immigration rate to one million per year.
A plan drafted by a deputy minister lays out three options for how many permanent residents the country could accept in 2023 - as well as the pros and cons of each option.
The most ambitious of the three would have seen Canada accept a million permanent residents for that year.
The memorandum, which was put together for cabinet's consideration back in 2020, offers additional context for the path the government eventually chose on Canada's modern immigration targets, which have faced criticism in recent months amid inflation, housing and health-care strains.
It was submitted before then immigration minister Marco Mendicino unveiled the Liberals' plan to support economic recovery from the pandemic through immigration. Ultimately, the cabinet settled on ushering in an annual rate of about one per cent of Canada's population - 401,000 permanent residents in 2021, 411,000 in 2022 and 421,000 in 2023.
"This proposal puts forward options that reflect the Government's ambition and priorities for immigration," it said. "All options signal future growth and reflect the continuing importance of immigration, with broad ranges for flexibility amidst uncertainty."
The document was part of a 112-page filing to the Federal Court for a legal challenge against Ottawa recently over family reunification.
Due to lockdowns and travel restrictions, the initial 2020 immigration plan had been completely upended, falling far short of the initial 341,000 target.
With the border still closed and observers expressing concerns over the shortfall on Canada's economy, the proposal with the three options tabled to the cabinet called for a growth plan.
"The proposed immigration levels plan aims to protect and enhance Canada's immigration advantage, leverage it to welcome a growing number of newcomers who will contribute to short-term recovery and long-term growth, and seize the opportunity to help shape and support a future vision for our country," said the plan.
A "forward-looking" plan, it said, would support family reunification, boost francophone communities outside Quebec and keep Canada competitive on the world stage while, in the short run, addressing regional labour market needs and allowing more international students and foreign workers to become permanent residents.
Consultations with provinces, said the report, indicated British Columbia and Alberta advocated a pause in immigration growth while Ontario and others emphasized increases that were "modest, incremental, stable and managed."
Department officials provided three options:
Growth through carrying over the 2020 shortfall over three years: raised the 2021 level from 341,000 to 401,000, to 411,000 in 2022 and 421,000 in 2023.
Staying the course of the original plan - 351,000 in 2021 and 361,000 in 2022, while consulting with Canadians on a steeper trajectory of future growth.
Exponential levels of growth that would see the 2021 target intact and the 2022 target up to 500,000 and one million in 2023.
In laying out the three options, officials did not indicate a preferred choice. Regarding the final option, they did say maintaining the 2021 original target would allow the department a year to prepare for the planned surge in 2022 and 2023.
"This option not only supports economic recovery, but also goes beyond through a vision of ambitious growth," said the memorandum.
It cited public confidence in Canada's "long history of managed migration" as an advantage to roll out such a plan when other countries such as the United States, the United Kingdom, Australia, New Zealand and Switzerland had signalled a desire to reduce immigration levels.
The government would settle on slightly raising the immigration intake for 2021-23 from the previous plan.
The fact that an intake of one million for 2023, almost three times above the original target of the year before, was put forward for consideration floored critics.
"Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada was clearly foolish to think that one million was a number that would maintain public support for immigration," said University of Ottawa law and public health professor Amir Attaran, who obtained the cabinet memo.
"Obviously, we've never been close to a million and IRCC wanted cabinet to consider doing something clearly out of precedent. It wasn't gradualism. It was extremism."
Vance Langford, co-president of the Canadian Immigration Lawyers Association, isn't sure how serious the government was with the one million option, but said it's probably the first time in Canadian history that it would have ever been presented as an option.
"The IRCC provides technocratic support to cabinet on the levels plan so they assess scenarios based on Canada's policy goals, budget, operational capacity, economy, labour market needs, global factors, etc.," he explained.
"The cabinethas the final say and it includes political considerations in their decision making. The memo that you shared shows how IRCC was transparent about the high risk of significantly increasing PR levels amid the pandemic. And the cabinet decided to proceed with a significant increase anyway."
The report did acknowledge this most ambitious option could pose challenges to the system in terms of processing capacity and infrastructure as the department and its partners, such as the Canada Border Services Agency, would need $4 billion to $6 billion to manage the significant increases in admissions. It also mentioned the pressure it could put on housing, education and health service infrastructure for provinces and municipalities.
"We are at a unique juncture in Canadian history. We are facing the challenge of our generation, and we will meet our moment," Mendicino told reporters on Oct. 30, 2020, after he tabled the federal government's 2021-23 immigration plan in Parliament.
"Before the pandemic, our government's goal to drive the economy forward through immigration was ambitious. Now, it is simply vital."
While the department has worked hard to improve the immigration system, Langford, the Calgary-based lawyer, said there seems to be a disconnect between the government's commitment and capacity for such growth.
Canadians' support for high immigration would take a hit, however, amid the skyrocketing inflation, global interest rate hikes and housing crisis over the past year.
The public pushback prompted current Immigration Minister Marc Miller to hold back any further immigration increases by keeping the levels at 485,000 new permanent residents in 2024, 500,000 in 2025 and plateau at 500,000 in 2026.
Gabriela Ramo, chair of the Canadian Bar Association's immigration section, says tying immigration levels to housing and health care oversimplifies these complex socioeconomic issues, and in tough times, people tend to look for easy solutions but there's so much at play that immigration is just a small piece.
"I'm not looking at it and say, if they had done 50,000 less, then the housing crisis would be better. I really don't think that's the case," said the Toronto lawyer.
"For me, the key issues are you pick levels, how are you going to deliver these? How are you going to ensure accountability? What are you doing to ensure fairness? Those are the issues we need to be talking about and making sure that our immigration system remains robust, approachable and accountable and responsible."
The Immigration Department declined to comment on the proposed levels plan it submitted to the cabinet but said annual immigration targets take into account demographic and regional needs, the labour market, humanitarian commitments, family reunification and processing capacity.
CREDIT: Nicholas Keung Toronto Star

Document 474 of 500

Teachers, province found right formula: Despite strains between government and unions, four agreements were reached without job action

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/teachers-province-found-right-formula/docview/2954715658/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Teachers%2C+province+found+right+formula%3A+Despite+strains+between+government+and+unions%2C+four+agreements+were+reached+without+job+action&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Four teacher unions, four collective agreements reached.
No job action.
But with no love lost between the unions and the Ford government, the question is how they were able to reach deals and avoid any in-school upset as has happened in almost every bargaining round over the past 16 years.
The answer? Timing. Turning to arbitration. And very little public sniping.
"The pathway that we ended up choosing, as OSSTF, was really quite unique and it had to do with the fact that we had the pandemic, a once-in-a-generation event, and really wanting to be able to get the best possible deal for our members," said Karen Littlewood, president of the Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation.
"But we had a reluctant partner at the table in the government ... It was very challenging. So over the course of the year, we had proposed the pathway that we ended up going with, and that our members approved, which was to go to voluntary binding interest arbitration for any items that were not able to be achieved by the date that we set."
It was a "once-in-a-lifetime thing" she added, but with the government feeling the heat on the Greenbelt scandal and then a chaotic bargaining round with school staffers represented by the Canadian Union of Public Employees, she felt "they needed good news" and her union was the first to reach an agreement for teachers.
The government also lost a court battle over its public sector wage-capping legislation, Bill 124, that was deemed unconstitutional and has since cost it $4 billion, with about $2.6 billion in catch-up pay for teachers and education workers to date.
Though other teacher unions initially fussed, they too ended up agreeing to binding arbitration for salary, including the province's Catholic teachers who were the last to reach an agreement - signing off on one a week ago that goes to members for a ratification vote later this month. (Each teacher union sent other, various items to arbitration as well.)
Meanwhile, Education Minister Stephen Lecce kept his message consistent: The government was keen on stability for students after the chaos of COVID and school shutdowns, and with little patience expected from parents should strikes be called.
After the success of the OSSTF deal, he repeatedly urged the other teacher unions to take the same route.
After announcing the final, tentative teacher deal last Tuesday - with the Ontario English Catholic Teachers' Association - Lecce was asked about the success of arbitration.
"I'm not going to prejudice future (rounds of bargaining) other than just to simply say, we all had to come around the table with a focus on keeping kids in school after years of difficulty. This matters today and it would matter in the future, so it was an effective way to reach a deal to provide stability for families and keep kids in school. That's where we're focused on."
Lecce said "the last time this type of stability has been achieved - we have to go back to 2008. It's been quite a long time that families have had years of peace and we are restoring that sense of focus on academics and sense of hope and stability in the classroom. Everyone will benefit from this."
(In 2017, then-Liberal education minister Mitzie Hunter negotiated two-year contract extensions for teachers, rather than new contracts, to avoid labour strife before the 2018 election.)
With the latest round of contracts expiring after the next provincial election, the deals also provide the PCs with education peace for the remainder of their mandate, as most agreements include sending any local bargaining issues to arbitration as well and barring strikes or lockouts.
Deals have also been reached with almost all support staff in the province, save for one small union that is still at the negotiating table with the government.
Labour expert Stephanie Ross, a professor at McMaster University's School of Labour Studies, said "there were forms of pressure that could have led to job action that were used in this round" of bargaining, noting that some teacher unions did take strike votes.
However, she added, "it is true that there was also enormous pressure to avoid more instability in education and the pandemic and its ongoing impact on people's experience of education made full-blown, collective action very difficult to contemplate, on both sides."
She also said the "diminishing confidence that education unions have, that they can make headway in collective bargaining through the traditional means, without government repression" played a role, noting the previous Liberal government forced contracts on teachers and the PC government's attempt to use legislation and the notwithstanding clause to pre-empt a strike by CUPE education workers and impose a contract on them.
Carlo Fanelli, a professor in the work and labour studies program at York University, warned that "we should be very cautious about putting all our hopes and aspirations into the progressive potential of arbitration, because it politicizes the situation, the bargaining over wages ... But of course, this is not to deny that sometimes when bargaining reaches an impasse, there needs to be a mechanism for resolving this."
For one long-time education official, both sides seemed to be on their best behaviour despite the year-plus it took to reach deals, and what helped keep expectations on all sides reasonable was avoiding open or transparent bargaining.
The official, who did not wish to be named because of the sensitivity around bargaining issues, also said both Lecce and the union leadership "to their credit, did a much better job of keeping discussions, for the most part, behind closed doors and not sniping at each other in public."
Littlewood, who was personally targeted by some in the labour movement for agreeing to arbitration to reach a deal - even though the arbitrator William Kaplan is well-known and well-regarded on all sides - said "I think the way you're going to be able to work together with the Conservative government, who typically wouldn't favour education, is at least to remain civil. I've tried really hard to do that."
However, she added, "I have to make sure I keep some things in my head and I don't say them out loud. But it was really important. It was absolutely deliberate to not have those kinds of verbal attacks."
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Title: Teachers, province found right formula:   Despite strains between government and unions, four agreements were reached without job action

Document 475 of 500

Teacher unions don't like the Ford government. So how did the province land deals with all four of them?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/teacher-unions-dont-like-ford-government-so-how/docview/2954715588/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Teacher+unions+don%27t+like+the+Ford+government.+So+how+did+the+province+land+deals+with+all+four+of+them%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-03-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Four teacher unions, four collective agreements reached.
No job action.
But with no love lost between the unions and the Ford government, the question is how they were able to reach deals and avoid any in-school upset as has happened in almost every bargaining round over the past 16 years.
The answer? Timing. Turning to arbitration. And very little public sniping.
"The pathway that we ended up choosing, as OSSTF, was really quite unique and it had to do with the fact that we had the pandemic, a once-in-a-generation event, and really wanting to be able to get the best possible deal for our members," said Karen Littlewood, president of the Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation.
"But we had a reluctant partner at the table in the government ... It was very challenging. So over the course of the year, we had proposed the pathway that we ended up going with, and that our members approved, which was to go to voluntary binding interest arbitration for any items that were not able to be achieved by the date that we set."
It was a "once-in-a-lifetime thing" she added, but with the government feeling the heat on the Greenbelt scandal and then a chaotic bargaining round with school staffers represented by the Canadian Union of Public Employees, she felt "they needed good news" and her union was the first to reach an agreement for teachers.
The government also lost a court battle over its public sector wage-capping legislation, Bill 124, that was deemed unconstitutional and has since cost it $4 billion, with about $2.6 billion in catch-up pay for teachers and education workers to date.
Though other teacher unions initially fussed, they too ended up agreeing to binding arbitration for salary, including the province's Catholic teachers who were the last to reach an agreement - signing off on one a week ago that goes to members for a ratification vote later this month. (Each teacher union sent other, various items to arbitration as well.)
Meanwhile, Education Minister Stephen Lecce kept his message consistent: The government was keen on stability for students after the chaos of COVID and school shutdowns, and with little patience expected from parents should strikes be called.
After the success of the OSSTF deal, he repeatedly urged the other teacher unions to take the same route.
After announcing the final, tentative teacher deal last Tuesday - with the Ontario English Catholic Teachers' Association - Lecce was asked about the success of arbitration.
"I'm not going to prejudice future (rounds of bargaining) other than just to simply say, we all had to come around the table with a focus on keeping his kids in school after years of difficulty. This matters today and it would matter in the future, so it was an effective way to reach a deal to provide stability for families and keep kids in school. That's where we're focused on."
Lecce said "the last time this type of stability has been achieved - we have to go back to 2008. It's been quite a long time that families have had years of peace and we are restoring that sense of focus on academics and sense of hope and stability in the classroom. Everyone will benefit from this."
(In 2017, then Liberal education minister Mitzie Hunter negotiated two-year contract extensions for teachers, rather than new contracts, to avoid labour strife before the 2018 election.)
With the latest round of contracts expiring after the next provincial election, the deals also provide the PCs with education peace for the remainder of their mandate, as most agreements include sending any local bargaining issues to arbitration as well and barring strikes or lockouts.
Deals have also been reached with almost all support staff in the province, save for one small union that is still at the negotiating table with the government.
Labour expert Stephanie Ross, a professor at McMaster University's School of Labour Studies, said "there were forms of pressure that could have led to job action that were used in this round" of bargaining, noting that some teacher unions did take strike votes.
However, she added, "it is true that there was also enormous pressure to avoid more instability in education and the pandemic and its ongoing impact on people's experience of education made full-blown, collective action very difficult to contemplate, on both sides."
She also said the "diminishing confidence that education unions have, that they can make headway in collective bargaining through the traditional means, without government repression" played a role, noting the previous Liberal government forced contracts on teachers and the PC government's attempt to use legislation and the notwithstanding clause to pre-empt a strike by CUPE education workers and impose a contract on them.
Carlo Fanelli, a professor in the work and labour studies program at York University, warned that "we should be very cautious about putting all our hopes and aspirations into the progressive potential of arbitration, because it politicizes the situation, the bargaining over wages ... But of course, this is not to deny that sometimes when bargaining reaches an impasse, there needs to be a mechanism for resolving this."
For one long-time education official, both sides seemed to be on their best behaviour despite the year-plus it took to reach deals, and what helped keep expectations on all sides reasonable was avoiding open or transparent bargaining - the route taken by CUPE in 2022 and by OSSTF before the pandemic hit, informing members of everything going on at the table.
The official, who did not wish to be named because of the sensitivity around bargaining issues, also said both Lecce and the union leadership "to their credit, did a much better job of keeping discussions, for the most part, behind closed doors and not sniping at each other in public.
"I think it's an indication that the traditional way of doing things where you keep your conversations, for the most part, at the table does lead to a deal without job action - or it's more likely to, anyways."
New teacher union leadership also helped, the official added.
For Littlewood, who was personally targeted by some in the labour movement for agreeing to arbitration to reach a deal - even though the arbitrator William Kaplan is well-known and well-regarded on all sides - said "I think the way you're going to be able to work together with the Conservative government, who typically wouldn't favour education, is at least to remain civil. I've tried really hard to do that."
However, she added, "I have to make sure I keep some things in my head and I don't say them out loud. But it was really important. It was absolutely deliberate to not have those kinds of verbal attacks."
CAPTION: OSSTF president Karen Littlewood speaks to members at their annual general meeting in Toronto on Aug. 18, 2022.Education Minister Stephen Lecce announced a tentative agreement with the province's Catholic teachers Tuesday morning at Queen's Park, alongside parliamentary assistant and MPP Patrice Barnes (Ajax), who is also a former Durham public board trustee.

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Toronto+signed+a+secret+deal+that+would+have+let+it+back+out+of+hosting+the+2026+World+Cup%2C+March+8+The+uncertainty+of+the+pharmacare+pledge%3A+Editorial%2C+March+5+Ex-chief+justices+ring+alarm+over+Doug+Ford+wanting+%27like-minded%27+judges%2C+March+6+Canada+needs+to+show+leadership+of+ending+famine+in+Gaza%3A+Phillips%2C+March+8+I%27m+back%2C+long+COVID+never+went+away%2C+and+the+world+is+still+a+mess%3A+Mallick%2C+March+6&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-03-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tory should be accountable for World Cup costs
Toronto signed a secret deal that would have let it back out of hosting the 2026 World Cup, March 8
So former mayor John Tory, some senior councillors and select staffers knew that there was a deadline to get out of the soccer deal if funding from other levels of government wasn't locked down and, despite being aware of the city's precarious financial position, chose to ignore the clause, say nothing and stay committed to hosting the increasingly expensive games. People who rely on their elected representatives to do their jobs are left holding the bag.
Peter Bradley, Mississauga
Pharmacare has many pitfalls
The uncertainty of the pharmacare pledge: Editorial, March 5
Methinks the pharmacare headline above, chosen by the Star Editorial Board, is an apt description of the potential pitfalls of the pharmacare legislation. This accommodation was unwisely negotiated by the New Democrats with the federal Liberals while they are staring at defeat in the 2025 election. We now know governments covered 46 per cent of $36.6 billion annual prescription drug spending in 2021-22. However, the larger 54 per cent spending was covered by private insurers and was paid-out-of-pocket. The Ontario government could be saddled with the prohibitive cost of a complete pharmacare plan burden. As stated in the editorial, "the NDP will be judged to be merely the enabler of deeply unpopular government."
Rudy Fernandes, Mississauga
Ontarians must stand up for judicial process
Ex-chief justices ring alarm over Doug Ford wanting 'like-minded' judges, March 6
It's no surprise that judges, retired judges and lawyers alike are opposing the changes that the current premier is attempting to make to the judicial process, but they represent a small percentage of Ontarians.
The largest percentage of Ontario's population, you and me, should be very concerned with what is being proposed, because one day many of us could find ourselves in front of one of these "tough judges" who are "like-minded" with you know who. Do we really want to have a judge with no compassion and a rigid sense of right and wrong? We, the people, should be stepping up and speaking out against this infringement on our democratic process.
Contact your local MPP or the office of the premier himself to express concern on an obvious infringement on a time honoured process. Time to take a stand!
Patricia Steward, East York
Despite lack of seatbelts, school buses are still safe
The recent accident involving a school bus near Woodstock, Ont., that left five children injured will renew calls, in some quarters, for the instalment of seatbelts on school busses. According to Canada Safety Council and many experts, seatbelts would not make school buses safer as Transport Canada applies "40 safety standards to the design and construction of school buses made in and imported into Canada." The council follows that "schools buses are not passenger vehicles ... They are bigger, heavier, and sit higher" so they have a body-on frame design. The bus would have to be completely re-engineered with seatbelts integrated at the design stage. Research has shown lap belts could increase the risk of head injuries in a head-on collision. The child's head could hit the seat in front, resulting in serious head and neck injuries. Combination lap and shoulder belts would require stiffer seats, which could increase injury to unbelted students. Moreover, the Canada Safety Council notes shoulder belts can lead to abdominal injuries because of "submarining," when children slip down risking injuries to organs covered by the lap belts. Beyond the engineering problems, staff would be needed to ensure the seatbelts are used, adjusted properly between uses to fit children of different sizes and repaired when damaged. In an emergency, seatbelts could also hinder evacuation. Young children should not be placed in a situation where they are responsible for their safety, the council says.
Another issue worthy of consideration is not seatbelts, but reductions in school bus services. Without a bus, children are exposed to risk by walking to and from school or using other forms of transportation. Statistically the school bus is the safest way for children to get to and from school. According to Transport Canada, children are 16 times safer riding in a school bus on their way to and from school rather than in a passenger vehicle.
Emile Therien, Ottawa
Remember
our humanity
Canada needs to show leadership of ending famine in Gaza: Phillips, March 8
Columnist Andrew Phillips just challenged readers of the Star to take a test they "don't want to pass." Actually, his test of one's humanity, consists of imagining a dying child in a Gaza hospital. To pass the test is to admit that as a human being, it is unbearable to watch another fellow human, an innocent child, starving to death. Thus the onlooker should do something to stop the deaths of more. Thanks to Phillips' moral courage for finally framing this unending war as a test of our humanity. For too long, questionable political considerations and explanations have been used to cover up our inhumanity in not achieving a permanent ceasefire to the killing.
Tony D'Andrea, Toronto
The last time there was an emergency airdrop for aid, allies did it into Berlin. Why would anyone send military aid to Israel so that they can block humanitarian aid to Gaza? This is Second World War level of atrocity.
David Slater, Toronto
Welcome back, Heather
I'm back, long COVID never went away, and the world is still a mess: Mallick, March 6
The opinion pages sparkled this week with the return of the inimitable columnist Heather Mallick. She brings not only rare insight and wit to her column, but in detailing her struggles with long COVID she is shining a light on serious threat to public health.
More than two million Canadians share Mallick's plight, yet this scourge is woefully under-reported and, apparently, no medical cure is imminent. Where is the investigative reporting? What are governments doing, if anything, to meet this health crisis? We don't know because there aren't enough Heathers out there sounding the alarm.
Jim Lang, Thornbury, Ont.
